This shearing of myofibrils sets off a chain of events beginning with a deformation of T-tubules and a corresponding disruption of calcium homeostasis that mediates the secretion of the calcium-activated neutral proteases (such as calpain) involved in further degradation of structural muscle proteins.
summary: Eccentric contractions cause nonuniform myofibril lengthening, leading to shearing and triggering a cascade of events involving T-tubule deformation, calcium dysregulation, and activation of proteases that degrade muscle proteins.

content: There is evidence of a dose–response relationship, whereby higher exercise volumes correlate with a greater degree of myodamage. Symptoms of EIMD include decreased force-producing capacity, increased musculoskeletal stiffness and swelling, delayed-onset muscle soreness (DOMS), and a heightened physiological stress response typified by an elevated heart rate response to submaximal exercise and heightened lactate production.
summary: Exercise volume directly correlates with the extent of muscle damage. Symptoms of EIMD include reduced strength, stiffness, soreness, and increased physiological stress.

content: EIMD decreases when a person performs the same exercise consistently, a phenomenon commonly known as the repeated bout effect. Several factors are thought to be responsible for the repeated bout effect, including an adaptive strengthening of connective tissue, increased efficiency in the recruitment of motor units, enhanced synchronization of motor units, a more even distribution of the workload among fibers, and a greater contribution of muscle synergists.
summary: Repeated exposure to the same exercise reduces EIMD through adaptations like connective tissue strengthening, improved motor unit recruitment, and workload distribution.

content: The effects of the repeated bout effect can last for several months, even in the absence of eccentric training during this period. Evidence that the upper-extremity muscles have a greater predisposition to EIMD than the leg muscles suggests a protective benefit in muscles that are frequently used during everyday activities.
summary: The protective benefits of the repeated bout effect can persist for months, even without continued training. Upper-body muscles, more frequently used, show greater resistance to EIMD.

content: Muscles become increasingly less susceptible to damage with recurring exercise—a function of the repeated bout effect. There is evidence that myodamage is indeed present in trained lifters, albeit to a lesser extent than in novices.
summary: Regular exercise reduces muscle susceptibility to damage. Trained individuals still experience some EIMD, but to a lesser degree than untrained individuals.

content: Gibala and colleagues (246) recruited 6 resistance-trained men to perform 8 sets of 8 repetitions at a load equivalent to 80% of 1RM. The researchers employed a unilateral protocol whereby one arm performed only concentric actions while the other arm performed only eccentric actions. Muscle biopsies taken 21 hours after the exercise bout showed a significantly greater disruption in fibers from the eccentrically trained arms versus the concentrically trained arms.
summary: A study comparing concentric and eccentric training in trained individuals showed significantly greater muscle damage in the eccentrically trained arm, highlighting the greater impact of eccentric actions.

content: The repeated bout effect only attenuates the magnitude of muscle damage as opposed to preventing its occurrence. Some researchers have questioned whether EIMD confers any anabolic effects, based on research showing marked hypertrophy from low-intensity BFR training with ostensibly minimal tissue damage (6, 737).
summary: The repeated bout effect reduces, but does not eliminate, muscle damage. Some researchers question the necessity of EIMD for muscle growth, citing hypertrophy observed with minimal damage in BFR training.

content: The BFR technique combines light loads (20% to 50% of 1RM) with occlusion via pressure cuff to impede venous return without obstructing arterial inflow. Regular performance of BFR induces marked hypertrophy, often similar to what is observed with the use of heavy loads.
summary: Blood flow restriction (BFR) training involves light weights and occlusion to restrict venous outflow, inducing hypertrophy comparable to heavy lifting.

content: It is hypothesized that BFR confers these hypertrophic benefits while minimizing disruption of myofibers.
summary: BFR training is thought to promote hypertrophy while minimizing muscle fiber damage.

content: The repeated bout effect only attenuates the magnitude of muscle damage as opposed to preventing its occurrence. Muscle damage is a known consequence of reperfusion subsequent to ischemia.  It is hypothesized that BFR confers these hypertrophic benefits while minimizing disruption of myofibers.  Markers of muscle damage following BFR have been demonstrated elsewhere, including lengthy decrements in maximal voluntary contraction, heightened delayed-on-set muscle soreness, and elevated sarcolemmal permeability. Takarada and colleagues (737) demonstrated that although markers of muscle damage were attenuated after BFR training, there was evidence of fine microdamage within myofibers, leaving open the possibility that damage may have contributed to the results.
summary: BFR training minimizes muscle damage compared to traditional resistance training, but some microdamage still occurs, potentially contributing to hypertrophy.

content: The BFR technique combines light loads (20% to 50% of 1RM) with occlusion via pressure cuff to impede venous return without obstructing arterial inflow. Regular performance of BFR induces marked hypertrophy, often similar to what is observed with the use of heavy loads. Some researchers have questioned whether EIMD confers any anabolic effects, based on research showing marked hypertrophy from low-intensity BFR training with ostensibly minimal tissue damage (6, 737).  It remains possible that hypertrophy would have been enhanced to an even greater extent had EIMD been heightened in the BFR group.
summary: BFR training uses light loads and occlusion to induce hypertrophy, potentially through mechanisms independent of significant muscle damage.

content: The two types of training activate and suppress distinctly different subsets of genes and cellular signaling pathways (295), thereby bringing about divergent muscular adaptations.  Downhill running is associated with peak creatine kinase levels of between 100 to 600 IU, whereas those of resistance range from 2,000 to 10,000 IU (145). Peak creatine kinase activity is noted approximately 12 to 24 hours after downhill running, whereas that associated with resistance training is not evident until about 48 hours after the training bout and can peak 4 to 6 days postworkout (672). Creatine kinase levels do not necessarily reflect the degree or time course of myodamage (146), calling into question their practical relevance with respect to exercise training. What can be inferred from aerobic training data is that muscle damage by itself is not sufficient to induce significant muscle growth.
summary: Downhill running and resistance training induce distinct muscle adaptations, with different creatine kinase profiles and potential mechanisms for hypertrophy.

content: If EIMD does play a role in compensatory hypertrophy, it can do so only in the presence of resistance-based mechanical overload.  A study involved two groups: a control group and a pretrained group. The control group carried out eccentric cycle ergometry at a 'somewhat hard' level for 20 minutes, 44 times per week over an 8-week period. The pretrained group carried out the identical protocol to the control group, except that it included a 3-week ramp-up period during which subjects performed exercise at a low intensity to gradually acclimate their muscles to the training stimulus. Similar increases in muscle girth were found between the groups at the study’s end. The study had methodological limitations including the use of untrained subjects, unequal training duration between the groups, and a small sample size. The pretrained group did show evidence of myodamage as assessed by elevated creatine kinase levels, although the extent was significantly less than that noted in the control group.
summary: A study comparing pretrained and untrained individuals suggests that prior adaptation may reduce muscle damage and lead to similar hypertrophy gains.

content: The regeneration and repair of muscle tissue following EIMD is carried out by novel transcriptional programs that are associated with or promoted by inflammatory processes, satellite cell activity, IGF-1 production, and cell swelling. Research suggests that EIMD can enhance muscular adaptations, although excessive damage has a negative effect on muscle development.  It remains to be determined the extent to which these mechanisms are synergistic and whether an optimal combination exists to maximize the hypertrophic response to resistance training. Although EIMD can be deleterious from a performance standpoint, some researchers have speculated that the associated increases in inflammation and protein turnover are necessary for muscle growth. The rationale is based on the hypothesis that structural alterations associated with damage influence gene expression in a manner that strengthens the affected tissue, thereby serving to protect the muscle against further injury. Substantial evidence links muscle damage with factors involved in the hypertrophic response to exercise.
summary: Muscle damage triggers complex repair mechanisms that may contribute to hypertrophy, but excessive damage can be detrimental. The optimal balance between damage and repair for maximizing muscle growth remains unclear.

content: Komulainen and colleagues exposed the tibialis anterior muscles of anesthetized Wistar rats to repeated concentric or eccentric muscle actions. Eccentric muscle actions produced massive injury to the muscle; beta-glucuronidase activity (a measure of myodamage) showed a 7.1-fold increase from baseline. Concentric muscle actions resulted in a modest 2.6-fold increase in beta-glucuronidase activity, indicating that the damage was relatively minor. Similar increases in muscle cross-sectional area were noted in both groups, suggesting a threshold for EIMD-induced growth beyond which myodamage provides no additional beneficial hypertrophic effects. The study is confounded by evaluating polar-extreme levels of damage. Whether a dose–response relationship exists between hypertrophy and moderate levels of EIMD, therefore, cannot be determined. Moreover, the severe damage experienced in the eccentric muscle actions may have been so excessive that it negatively affected remodeling.
summary: A rat study suggests a threshold for muscle damage beyond which further damage does not enhance hypertrophy, highlighting the need to investigate the dose-response relationship between EIMD and muscle growth.

content: In a human trial, Flann and colleagues randomly assigned 14 young, healthy men and women into one of two groups: a control group and a pretrained group. The body’s response to EIMD can be equated to its response to infection (656). After a damaging exercise bout, neutrophils migrate to the injury site while agents are released by affected fibers that attract macrophages to the region as well (475). Neutrophils are more abundant in the human body than any other type of white blood cell. In addition to possessing phagocytic capabilities, neutrophils release proteases that aid in breaking down cellular debris from EIMD.
summary: Human and animal studies demonstrate that the body's response to muscle damage involves immune cells and proteases that contribute to tissue repair.

content: In a human trial, Flann and colleagues randomly assigned 14 young, healthy men and women into one of two groups: a control group and a pretrained group.
summary: A study by Flann et al. randomly assigned participants to control and pretrained groups.

content: The body’s response to EIMD can be equated to its response to infection (656).
summary: EIMD elicits a physiological response comparable to an infection.

content: After a damaging exercise bout, neutrophils migrate to the injury site while agents are released by affected fibers that attract macrophages to the region as well (475).
summary: Following damaging exercise, neutrophils and macrophages are recruited to the injury site.

content: Neutrophils are more abundant in the human body than any other type of white blood cell.
summary: Neutrophils are the most prevalent white blood cell type.

content: In addition to possessing phagocytic capabilities, neutrophils release proteases that aid in breaking down cellular debris from EIMD. They also secrete cytolytic and cytotoxic substances that can exacerbate damage to injured muscle and inflict damage to healthy neighboring tissues (764).
summary: Neutrophils contribute to EIMD by phagocytosing debris and releasing proteases, but they can also cause further damage.

content: Hence, their primary role in skeletal muscle is likely confined to myolysis and other facets associated with the removal of cellular debris as opposed to the regeneration of contractile tissue. Despite a lack of evidence directly linking neutrophils to hypertrophy, it is conceivable that they may mediate anabolism by signaling other inflammatory cells necessary for muscle remodeling.
summary: Neutrophils primarily function in debris removal during EIMD, but may indirectly contribute to muscle growth by signaling other cells.

content: One such possibility is reactive oxygen species (ROS) (779), which have been shown to mediate intracellular signaling in response to intense physical activity (258, 340, 341, 574, 760).
summary: ROS are implicated in intracellular signaling during intense physical activity.

content: Neutrophils are associated with the production of numerous ROS variants, including hydrogen peroxide, superoxide, hydroxyl radical, and hypochlorous acid (372).
summary: Neutrophils produce various ROS, including hydrogen peroxide, superoxide, hydroxyl radical, and hypochlorous acid.

content: ROS are associated with hypertrophy of both smooth muscle and cardiac muscle (734), and some speculate that anabolic effects extend to skeletal muscle as well (737).
summary: ROS are linked to hypertrophy in smooth and cardiac muscle, with potential implications for skeletal muscle.

content: In support of this hypothesis, transgenic mice displaying suppressed levels of selenoproteins (a class of proteins that act as powerful antioxidants) had 50% more muscle mass following synergist ablation compared to wild-type controls (313).
summary: Mice with reduced antioxidant capacity exhibited increased muscle mass after synergist ablation, suggesting a role for ROS in muscle growth.

content: These findings suggest that redox-sensitive signaling pathways may enhance exercise-induced muscular adaptations.
summary: Redox signaling pathways may contribute to muscle adaptations induced by exercise.

content: ROS have been shown to mediate anabolism via activation of the MAPK pathway. The treatment of C2 myoblasts with an ROS variant heightens MAPK signaling, and the temporal response varies between MAPK subfamilies (ERK1/2, JNK, and p38 MAPK) (357).
summary: ROS activate the MAPK pathway, influencing muscle cell signaling.

content: Given that eccentric exercise is associated with greater MAPK activation compared to concentric or isometric actions (446, 454), it is conceivable that ROS production contributes to this stimulus.
summary: Eccentric exercise, which induces greater MAPK activation, may be linked to ROS production.

content: There also is evidence that ROS enhance growth processes by amplifying IGF-1 signaling. In vitro ROS treatment of mouse C2C12 myocytes significantly increased phosphorylation of the IGF-1 receptor, whereas phosphorylation was markedly suppressed with antioxidant provision (283).
summary: ROS promote muscle growth by enhancing IGF-1 signaling.

content: These findings suggest a crucial role for ROS in the biological actions of IGF-1.
summary: ROS play a vital role in the biological effects of IGF-1.

content: Interestingly, there is evidence that ROS interfere with the signaling of various serine/threonine phosphatases, such as calcineurin. ROS activity impairs calcineurin activation by blocking its calmodulin-binding domain (128).
summary: ROS can disrupt the signaling of certain phosphatases, including calcineurin.

content: Calcineurin is thought to be involved in both skeletal muscle growth (193, 490) and fiber phenotype transformation (560), and thus its inhibition may be detrimental to anabolism. Moreover, some studies have failed to demonstrate that ROS are in fact activated in response to EIMD (644).
summary: Calcineurin, involved in muscle growth and fiber type transformation, is inhibited by ROS, potentially hindering anabolism. However, some studies contradict the notion of ROS activation during EIMD.

content: When considering the body of literature as a whole, any anabolic effects of ROS are likely dependent on exercise mode (i.e., anaerobic versus aerobic), the species of ROS produced, and perhaps other factors.
summary: The anabolic effects of ROS likely depend on exercise type, ROS species, and other factors.

content: In contrast to neutrophils, research indicates a potential role for macrophages in regenerative processes following EIMD (764), and some researchers even speculate that they are necessary for muscle growth (372).
summary: Macrophages may play a role in muscle regeneration and growth after EIMD.

content: Macrophages appear to exert anabolic effects by secreting local growth factors associated
summary: Macrophages promote muscle growth by secreting growth factors.

content: It was originally thought that myodamage directly led to the production of pro-inflammatory myokines. A study by Toft and colleagues (770) showed that IL-6 levels were only modestly elevated relative to increases in creatine kinase following 60 minutes of eccentric cycle ergometry exercise, suggesting a weak association between EIMD and IL-6 production.
summary: Contrary to previous beliefs, EIMD may not directly cause the release of pro-inflammatory myokines like IL-6.

content: These results are consistent with those of others showing poor correlation in the time course of IL-6 and creatine kinase appearance. The totality of findings has led to the supposition that IL-6 release is predominantly a function of muscle contraction.
summary: IL-6 release is primarily driven by muscle contraction, not necessarily by EIMD.

content: Mechanistically, some researchers have hypothesized that this facilitates the mobilization of substrate from fuel depots so that glucose homeostasis is maintained during intense exercise.
summary: IL-6 release may help maintain glucose homeostasis during intense exercise by mobilizing fuel substrates.

content: It is important to note that only IL-6 and IL-8 have been shown to be released from skeletal muscle in the absence of damaging exercise.
summary: Only IL-6 and IL-8 are released from skeletal muscle without damaging exercise.

content: Many other myokines may play a role in the hypertrophic response to EIMD.
summary: Numerous other myokines may contribute to muscle growth after EIMD.

content: Systemic IL-15 levels and IL-15 mRNA in skeletal muscle are markedly elevated after eccentric (but not concentric) exercise, giving credence to the notion that elevations are contingent on damage to fibers.
summary: IL-15 levels increase after eccentric, but not concentric, exercise, suggesting a link to muscle fiber damage.

content: Some studies show that IL-15 directly regulates hypertrophy by increasing muscle protein synthesis and reducing proteolysis in differentiated myotubes.
summary: IL-15 directly promotes muscle hypertrophy by increasing protein synthesis and reducing protein breakdown.

content: There also is evidence that fibroblast growth factors (FGFs)—powerful proliferative agents involved in hypertrophic processes—are preferentially upregulated following eccentric exercise.
summary: FGFs, which promote cell proliferation and hypertrophy, are upregulated after eccentric exercise.

content: Research indicates that FGFs are secreted from damaged fibers and that their time course of release parallels the increased creatine kinase levels associated with EIMD. These findings lend mechanistic support to the hypothesis that damaging exercise promotes an anabolic stimulus.
summary: FGF release after EIMD supports the idea that damaging exercise stimulates muscle growth.

content: A large body of evidence links EIMD with satellite cell activity.
summary: EIMD is associated with increased satellite cell activity.

content: Damaged myofibers must rapidly acquire additional myonuclei to aid in tissue repair and regeneration or otherwise face cell death.
summary: Damaged muscle fibers require additional myonuclei for repair and regeneration.

content: There also is evidence that fibroblast growth factors (FGFs)—powerful proliferative agents involved in hypertrophic processes—are preferentially upregulated following eccentric exercise. Research indicates that FGFs are secreted from damaged fibers and that their time course of release parallels the increased creatine kinase levels associated with EIMD. These findings lend mechanistic support to the hypothesis that damaging exercise promotes an anabolic stimulus.
summary: Eccentric exercise upregulates FGFs, which are released from damaged muscle fibers, supporting the idea that damaging exercise promotes muscle growth.

content: A large body of evidence links EIMD with satellite cell activity. Damaged myofibers must rapidly acquire additional myonuclei to aid in tissue repair and regeneration or otherwise face cell death. Satellite cells facilitate these means by proliferating and fusing to damaged fibers. Because satellite cells tend to populate under the myoneural junction, it is speculated that they may be further stimulated by activation from motor neurons innervating damaged fibers, enhancing the regenerative response.
summary: EIMD activates satellite cells, which are crucial for muscle repair and regeneration by fusing with damaged fibers.

content: It has been hypothesized that under certain conditions stimulated satellite cells fuse to each other to form new myofibers, but evidence as to how this relates to traditional resistance training practices is currently lacking. Initial signaling to activate satellite cells following EIMD is purported to originate from muscle-derived nitric oxide, potentially in combination with the release of HGF. The process appears to be controlled at least to some extent by the cyclooxygenase (COX)-2 pathway, which is considered necessary for maximizing exercise-induced hypertrophic adaptations.
summary: Satellite cell activation after EIMD is initiated by nitric oxide and HGF, potentially controlled by the COX-2 pathway, but more research is needed on its role in muscle growth.

content: COX-2 acts to promote the synthesis of prostaglandins believed to stimulate satellite cell proliferation, differentiation, and fusion. Research shows an enhanced myogenic response when inflammatory cells are abundant and a blunted response in their absence, suggesting that inflammatory processes subsequent to damaging exercise are critical to remodeling.
summary: COX-2 promotes muscle growth by stimulating satellite cell activity, and inflammation plays a crucial role in this process.

content: The hypertrophic importance of COX-2 is further supported by research investigating the effects of COX-inhibiting nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) on the postexercise satellite cell response. A majority of studies show decreased postexercise satellite cell activity when NSAIDs are administered, which would conceivably limit long-term muscle growth, although these findings are not universal.
summary: COX-2 inhibition by NSAIDs reduces satellite cell activity, potentially limiting muscle growth, although findings are not consistent.

content: It is important to point out that mechanical stimuli alone can instigate satellite cell proliferation and  Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are a class of analgesics commonly used to relieve pain and swelling associated with delayed-onset muscle soreness. NSAIDs are thought to promote pain-reducing effects by inhibiting the activity of cyclooxygenase (COX), a family of enzymes that catalyze the conversion of arachidonic acid to pro-inflammatory prostanoids.
summary: Mechanical stimuli and NSAIDs both influence satellite cell activity, with NSAIDs primarily targeting pain relief by inhibiting COX enzymes.

content: Thirty million people are estimated to take NSAIDs on a daily basis. The use of NSAIDs is especially widespread among those who participate in intense exercise programs. Prostanoids are purported to stimulate the upstream regulators of protein synthesis including PI3K and extracellular signal-regulated kinases. There is evidence that prostanoids are intricately involved in enhancing satellite cell proliferation, differentiation, and fusion.
summary: NSAIDs are commonly used, especially by athletes, and prostanoids, which are influenced by NSAIDs, play a role in muscle protein synthesis and satellite cell activity.

content: These data provide compelling evidence that COX enzymes are important and perhaps even necessary for maximizing resistance training–induced muscle hypertrophy. Acute studies on the use of NSAIDs do not seem to show a detrimental impact on postexercise protein synthesis. Although NSAID administration in animal trials following chronic overload has consistently found impairments in protein metabolism, only one human trial showed a blunting of protein synthesis.
summary: COX enzymes are crucial for muscle hypertrophy, but the impact of NSAIDs on protein synthesis is complex and requires further research.

content: Discrepancies between findings may be related to methodological variances, physiological differences between species, or differences in the mechanisms of the NSAIDs used (i.e., selective versus nonselective COX inhibitors). The body of literature strongly suggests that NSAID usage interferes with satellite cell function. This has been shown in vitro as well as in vivo in both animal and human trials.
summary: Discrepancies in NSAID research may be due to methodological differences, species variations, or NSAID type. However, evidence consistently shows that NSAIDs negatively impact satellite cell function.

content: It has been proposed that hypertrophy is limited by a myonuclear domain ceiling, estimated at approximately 2,000 µm2; beyond this ceiling, additional nuclei must be derived from satellite cells for further increases in hypertrophy to occur. Therefore, a blunting of satellite cell function would seemingly limit a person’s hypertrophic potential by restricting the satellite cell pool.
summary: Satellite cell function is crucial for muscle growth, as it determines the number of nuclei available for hypertrophy, which is limited by a myonuclear domain ceiling.

content: Results from studies that have directly investigated the effects of NSAIDs on hypertrophy are conflicting. Animal studies indicate that NSAID administration markedly reduces overload-induced muscle growth. Human trials have either failed to demonstrate hypertrophic impairments or showed a positive effect from NSAID use during regimented resistance training.
summary: Research on the impact of NSAIDs on hypertrophy is inconclusive, with animal studies showing negative effects and human studies showing mixed results.

content: It is possible that NSAIDs reduce protein breakdown to a similar or even greater degree than they suppress protein synthesis, thus resulting in a nonnegative protein balance. The fact that the study showing increased hypertrophy from the use of NSAIDs was carried out in elderly adults (60 to 85 years of age) raises the possibility that positive benefits were due to a suppression of chronic inflammation, which has been shown to impair anabolism and accelerate proteolysis.
summary: NSAIDs may have a neutral or even positive effect on protein balance by reducing protein breakdown, and their benefits in elderly adults may be due to reduced chronic inflammation.

content: It is also conceivable that the extent of hypertrophy in human trials was below the subjects’ myonuclear domain ceiling. This would seemingly explain why animal models using techniques designed to promote extreme rates of hypertrophy (i.e., synergist ablation, chronic stretch) far beyond that of traditional resistance training in humans show substantial hypertrophic impairment, because a robust satellite cell pool would be required for continued muscle growth.
summary: The extent of hypertrophy in human trials may be limited by the myonuclear domain ceiling, explaining why animal models with extreme hypertrophy show greater impairment from NSAIDs.

content: Evidence indicates that the occasional use of NSAIDs will not impair muscle hypertrophy. Whether chronic NSAID administration is detrimental to muscle growth remains undetermined and likely is population specific: those with chronic low-grade inflammation might benefit from their usage, whereas healthy, well-trained people could see long-term impairments.
summary: Occasional NSAID use does not appear to impair muscle growth, but chronic use may have detrimental effects on muscle growth in healthy individuals, while potentially benefiting those with chronic inflammation.

content: EIMD may not be additive or redundant with respect to maximizing muscle protein accretion. EIMD potentiates IGF-1 production.
summary: EIMD may enhance muscle protein accretion independently of other factors, potentially by increasing IGF-1 production.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM. The deadlift is a posterior chain exercise targeting the erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. Conventional deadlifts place greater demand on the erector spinae compared to sumo deadlifts.
summary: Describes the squat and deadlift exercises, their target muscle groups, and variations.

content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: Defines progressive overload as the gradual increase of training stress.

content: Evidence indicates that the occasional use of NSAIDs will not impair muscle hypertrophy. Whether chronic NSAID administration is detrimental to muscle growth remains undetermined and likely is population specific: those with chronic low-grade inflammation might benefit from their usage, whereas healthy, well-trained people could see long-term impairments.
summary: Discusses the potential effects of NSAIDs on muscle hypertrophy, noting that chronic use may be detrimental for some populations.

content: EIMD may not be additive or redundant with respect to maximizing muscle protein accretion. EIMD potentiates IGF-1 production. The early-phase activation of MGF as a result of the damaging protocol suggests that this IGF-1 isoform is preferentially involved in the repair and remodeling process following EIMD.
summary: Explains the role of EIMD in muscle protein accretion and its connection to IGF-1 production and MGF activation.

content: McKay and colleagues (479) studied the effects of performing a series of 300 lengthening knee extension contractions on all three IGF-1 isoforms in untrained young men. The results of McKay and colleagues (479) showed a significant increase in MGF mRNA 24 hours postexercise. Expression of both IGF-1Ea and IGF-1Eb mRNA were not elevated until 72 hours after training in the study by McKay and colleagues (479).
summary: Summarizes a study by McKay and colleagues on the effects of lengthening knee extension contractions on IGF-1 isoforms.

content: Bamman and colleagues (53) evaluated the effects of 8 sets of 8 eccentric versus concentric actions on muscle IGF-1 mRNA concentration. The eccentric exercise resulted in a significant 62% increase in IGF-1 mRNA concentrations as opposed to a non-significant increase from concentric exercise. Eccentric exercise caused a reduction of IGFBP-4 mRNA—a strong inhibitor of IGF-1—by 57% whereas the concentric condition showed only modest changes in the levels of this protein. Results were positively correlated with markers of muscle damage, suggesting that the IGF-1 system is involved in the repair process.
summary: Describes a study by Bamman and colleagues comparing the effects of eccentric and concentric exercise on IGF-1 mRNA and IGFBP-4 mRNA levels.

content: Garma and colleagues (241) compared the acute anabolic response of volume-equated bouts of eccentric, concentric, and isometric exercise in rodents. Results of Garma and colleagues (241) showed similar effects on cell signaling independent of the type of muscle action; no significant differences were seen in IGF-1 mRNA levels, and pre- to postexercise increases were actually greatest in the isometric condition.
summary: Summarizes a study by Garma and colleagues comparing the anabolic response to different types of exercise in rodents.

content: Increases in cellular hydration are associated with an increase in muscle protein synthesis and a concomitant decrease in proteolysis. The inflammatory response that accompanies damaging exercise involves a buildup of fluid and plasma proteins within damaged muscle. Depending on the extent of damage, the amount of accumulated fluid can exceed the capacity of the lymphatic drainage system, which leads to tissue swelling. Trauma to capillaries may increase the magnitude of edema.
summary: Explains the relationship between cellular hydration, inflammation, and edema after damaging exercise.

content: Swelling associated with an acute bout of eccentric elbow flexion exercise in untrained subjects produced an increase in arm circumference of as much as 9%, and values remained elevated for up to 9 d. Nosaka and Clarkson (538) found that edema increased arm circumference by as much as 1.7 inches (4.3 cm) after unaccustomed eccentric exercise, and swelling was evident in all subjects by 3 days after performance. Although swelling is diminished over time with regimented exercise via the repeated bout effect, substantial edema can persist even in well-trained subjects for at least 48 hours postworkout.
summary: Describes the extent and duration of swelling after eccentric exercise in untrained and trained individuals.

content: Whether the swelling associated with EIMD contributes to myofiber hypertrophy is unknown. There is some evidence that the use of NSAIDs, which blunt the inflammatory response and hence moderate the extent of cell swelling, impairs the increase in muscle protein synthesis normally associated with resistance exercise. It is feasible that deleterious effects on anabolism may be related to a decrease in cell swelling. Other studies have failed to show an impaired muscle protein synthetic response following NSAID administration. Factors such as impaired satellite cell and macrophage activity may also be responsible for any negative effects.
summary: Discusses the potential role of edema in muscle hypertrophy and the conflicting evidence regarding NSAID use.

content: A sound theoretical rationale exists for how EIMD may contribute to the accretion of muscle proteins. Although exercise-induced hypertrophy can apparently occur without significant myodamage (832), evidence implies that microtrauma enhances the adaptive response or at least initiates the signaling pathways that mediate anabolism. A cause–effect relationship between EIMD and hypertrophy has yet to be established, and if such a relationship does exist, the degree of damage necessary to maximize muscle growth remains to be determined.
summary: Explores the potential link between EIMD and muscle hypertrophy, acknowledging the need for further research.

content: There is clear evidence that excessive EIMD reduces a muscle’s force-producing capacity. This in turn interferes with the ability to train at a high level, which impedes muscle development. Moreover, although training in the early recovery phase of EIMD does not seem to exacerbate muscle damage, it may interfere with the recovery process (395, 537). Current research indicates that a protocol that elicits a moderate amount of damage would be most appropriate for maximizing the hypertrophic response.
summary: Highlights the importance of finding the optimal level of EIMD for muscle growth and warns against excessive damage.

content: Considering that a ceiling effect slows the rate of hypertrophy as one gains training experience, EIMD may be particularly relevant to the anabolic response in well-trained people.
summary: Suggests that EIMD may be more beneficial for well-trained individuals due to the ceiling effect on hypertrophy.

content: Numerous intracellular signaling pathways have been identified in skeletal muscle including PI3K/Akt, MAPK, phosphatidic acid, AMPK, and calcium-dependent pathways. The serine/threonine kinase mTOR has been shown to be critical to mechanically induced hypertrophic adaptations. Mechanical tension appears to be the most important factor in training-induced muscle hypertrophy. Mechanosensors are sensitive to both the magnitude and duration of loading, and these stimuli can directly mediate intracellular signaling to bring about hypertrophic adaptations.
summary: Describes the key signaling pathways and mechanisms involved in muscle hypertrophy.

content: Mechanical tension appears to be the most important factor in training-induced muscle hypertrophy. Mechanosensors are sensitive to both the magnitude and duration of loading, and these stimuli can directly mediate intracellular signaling to bring about hypertrophic adaptations.
summary: Mechanical tension is the primary driver of muscle hypertrophy, with mechanosensors responding to load magnitude and duration to initiate intracellular signaling.

content: Numerous intracellular signaling pathways have been identified in skeletal muscle including PI3K/Akt, MAPK, phosphatidic acid, AMPK, and calcium-dependent pathways. The serine/threonine kinase mTOR has been shown to be critical to mechanically induced hypertrophic adaptations.
summary: Several intracellular signaling pathways, including mTOR, are activated by mechanical stimuli and contribute to muscle hypertrophy.

content: There is compelling evidence that the metabolic stress associated with resistance training promotes increases in muscle protein accretion. Hypothesized factors involved in the process include increased fiber recruitment, heightened myokine production, cell swelling, and systemic hormonal alterations.
summary: Metabolic stress from resistance training stimulates muscle protein synthesis through various mechanisms, including increased fiber recruitment and hormonal changes.

content: Research suggests that EIMD can enhance muscular adaptations, although excessive damage has a negative effect on muscle development. Hypothesized factors involved in the process include the initiation of inflammatory processes, increased satellite cell activity, the mediation of IGF-1 production, and cell swelling.
summary: Exercise-induced muscle damage (EIMD) can promote muscle growth, but excessive damage is detrimental. This process involves inflammation, satellite cell activation, and IGF-1 production.

content: The extent to which these mechanisms are synergistic, and whether an optimal combination exists to maximize the hypertrophic response to resistance training, remains to be determined.
summary: The interplay between different muscle growth mechanisms requires further research to determine optimal training strategies.

content: A number of research-based methods exist for examining the muscular response to mechanical stimuli. For example, synergist ablation of the gastrocnemius muscle results in the soleus and plantaris muscles being forced to carry out plantar flexion. The heightened load on these muscles results in increases in muscle cross-sectional area of 30% to 50% within several weeks postsurgery.
summary: Various research methods, such as synergist ablation, can be used to study muscle responses to mechanical loading, demonstrating significant hypertrophy in overloaded muscles.

content: Neuromuscular electrical stimulation also is frequently used to promote hypertrophy in animal models. This technique, which involves stimulating muscles with high-frequency electrical impulses (levels above 60 Hz), produces significant gains in muscle mass in just a few sessions. In humans, however, resistance training is the primary means for increasing muscle growth.
summary: While electrical stimulation can induce muscle hypertrophy in animals, resistance training remains the primary method for muscle growth in humans.

content: Resistance training programs are a composite of program design variables that include volume, frequency, load, exercise selection, type of muscle action, rest interval length, repetition duration, exercise order, range of motion, and intensity of effort. These variables can be manipulated to stimulate the neuromuscular system, and they do so in different ways.
summary: Resistance training programs involve numerous variables that can be adjusted to stimulate muscle growth and adaptation.

content: Consistent with the SAID principle (specific adaptations to imposed demands), the way such stimuli are applied influences phenotypic adaptations.
summary: Training adaptations are specific to the type and intensity of stimuli applied, emphasizing the importance of tailored training programs.

content: Volume refers to the amount of exercise performed over a period of time. Volume is often expressed as the number of repetitions completed in a resistance training bout (sets × repetitions). However, this value does not take into account the amount of load lifted. Thus, a more appropriate term to reflect the total work completed is volume load, which is the product of sets × repetitions × load.
summary: Training volume encompasses repetitions and sets, but volume load considers the weight lifted, providing a more accurate measure of total work.

content: Although an increase in training frequency can create the largest increase in weekly volume load provided volume per session is kept static, an increase in the number of sets performed (and thus total repetitions) in a training bout can also substantially increase training volume (298).
summary: Both increasing training frequency and sets per session can effectively increase training volume.

content: Research provides compelling evidence that higher-volume loads are necessary to maximize anabolism. Terzis and colleagues (755) showed that phosphorylation of p70S6K and ribosomal protein S6 increases 30 min following resistance training in a volume-dependent manner. The fact that it did not reach a plateau in the volumes studied suggests that higher volumes might have led to even greater increases.
summary: Higher training volume is associated with greater anabolic signaling and muscle protein synthesis.

content: Intriguingly, the study found similar elevations in mTOR independent of training volume, suggesting that increased training volumes may augment S6 phosphorylation via alternative anabolic pathways, anticatabolic pathways, or a combination of the two.
summary: While mTOR activation is similar across volumes, higher volumes may stimulate additional anabolic pathways.

content: Burd and colleagues (104) found significantly greater increases in muscle protein synthesis 5 hours postworkout in a higher-volume (3 sets) compared with a lower-volume (1 set) resistance training protocol. Muscle protein synthesis in the 3-set condition remained significantly elevated (by 2.3-fold) at 29 hours postworkout, whereas levels in the 1-set condition had returned to baseline.
summary: Multiple-set training protocols significantly elevate muscle protein synthesis compared to single-set protocols, with prolonged effects lasting for hours.

content: Multiple-set protocols in resistance training programs have greater positive effects on intracellular signaling and muscle protein synthesis than single-set protocols. A clear dose–response relationship was noted between volume and hypertrophy—that is, higher volumes correlate to greater hypertrophic adaptations, at least up to a certain point.
summary: Multiple-set training consistently leads to greater muscle growth compared to single-set training, with a clear dose-response relationship between volume and hypertrophy.

content: A meta-analysis encompassing eight studies and 277 subjects found a 40% greater effect size from the performance of multiple- versus single-set protocols. Effect sizes for hypertrophy tended to parallel increases in the number of sets performed up to a maximum of 6 sets (0.24 for 1 set, 0.34 for 2 or 3 sets, and 0.44 for 4 to 6 sets). Every study included in the analysis showed an effect size superiority for multiple- versus single-set training, indicating meaningful differences between protocols.
summary: Multiple-set training consistently outperforms single-set training for hypertrophy, with the greatest benefits observed in protocols with 4-6 sets.

content: The cross-sectional area of the elbow flexors increased from 0.15% per day when 7 through 38 repetitions were performed per session to 0.26% per day when 42 through 66 repetitions were performed per session. The rate of increase diminished to 0.18% per day with volumes in the range of 74 through 120 repetitions per session, suggesting that very high volumes impair the hypertrophic response, perhaps by causing an overtrained state.
summary: Optimal hypertrophy is achieved with moderate repetition ranges, with excessively high volumes potentially hindering growth.

content: With respect to total sets, hypertrophic increases peaked between 4 and 6 sets (0.24% increase in cross-sectional area per day); lesser responses were noted from the performance of 3 to 3.5 sets and ≥9 sets (0.17% and 0.18% increase per day, respectively). With respect to the quadriceps, the findings were similar across a wide spectrum of clusters; 0.12% to 0.13% increases in cross-sectional area per day were seen from the performance of 21 through 100+ repetitions per session. The only exception was in the cluster of 66 through 90 repetitions per day, in which cross-sectional area increases were on the order of 0.08% per day.
summary: Optimal hypertrophy for both elbow flexors and quadriceps occurs with 4-6 sets, with diminishing returns at both lower and higher volumes.

content: Considering that a ceiling effect slows the rate of hypertrophy as one gains training experience, EIMD may be particularly relevant to the anabolic response in well-trained people.
summary: For experienced lifters, EIMD may be a more effective strategy for stimulating muscle growth due to the potential for a ceiling effect on hypertrophy with traditional training.

content: The cross-sectional area of the elbow flexors increased from 0.15% per day when 7 through 38 repetitions were performed per session to 0.26% per day when 42 through 66 repetitions were performed per session. With respect to total sets, hypertrophic increases peaked between 4 and 6 sets (0.24% increase in cross-sectional area per day); lesser responses were noted from the performance of 3 to 3.5 sets and ≥9 sets (0.17% and 0.18% increase per day, respectively).
summary: This chunk details the relationship between repetition range and set volume on elbow flexor hypertrophy.

content: The rate of increase diminished to 0.18% per day with volumes in the range of 74 through 120 repetitions per session, suggesting that very high volumes impair the hypertrophic response, perhaps by causing an overtrained state. With respect to the quadriceps, the findings were similar across a wide spectrum of clusters; 0.12% to 0.13% increases in cross-sectional area per day were seen from the performance of 21 through 100+ repetitions per session. The only exception was in the cluster of 66 through 90 repetitions per day, in which cross-sectional area increases were on the order of 0.08% per day.
summary: This chunk explores the impact of repetition volume on quadriceps and elbow flexor hypertrophy, noting a potential plateau at very high volumes.

content: Analysis of the optimal number of sets showed a benefit to higher volumes, and the greatest response was seen in studies incorporating ≥10 sets. Multiset protocols favoring high volumes of resistance training optimize the hypertrophic response. To avoid overtraining, people should increase volume progressively over the course of a training cycle and integrate periods of reduced training volume on a regular basis to facilitate the recovery process.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of high volume training for hypertrophy and provides recommendations for safe volume progression.

content: In a comprehensive long-term study on the topic, Radaelli and colleagues (599) compared muscle thickness of the upper extremities following 6 months of resistance training using 1, 3, or 5 sets per exercise. Training was carried out 3 days per week using loads equating to 8- to 12RM. Results showed a clear and marked positive relationship between volume and muscle growth. Effect sizes for the elbow flexors were trivial for the 1-set group (0.10), moderate for the 3-set group (0.73), and large for the 5-set group (1.10). Changes in muscle thickness in the elbow extensors were even more dependent on higher levels of volume; only trivial effect sizes were seen for both the 1- and 3-set groups (0.05), whereas the effect size was large for the 5-set group (2.33).
summary: This chunk summarizes a long-term study on the relationship between sets per exercise and muscle growth in the upper extremities.

content: Because hypertrophic increases did not plateau at the 5-set condition, it is possible that even higher volumes could yield further gains. Research shows that hypertrophic advantages from higher training volumes are associated with an augmented satellite cell response. Hanssen and colleagues (286) reported a greater increase in the number of satellite cells in the quadriceps femoris after 11 weeks of performing 3 sets compared to 1 set of lower-body exercises. No significant differences were seen in the upper-body musculature, suggesting an enhanced effect of volume on the leg muscles.
summary: This chunk explores the potential for further hypertrophy gains with even higher training volumes and links this to increased satellite cell activity.

content: These findings are consistent with previous work showing significantly greater hypertrophy from a multiple- versus single-set protocol in the lower body (11% vs. 7%, respectively), whereas no significant differences were noted in the upper-body musculature (624). Both studies were carried out with untrained subjects, so whether discrepancies persist in those with considerable lifting experience remains unclear.
summary: This chunk compares the effectiveness of single- versus multi-set training protocols for lower and upper body hypertrophy.

content: McBride et al. (468) studied 28 untrained young men and women. The McBride et al. (468) study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol consisting of 5 exercises including the biceps curl, leg press, chest fly, sit-up, and back extension. One group in the McBride et al. (468) study performed a single set of each exercise, while the other group performed 6 sets for the biceps curl and leg press and 3 sets for all other exercises. All subjects in the McBride et al. (468) study performed 6- to 15RM of the exercises twice weekly. The McBride et al. (468) study lasted 12 weeks. The McBride et al. (468) study used DXA to measure hypertrophy.
summary: This chunk describes the methodology of a study by McBride et al. (468) comparing single- and multi-set training protocols.

content: The Mitchell et al. (496) study involved 18 untrained young men. Participants in the Mitchell et al. (496) study were randomly assigned to perform 2 of 3 unilateral knee extension protocols: 3 sets at 30% of 1RM; 3 sets at 80% of 1RM; 1 set at 80% of 1RM. Each participant in the Mitchell et al. (496) study trained both legs and was thus assigned to 2 of the 3 possible training conditions. Training in the Mitchell et al. (496) study was carried out 3 days per week. The Mitchell et al. (496) study lasted 10 weeks. The Mitchell et al. (496) study used MRI to measure hypertrophy.
summary: This chunk outlines the design of a study by Mitchell et al. (496) investigating the effects of different training protocols on knee extension hypertrophy.

content: The Munn et al. (513) study involved 11 untrained young men and women. Participants in the Munn et al. (513) study were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of 1 or 3 sets per exercise in either a slow or fast fashion. Training in the Munn et al. (513) study was carried out using elbow flexion exercises at 6- to 8RM for 3 days per week. The Munn et al. (513) study lasted 6 weeks. The Munn et al. (513) study used skinfold and circumference measurements to measure hypertrophy.
summary: This chunk describes the methodology of a study by Munn et al. (513) examining the impact of set number and exercise tempo on elbow flexion hypertrophy.

content: The Oshow-ski et al. (553) study involved 27 resistance-trained young men. Participants in the Oshow-ski et al. (553) study were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of 1, 2, or 4 sets per exercise. All subjects in the Oshow-ski et al. (553) study performed a 4-day split-body routine working each of the major muscle groups with multiple exercises in a session at 7- to 12RM. The Oshow-ski et al. (553) study lasted 10 weeks.
summary: This chunk details the experimental design of a study by Oshow-ski et al. (553) investigating the effects of different set numbers on hypertrophy in resistance-trained individuals.

content: The Oshow-ski et al. (553) study involved 27 resistance-trained young men. Participants in the Oshow-ski et al. (553) study were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of 1, 2, or 4 sets per exercise. All subjects in the Oshow-ski et al. (553) study performed a 4-day split-body routine working each of the major muscle groups with multiple exercises in a session at 7- to 12RM. The Oshow-ski et al. (553) study lasted 10 weeks. The Oshow-ski et al. (553) study used ultrasound to measure hypertrophy.
summary: A study on resistance-trained men examined the effects of different set numbers (1, 2, or 4) on hypertrophy over 10 weeks.

content: The Radaelli et al. (597) study involved 20 untrained elderly women. Participants in the Radaelli et al. (597) study were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either 1 or 3 sets per exercise. All subjects in the Radaelli et al. (597) study performed 10 exercises targeting the entire body at 10- to 20RM. Training in the Radaelli et al. (597) study was carried out twice weekly. The Radaelli et al. (597) study lasted 13 weeks. The Radaelli et al. (597) study used ultrasound to measure hypertrophy.
summary: A study on untrained elderly women investigated the impact of 1 or 3 sets per exercise on hypertrophy over 13 weeks.

content: The Radaelli et al. (599) study involved 48 recreationally trained young men. Participants in the Radaelli et al. (599) study were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of 1, 3, or 5 sets per exercise. All subjects in the Radaelli et al. (599) study performed 8- to 12RM for multiple exercises for the entire body. Training in the Radaelli et al. (599) study was carried out 3 days per week. The Radaelli et al. (599) study lasted 6 months. The Radaelli et al. (599) study found that the 5-set condition resulted in significantly greater increases in elbow flexor muscle thickness compared to the other two conditions. Only the 3- and 5-set groups significantly increased elbow flexor muscle thickness from baseline in the Radaelli et al. (599) study. The Radaelli et al. (599) study found that the 5-set condition resulted in significantly greater increases in elbow extensor muscle thickness compared to the other two conditions. Only the 5-set group significantly increased elbow extensor thickness from baseline in the Radaelli et al. (599) study.
summary: A study on recreationally trained men investigated the effects of 1, 3, or 5 sets per exercise on elbow muscle thickness over 6 months.

content: Radaelli et al. (600) studied 27 untrained elderly women. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either 1 or 3 sets per exercise. All subjects performed 10 exercises targeting the entire body at 10- to 20RM. Training was carried out twice weekly for 6 weeks. Ultrasound measurements were taken to assess muscle thickness. No significant differences in muscle thickness were found between the 1-set and 3-set groups.
summary: A study on untrained elderly women compared the effects of 1 and 3 sets per exercise on muscle thickness over 6 weeks.

content: Radaelli et al. (598) studied 20 untrained elderly women. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either 1 or 3 sets per exercise. All subjects performed 10 exercises targeting the entire body at 6- to 20RM. Training was carried out twice weekly for 20 weeks. Ultrasound measurements were taken to assess muscle thickness. Significantly greater increases in quadriceps thickness were observed in the high-volume group.
summary: A study on untrained elderly women compared the effects of 1 and 3 sets per exercise on quadriceps thickness over 20 weeks.

content: Rhea et al. (611) studied 18 resistance-trained young men. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either 1 or 3 sets per exercise. All subjects performed 4- to 10RM on the bench press and leg press. Subjects also performed an additional set of multiple exercises considered unrelated to the bench press or leg press. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 12 weeks. BodPod measurements were taken to assess lean body mass. No significant differences in lean body mass were found between the 1-set and 3-set groups.
summary: A study on resistance-trained men examined the effects of 1 or 3 sets per exercise on lean body mass over 12 weeks.

content: The Munn et al. (513) study used skinfold and circumference measurements to measure hypertrophy.
summary: A study used skinfold and circumference measurements to assess hypertrophy.

content: Ribeiro et al. (612) studied 30 untrained elderly women. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol consisting of 8 exercises for the total body performed either 1 or 3 days per week. Training lasted for 12 weeks. BodPod measurements were taken to assess lean body mass. No significant differences in lean body mass were found between the 1-set and 3-set groups.
summary: A study by Ribeiro et al. examined the effects of 1 vs. 3 days of resistance training per week on lean body mass in untrained elderly women over 12 weeks.

content: Ronnestad et al. (624) studied 21 untrained young men. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol in which one group performed 3 sets of upper-body exercises and 1 set of lower-body exercises, while the other group performed 3 sets of lower-body exercises and 1 set of upper-body exercises. Training consisted of 8 exercises for the entire body performed at 7- to 10RM and carried out 3 days per week for 11 weeks. MRI measurements were taken to assess thigh muscle cross-sectional area. Significantly greater increases in thigh muscle cross-sectional area were observed for the higher-volume condition.
summary: Ronnestad et al. investigated the impact of upper- vs. lower-body training volume on thigh muscle growth in young men over 11 weeks.

content: Soon-este et al. (712) studied 8 untrained young men. The study used a within-subject crossover design in which all subjects performed a 2-day-per-week resistance training protocol of preacher curls so that one arm used 3 sets in a session and the other arm used a single set in the following session. Training was performed at 80% of 1RM and carried out 2 days per week for 12 weeks. MRI measurements were taken to assess upper-arm cross-sectional area. Significantly greater increases in upper-arm cross-sectional area were observed for the high-volume condition.
summary: Soon-este et al. compared the effects of single- vs. multi-set training on upper-arm muscle growth in a crossover design over 12 weeks.

content: Starkey et al. (722) studied 48 untrained mixed-aged men and women. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of knee flexions and extensions performed either 1 or 3 days per week. All subjects performed 8 to 12 reps for 3 days per week. Training lasted for 14 weeks. Ultrasound measurements were taken to assess the thickness of the anterior and posterior thigh muscles. No significant differences in the thickness of the anterior or posterior thigh muscles were found between the 1-day and 3-day groups, although only the high-volume group significantly increased hypertrophy of the vastus medialis relative to control.
summary: Starkey et al. examined the effects of 1 vs. 3 days of knee flexion/extension training on thigh muscle thickness in a mixed-aged population over 14 weeks.

content: Evidence for a dose–response relationship between volume and hypertrophy is compelling: higher training volumes are clearly and positively associated with greater muscular gains. Multiset routines totaling 40 to 70 repetitions per muscle group per session can be considered a general guideline for those with limited training experience based on the findings of Wernbom and colleagues (814). More advanced lifters seem to require greater volumes to maximize muscle protein accretion, perhaps double that of untrained people.
summary: Higher training volume is associated with greater muscle growth, with recommendations varying based on training experience.

content: Consistently employing high volumes over time hastens the onset of overtraining. Periodizing programming by progressively increasing volume over the course of a training cycle appears beneficial. Periods of reduced training volume should be integrated on a regular basis to facilitate the recovery process.
summary: Training volume should be periodised, with progressive increases followed by periods of reduced volume to prevent overtraining and promote recovery.

content: There is undoubtedly a limit above which additional volume confers no additional hypertrophic benefits. The relationship between volume and hypertrophy is hypothesized to follow an inverted-U curve, whereby muscle accretion peaks at a given volume load and, beyond this point, further increases in volume can actually impair muscular gains (figure 3.1) (298). The threshold for volume-related hypertrophic benefits varies based on genetics (see chapter 5); lifestyle-related factors such as nutritional status, daily stress levels, and sleep patterns also play a role in individual responses.
summary: There is an optimal training volume for hypertrophy, beyond which further increases may be detrimental. Individual responses vary based on genetics and lifestyle factors.

content: Some authors have posited that well-trained people require a particularly high training volume (>10 sets) to induce maximal hypertrophy (571).
summary: Highly trained individuals may require higher training volumes to maximize muscle growth.

content: Frequency of training pertains to the number of exercise sessions performed in a given period of time, generally a week (656). Frequency also includes the number of times a muscle group is worked over the course of a week.
summary: Training frequency refers to the number of exercise sessions and muscle group workouts per week.

content: All subjects performed 4- to 10RM on the bench press and leg press. All subjects performed 10-15 repetitions.
summary: Subjects in these studies performed exercises at a moderate intensity (4-10RM) with repetitions in the 10-15 range.

content: Subjects also performed an additional set of multiple exercises considered unrelated to the bench press or leg press.
summary: Subjects completed additional sets of exercises beyond the primary focus.

content: The relationship between volume and hypertrophy is hypothesized to follow an inverted-U curve, whereby muscle accretion peaks at a given volume load and, beyond this point, further increases in volume can actually impair muscular gains (figure 3.1) (298). The threshold for volume-related hypertrophic benefits varies based on genetics (see chapter 5); lifestyle-related factors such as nutritional status, daily stress levels, and sleep patterns also play a role in individual responses. Some authors have posited that well-trained people require a particularly high training volume (>10 sets) to induce maximal hypertrophy (571).
summary: The relationship between training volume and hypertrophy follows an inverted-U curve, with individual thresholds influenced by genetics and lifestyle factors.

content: Frequency of training pertains to the number of exercise sessions performed in a given period of time, generally a week (656). Frequency also includes the number of times a muscle group is worked over the course of a week. With respect to hypertrophy training, frequency can be varied to manipulate training volume. Neuromuscular factors limit how much volume can be incorporated into a single training session; beyond a given threshold, the quality of training begins to degrade.
summary: Training frequency, defined as the number of sessions per week and muscle group work, can be manipulated to adjust training volume, but neuromuscular limitations exist.

content: Split routines allow for a greater volume of work per muscle group per session, potentially enhancing muscular adaptations via the dose–response relationship between volume and hypertrophy. Distributing volume per muscle group over more frequent bouts can be an effective strategy for maintaining weekly volume with less fatigue per session. Hypertrophy-oriented routines generally involve a high volume of work per muscle group in a session but relatively infrequent training of each muscle group.
summary: Split routines prioritize higher volume per muscle group per session, while hypertrophy routines balance high session volume with less frequent training.

content: People often follow a split-body routine in which they perform multiple exercises for a specific muscle group in one training session. In comparison to a total-body routine, split routines allow total weekly training volume to be maintained or increased with fewer sets performed per training session and greater recovery afforded between sessions. Performing multiple exercises for a muscle group in the same bout heightens metabolic stress and thus may enhance anabolism.
summary: Split-body routines involve multiple exercises per muscle group per session, allowing for higher weekly volume and recovery compared to total-body routines.

content: A survey of competitive male bodybuilders revealed that more than 2/3 trained each muscle group only once per week, and none reported working a muscle group more than twice weekly; every respondent reported using a split-body routine. Total RNA has been shown to be elevated in humans 72 hours after a bout of maximal isometric electrical contractions. Because the majority of skeletal muscle RNA is ribosomal, these findings suggest that a cell’s potential for protein synthesis remains heightened even beyond the 2-day time point.
summary: Competitive bodybuilders predominantly use split routines, training each muscle group once or twice weekly. Elevated RNA levels 72 hours post-exercise suggest prolonged protein synthesis potential.

content: Metabolically fatigued muscle fibers display a greater membrane permeability consequent to an increase in free calcium ions, leading to the activation of potassium channels and proteolytic enzymes. Performing a multiset, high-volume routine consistent with hypertrophy training protocols may thus require at least 48 to 72 hours of rest between workouts for the same muscle group to ensure adequate repair, recovery, and adaptation.
summary: Muscle recovery requires 48-72 hours after high-volume training to allow for repair and adaptation, as metabolic fatigue increases membrane permeability and activates proteolytic enzymes.

content: Research examining the effects of frequency on long-term hypertrophic adaptations in humans is limited. A systematic review by Wernbom and colleagues determined that although novice lifters benefit from training muscle groups up to 4 days a week, those with more experience realize optimal gains with a weekly frequency of 2 or 3 days. There was insufficient data for determining whether higher frequencies would be beneficial in a well-trained population. The analysis did not account for greater volumes associated with higher training frequencies, thereby confounding the ability to draw conclusions on the specific impact of varying the number of weekly training sessions.
summary: Research on training frequency's impact on hypertrophy is limited, with novice lifters benefiting from up to 4 days of training per week, while experienced lifters see optimal gains with 2-3 days. More research is needed to determine the benefits of higher frequencies.

content: Only a handful of studies have examined training frequency when volumes are equated. Ribeiro and colleagues investigated a group of 10 competitive male bodybuilders who were randomly assigned to perform a split-body resistance training program either 4 or 6 times per week. Training for each exercise involved 4 sets that descended from 12RM to 6RM in a pyramid fashion (i.e., 12-, 10-, 8-, and 6RMs on successive sets, respectively). Volume was equated between the groups, in that those training 4 times a week trained more muscle groups in a session than did those training 6 times a week. Those training 6 times a week had similar increases in fat-free mass compared to those training 3 times a week. The 90-minute training sessions with higher per-session training volumes did not seem to compromise muscle growth compared to more frequent 60-minute sessions.
summary: Studies comparing training frequency while equating volume are limited. Research on bodybuilders suggests similar muscle growth with 4 or 6 training days per week, and higher per-session volume did not hinder muscle growth.

content: General hypertrophy training guidelines recommend allowing at least 48 hours between resistance bouts for the same muscle group. It has been surmised that training before muscle protein synthesis has fully run its course—which lasts up to approximately 48 hours postexercise—impairs muscle protein accretion. Research in rodents shows that myogenic responses are attenuated when recovery occurs less than 48 hours after.
summary: General hypertrophy guidelines recommend at least 48 hours of rest between training the same muscle group, as training before full muscle protein synthesis recovery may impair muscle growth.

content: Benton and colleagues (69) compared body composition changes in untrained middle-aged women performing either a total-body or split-body routine on a volume-equated basis. Those in the total-body group worked each muscle group 3 days a week; those in the split-body group worked each muscle group twice weekly. After 8 weeks, similar increases were seen in lean mass in both groups. McLester and colleagues (480) evaluated body composition changes between training 1 and 3 days a week on a volume-equated basis in resistance-trained men. After 12 weeks, the 3-day-a-week group displayed markedly greater increases in fat-free mass than those training once weekly.
summary: Studies comparing total-body and split-body routines on volume-equated bases show similar lean mass gains in untrained women, while resistance-trained men see greater fat-free mass increases with 3 days of training per week compared to 1 day.

content: In a study from my lab that compared muscular adaptations in a split- versus total-body routine in well-trained men (666), participants were pair-matched according to baseline strength and then randomly assigned to a split-body routine or a total-body routine. After 8 weeks, the group performing total-body workouts showed greater gains in both the upper- and lower-extremity muscles compared to those performing the split protocol. 16 of the 19 subjects reported regularly training each muscle once per week using a split routine prior to participating in the study.
summary: A study comparing split- and total-body routines in well-trained men found greater muscle gains in both upper and lower body with total-body training.

content: McLester and colleagues (480) evaluated body composition changes between training 1 and 3 days a week on a volume-equated basis in resistance-trained men. After 12 weeks, the 3-day-a-week group displayed markedly greater increases in fat-free mass than those training once weekly.
summary: A study comparing training frequency (1 vs. 3 days/week) in resistance-trained men found greater fat-free mass gains with 3 days/week.

content: In a study from my lab that compared muscular adaptations in a split- versus total-body routine in well-trained men (666), participants were pair-matched according to baseline strength and then randomly assigned to a split-body routine or a total-body routine. After 8 weeks, the group performing total-body workouts showed greater gains in both the upper- and lower-extremity muscles compared to those performing the split protocol. 16 of the 19 subjects reported regularly training each muscle once per week using a split routine prior to participating in the study.
summary: A study comparing split-body vs. total-body routines in well-trained men found greater muscle gains with total-body training.

content: A popular strategy to increase volume by manipulating training frequency is to split up a workout by performing multiple sessions in a day (often morning and evening). Häkkinen and Kallinen (274) found that greater increases in muscle cross-sectional area occurred when athletes performed 2 sessions per day rather than when they performed all sets in a single bout. Hartmann and colleagues (291) found that once-daily training produced slightly greater cross-sectional area increases compared to twice-daily splits in a group of nationally competitive male weightlifters over a 3-week period, although the differences were not statistically significant. The conflicting results leave open the possibility that double-split routines are a viable option for hypertrophy training provided that the person can fit such an approach into his or her daily schedule.
summary: Studies examining the effects of double-split training vs. single-session training on muscle hypertrophy yielded mixed results.

content: Arazi and Asadi (41) conducted a study with 39 untrained young men. The subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of 12 exercises targeting the entire body divided into a 1-, 2-, or 3-day-per-week schedule. All subjects trained at 60% to 80% of 1RM. The study duration was 8 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using circumference measurements. No significant differences in arm or thigh girth were found between the conditions.
summary: A study comparing 1-, 2-, and 3-day-per-week training schedules in untrained men found no significant differences in hypertrophy.

content: Benton et al. (69) conducted a study with 21 untrained middle-aged women. The subjects were randomly assigned to resistance training either 3 nonconsecutive days per week using a total-body protocol of 3 sets of 8 exercises or 4 consecutive days per week using an alternating split-body protocol of 3 sets of 6 upper-body exercises or 6 sets of 3 lower-body exercises. All subjects performed 8 to 12 reps at 50% to 80% of 1RM. The study duration was 8 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using BodPod. No significant differences in lean body mass were found between the conditions.
summary: A study comparing total-body vs. split-body training in untrained middle-aged women found no significant differences in lean body mass.

content: Calder et al. (115) conducted a study with 30 untrained young women. The subjects were randomly assigned to either a total-body group performing 4 upper-body exercises and 3 lower-body exercises twice a week or a split-body group performing the lower-body exercises on separate days from the upper-body exercises so that training was carried out over 4 weekly sessions. All subjects performed 5 sets of 6- to 12RM to concentric muscle failure. The study duration was 20 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using DXA. No significant differences in lean mass were found between the groups.
summary: A study comparing total-body vs. split-body training in untrained young women found no significant differences in lean mass.

content: Candow and Burke (120) conducted a study with 29 untrained middle-aged men and women. The subjects were randomly assigned to 9 resistance training exercises for the total body either 2 times per week performing 3 sets of 10 reps or 3 times per week performing 2 sets of 10 reps. The study duration was 6 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using DXA. No significant differences in lean body mass were found between the conditions.
summary: A study comparing 2 vs. 3 days/week training frequency in untrained middle-aged men and women found no significant differences in lean body mass.

content: Carneiro et al. (124) conducted a study with 53 untrained elderly women. The subjects were randomly assigned to a total-body resistance training protocol performed either twice or thrice each week. All subjects performed a single set of 10 to 15 reps for 8 exercises per session. The study duration was 12 weeks.
summary: A study examining the effects of twice vs. thrice weekly total-body training in untrained elderly women.

content: Carneiro et al. (124) conducted a study with 53 untrained elderly women. The subjects were randomly assigned to a total-body resistance training protocol performed either twice or thrice each week. All subjects performed a single set of 10 to 15 reps for 8 exercises per session. The study duration was 12 weeks. Volume was not equated. Hypertrophy was measured using DXA. No significant differences in lean body mass were found between the conditions. No significant differences in skeletal muscle mass were found between the groups.
summary: A study on untrained elderly women investigated the effects of twice- versus thrice-weekly total-body resistance training on hypertrophy, finding no significant differences in lean body mass or skeletal muscle mass.

content: Gentil et al. (244) conducted a study with 30 untrained young men.
