The subjects were randomly assigned to 8 upper-body resistance training exercises performed either in a single session once per week or split into 2 sessions of 4 exercises performed twice per week. Training consisted of 3 sets at 8- to 12RM. The study duration was 10 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using Ultrasound. No significant differences in elbow flexor thickness were found between the groups.
summary: A study on untrained young men compared the effects of single- versus split-session upper-body resistance training on hypertrophy, revealing no significant differences in elbow flexor thickness.

content: Hakkinen et al. (274) conducted a study with 10 resistance-trained young women. The study was a within-subject crossover in which all subjects performed a resistance training protocol as a single session 3.5 days per week and then with the training volume split into twice-daily training sessions for a total of 7 weekly sessions. The study duration was 3 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using Ultrasound. Greater increases in quadriceps thickness were found in the higher-frequency condition.
summary: A crossover study on resistance-trained young women compared single- versus twice-daily resistance training, finding greater quadriceps hypertrophy with the higher-frequency protocol.

content: Hartman et al. (291) conducted a study with 10 elite-level male weightlifters. The subjects were randomly assigned to an Olympic weightlifting protocol performed either as a single session 4 days per week or split into twice-daily training sessions separated by 3 hours for a total of 8 weekly sessions. Loads were 80% to 95% of 1RM. The study duration was 3 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using Ultrasound. No significant differences in thickness of the quadriceps femoris were found between the conditions.
summary: A study on elite male weightlifters compared single- versus twice-daily Olympic weightlifting, finding no significant differences in quadriceps hypertrophy.

content: McLester et al. (480) conducted a study with 25 recreationally trained young men and women. The subjects were randomly assigned to resistance training either 1 day per week of 3 sets to failure or 3 days per week of 1 set to failure. All subjects performed 9 exercises for the total body. The study duration was 12 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using skinfold technique and circumference measurements. A nonsignificant trend for greater increases in lean body mass was found in the higher-frequency condition.
summary: A study on recreationally trained young adults compared 1- versus 3-day-per-week resistance training to failure, observing a nonsignificant trend for greater lean body mass increases with the higher-frequency protocol.

content: Ribeiro et al. (614) conducted a study with 10 elite male bodybuilders. The subjects were randomly assigned to either a 4- or 6-day-per-week split-body resistance training routine. Subjects performed the same 23 exercises the same number of times per week. The distribution of exercises was more concentrated in the 4-day-per-week condition. The protocol involved 6- to 12RM for all exercises except those for the calves and abdominals, which were performed at 15- to 20RM.
summary: A study on elite male bodybuilders compared 4- versus 6-day-per-week split-body resistance training routines, with a more concentrated exercise distribution in the 4-day protocol.

content: Ribeiro et al. (614) conducted a study with 10 elite male bodybuilders. The subjects were randomly assigned to either a 4- or 6-day-per-week split-body resistance training routine. Subjects performed the same 23 exercises the same number of times per week. The distribution of exercises was more concentrated in the 4-day-per-week condition. The protocol involved 6- to 12RM for all exercises except those for the calves and abdominals, which were performed at 15- to 20RM. The study duration was 4 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using DXA. No significant differences in lean mass were found between the conditions.
summary: A study compared the effects of 4- and 6-day split-body training routines on hypertrophy in elite male bodybuilders, finding no significant difference in lean mass gains.

content: Schoenfeld et al. (666) conducted a study with 19 resistance-trained young men. The subjects were randomly assigned to resistance training either 1 day per week using a split-body routine or 3 days per week using a total-body routine. All subjects performed 8 to 12 reps of 7 exercises for the entire body. The study duration was 8 weeks. Volume was equated. Hypertrophy was measured using Ultrasound. Significantly greater increases in elbow flexor muscle thickness and a trend for greater increases in vastus lateralis thickness were found for the greater-frequency, total-body condition.
summary: A study compared the effects of split-body and total-body training on hypertrophy in resistance-trained young men, finding greater muscle thickness gains with the higher-frequency total-body routine.

content: Modulating training frequency is an effective strategy to manipulate volume loads. There appears to be a benefit to higher training frequencies, at least over short-term training protocols. Total-body routines represent an attractive option for maximizing training frequency for each muscle group. Split routines allow for a greater volume of work per muscle group per session, potentially enhancing muscular adaptations via the dose–response relationship between volume and hypertrophy.
summary: Training frequency can be manipulated to influence volume, with higher frequencies potentially leading to greater hypertrophy, particularly with total-body routines.

content: A case can be made for periodizing frequency over time, altering the number of times a muscle group is trained weekly in accordance with individual response. The lifter can maximize hypertrophy while reducing the potential for overtraining by alternating total-body and split routines (e.g., progressing from a cycle of 3 weekly sessions to 4 weekly sessions the next cycle, and then culminating in a cycle of 6 weekly sessions).
summary: Periodizing training frequency, alternating between total-body and split routines, can optimize hypertrophy and minimize overtraining risk.

content: The load lifted is widely considered one of the most important factors in the hypertrophic response to resistance training. Intensity of load refers to the percentage of 1RM employed in a given exercise. If someone has a maximal bench press of 100 lb (45.5 kg) and performs a set with 80 lb (36.4 kg), then the intensity of load would be expressed as 80% of 1RM. Intensity of load is often categorized into loading zones that correspond to repetition ranges.
summary: Load intensity, expressed as a percentage of 1RM, is a crucial factor in hypertrophy, with different repetition ranges associated with varying intensities.

content: Typically, repetition ranges are classified as heavy (1- to 5RM), medium (6- to 12RM), and light (15+RM). Formulas have been designed to estimate repetitions at a given percentage of 1RM, at best they can provide only a crude approximation of the relationship between repetitions and the percentage of 1RM. Genetic factors (e.g., muscle fiber typing, internal moment arm length), physiological factors (e.g., buffering capacity), and exercise types (e.g., upper body versus lower body, single joint versus multijoint) affect the generalizability of values.
summary: Repetition ranges are categorized as heavy, medium, and light, with formulas providing estimations of repetitions at specific intensities. However, individual factors influence the relationship between load and repetitions.

content: Hoeger and colleagues (310) found that a load of 80% of 1RM corresponded to a 10RM in the bench press, lat pulldown, and knee extension; however, this intensity of load varied from 6RM for the leg curl and 7- to 8RM for the arm curl, to 15RM for the leg press. The accuracy of these formulas declines substantially as loads become progressively lighter. Another study showed that, for individual subjects, repetitions to failure in the leg press ranged between 7 and 24 at 75% of 1RM, whereas the disparity widened to 30 to 71 at 30% of 1RM.
summary: Load-repetition relationships vary depending on the exercise and individual factors, with formulas providing only approximate estimations, especially at lighter loads.

content: Wernbom and colleagues (814) concluded that maximal hypertrophy is achieved through the use of a medium-repetition range. This hypothesis is primarily based on an extrapolation of mechanistic factors associated with the hypertrophic response to resistance training. Heavy loading is generally believed to promote neural adaptations and to have lesser effects on hypertrophy.
summary: Research suggests that a medium-repetition range (6-12RM) is optimal for maximizing muscle hypertrophy, while heavy lifting primarily benefits neural adaptations.

content: High intensities of load (>85% of 1RM) naturally result in high levels of mechanical tension on muscles. Because the duration of a heavy set is short (<15 seconds), energy during such training is primarily derived from the ATP-PC system and little contribution occurs from fast glycolysis. Thus, metabolite accumulation is relatively low, which is supported by research showing that peripheral fatigue induced via metabolic stress was significantly reduced when training in a low-repetition range (5 repetitions per set) compared to sets carried
summary: Heavy lifting (85%+ 1RM) primarily utilizes the ATP-PC system, resulting in lower metabolite accumulation and less metabolic stress compared to lighter loads.

content: Sets of ≥15 repetitions generally last 45 seconds or more, requiring the majority of energy production to be derived from the fast glycolytic system. This results in a substantial buildup of metabolites and acidosis, and generates a significant muscle pump.
summary: Higher repetition sets (≥15) rely heavily on the fast glycolytic system, leading to metabolite buildup, acidosis, and a pronounced muscle pump.

content: High intensities of load (>85% of 1RM) naturally result in high levels of mechanical tension on muscles. Because the duration of a heavy set is short (<15 seconds), energy during such training is primarily derived from the ATP-PC system and little contribution occurs from fast glycolysis. Thus, metabolite accumulation is relatively low, which is supported by research showing that peripheral fatigue induced via metabolic stress was significantly reduced when training in a low-repetition range (5 repetitions per set) compared to sets carried out for longer durations.
summary: High-intensity training (above 85% 1RM) primarily utilizes the ATP-PC system, resulting in low metabolite accumulation and high mechanical tension.

content: Sets of ≥15 repetitions generally last 45 seconds or more, requiring the majority of energy production to be derived from the fast glycolytic system. This results in a substantial buildup of metabolites and acidosis, and generates a significant muscle pump.
summary: High-repetition sets (≥15 reps) rely heavily on the fast glycolytic system, leading to metabolite buildup and muscle pump.

content: Training in a medium-repetition range is purported to provide an optimal combination of mechanical tension and metabolic stress for maximizing hypertrophic adaptations. Loads during such training are heavy enough to recruit the majority of fibers in the target musculature and to maintain their stimulation over a sufficient period of time. Moreover, sets generally last between 20 and 40 seconds, requiring a substantial contribution from fast glycolysis and correspondingly generating high levels of metabolic stress.
summary: Medium-repetition training (20-40 seconds per set) balances mechanical tension and metabolic stress for optimal hypertrophy.

content: Because of these factors, medium loading is often referred to as the hypertrophy range.
summary: Medium loading is considered optimal for hypertrophy due to its balance of mechanical tension and metabolic stress.

content: Recruitment during traditional resistance training under dynamic conditions is less clear. Type IIa fibers began to be recruited at 30% of 1RM, and about half of these fibers showed glycogen loss as the load approached 50% of 1RM. However, the Type IIax and IIx fibers were activated only when the load reached 60% of 1RM.
summary: Muscle fiber recruitment during dynamic resistance training is load-dependent, with Type IIa fibers activated earlier than Type IIax and IIx fibers.

content: Research has shown a corresponding increase in EMG amplitude during fatiguing contractions, ostensibly as a result of an increased contribution of higher-threshold motor units recruited to maintain force output. It has therefore been postulated that training to the point of concentric muscular failure, regardless of the magnitude of load, ultimately results in the recruitment of the full spectrum of available motor units.
summary: EMG amplitude increases during fatiguing contractions, suggesting recruitment of higher-threshold motor units. Training to failure likely recruits the full spectrum of motor units regardless of load.

content: My lab carried out two experiments to determine the minimum threshold for activating all available fibers in working muscle. Both mean and peak muscle activation were markedly greater during the heavy-load condition compared to the light-load condition (by 57% and 29%, respectively). Importantly, not a single subject displayed equal or greater activation during low-load training.
summary: Research suggests that intensities above 50% of 1RM are needed for full motor unit activation.

content: Although peak EMG amplitude was similar between conditions, mean activation and the area under the curve were significantly greater when training with 80% of 1RM versus 50% of 1RM in the bench press. Intensities above 50% of 1RM are needed for fully activating the entire motor unit pool. Training at a higher percentage of 1RM creates more recruitment and stimulation of higher-threshold motor units upon initiation of a set and trains muscle at an earlier point than when using light loads. Training with light loads maintains tension in the lower-threshold motor units for an extended period.
summary: Higher training intensities (above 50% 1RM) lead to greater motor unit activation and earlier recruitment compared to lighter loads.

content: Type I fiber hypertrophy is significantly greater when training with low loads than when training with high loads.
summary: Low loads promote greater Type I fiber hypertrophy compared to high loads.

content: Martineau and Gardiner (454) subjected rat plantaris muscles to peak concentric, eccentric, and isometric actions via electrical stimulation. Results showed tension-dependent phosphorylation of JNK and ERK1/2, and higher mechanical tension resulted in progressively greater phosphorylation. Peak tension is a better predictor of MAPK phosphorylation than either time under tension or rate of tension development.
summary: Mechanical tension, particularly peak tension, is a stronger driver of MAPK phosphorylation than time under tension or rate of tension development.

content: A linear relationship was found between time under tension and signaling of JNK, whereas the rate of tension change showed no effect, highlighting the importance of time under tension in anabolic signaling.
summary: Time under tension positively correlates with JNK signaling, emphasizing its importance in anabolic processes.

content: Hulmi and colleagues (327) found that early-phase postexercise MAPK and p70S6K phosphorylation responses were significantly greater following 5 sets of leg press exercises at 10RM compared to 15 sets at 1RM. Taylor and colleagues (749) demonstrated that the ERK1/2 pathway was similarly activated at the upper and lower limits of the medium-repetition range (85% vs. 65% of 1RM), but a strong trend was seen for greater circulating IGF-1 release at the higher intensity of load. Popov and colleagues (589) displayed diverse responses in anabolic signaling and myogenic gene expression following resistance exercise performed at 74% versus 54% of 1RM.
summary: Studies show that both moderate and heavy loads (10RM, 1RM, 74% 1RM, 54% 1RM) elicit anabolic signaling, with potential differences in specific pathways and growth factors.

content: Increases in heavy-load and moderate-load training are similar in the initial hours following resistance training on a volume-equated basis. Muscle protein synthesis appears to be blunted at lower intensities of load (<60% of 1RM) when training is not carried out to failure. Burd and colleagues (105) reported that muscle protein synthesis over 24 hours was actually greater when training to failure at 30% of 1RM compared to 90% of 1RM.
summary: Heavy and moderate loads elicit similar initial muscle protein synthesis responses, while lower intensities (<60% 1RM) require training to failure for optimal protein synthesis.

content: Findings suggest a robust acute response to resistance training regardless of intensity of load, provided training is carried out to muscular failure and, in the case of heavy loading, volume is equated. The responses are complex and suggest a synergism to training across loading zones.
summary: Resistance training, regardless of load, elicits a robust acute response when training to failure. Synergistic effects may occur across various loading zones.

content: Choi and colleagues (136) randomly assigned 11 young men to either a bulk-up protocol of 9 sets of knee extensions at 40% to 80% of 1RM with 30 seconds of rest between sets or a power-up protocol consisting of 5 sets at 90% of 1RM with 3 minutes of rest between sets. After 8 weeks, the results showed significantly greater increases in quadriceps hypertrophy for the bulk-up group. Masuda and colleagues (461) reported similar findings when employing an identical protocol.
summary: Studies show that higher training volume with moderate loads (40-80% 1RM) leads to greater hypertrophy compared to lower volume with heavier loads (90% 1RM).

content: All of the aforementioned studies used untrained subjects.
summary: Previous studies on hypertrophy and training protocols primarily focused on untrained individuals.

content: My lab (661) investigated heavy versus moderate loading in 20 well-trained men who were randomly assigned to one of two groups: a hypertrophy group that performed a bodybuilding-style routine, or a strength group that performed a powerlifting-style routine. The hypertrophy group protocol was a split routine in which each muscle was worked once per week with 3 exercises per session, performing 3 sets of 10 repetitions and resting 90 seconds between sets.
summary: A study investigated the effects of heavy versus moderate loading in well-trained men using bodybuilding and powerlifting protocols.

content: My lab (661) investigated heavy versus moderate loading in 20 well-trained men who were randomly assigned to one of two groups: a hypertrophy group that performed a bodybuilding-style routine, or a strength group that performed a powerlifting-style routine. The hypertrophy group protocol was a split routine in which each muscle was worked once per week with 3 exercises per session, performing 3 sets of 10 repetitions and resting 90 seconds between sets. The strength group protocol was a total-body routine in which each muscle was worked 3 times per week with one exercise per session, performing 7 sets of 3 repetitions and resting 3 minutes between sets. Volume load was equated so that subjects in both groups lifted approximately the same amount of weight per week. All sets were performed to the point of momentary concentric muscular failure.
summary: This study compared a bodybuilding-style routine (hypertrophy) to a powerlifting-style routine (strength) in well-trained men, both with equated volume.

content: After 8 weeks, subjects in both groups significantly increased biceps muscle thickness, and no differences were seen between groups. Subjects in both groups also significantly increased 1RM strength, but the strength group had greater increases in the bench press and showed a trend for greater increases in the squat. It should be noted that per-session training time in the strength group was 70 minutes, whereas that in the hypertrophy group was 17 minutes.
summary: After 8 weeks, both groups increased biceps size and strength, but the strength group showed greater gains in bench press and squat, likely due to longer training sessions.

content: After 8 weeks, the results showed significantly greater increases in quadriceps hypertrophy for the bulk-up group.
summary: A study found greater quadriceps hypertrophy in a group focused on bodybuilding.

content: All of the aforementioned studies used untrained subjects.
summary: Previous studies on hypertrophy and strength training involved untrained individuals.

content: Hypertrophy can be achieved in all loading zones. Low-load training emphasizes metabolic stress and promotes the greatest increases in local muscular endurance, whereas low-repetition, high-load training requires high mechanical tension and enhances the ability to lift heavier loads as a result of greater neural adaptations. There appears to be a fiber type–specific response in which heavy-load training produces greater cross-sectional area increases in Type II fibers and light loads have a preferential effect on Type I hypertrophy.
summary: Hypertrophy can be achieved across various loading zones, with low loads emphasizing metabolic stress and high loads focusing on mechanical tension and Type II fiber growth.

content: If the primary goal is maximizing hypertrophy without regard to strength-related factors, then training across a wide spectrum of repetition ranges (1 through 20+) is recommended to exploit all possible avenues for the complete development of the whole muscle. There is merit to focusing on a medium-repetition range (6- to 12RM), because it provides high levels of mechanical tension sufficient to stimulate the full array of fiber types while allowing for sufficient training volumes.
summary: For maximizing hypertrophy, training across various repetition ranges is beneficial, but a medium range (6-12RM) is recommended for balanced muscle development.

content: Incorporating heavy loading (1- to 5RM) enhances strength, which ultimately allows the use of heavier loads during medium-repetition lifting. Additionally, light-load training should be included both to ensure the optimal development of Type I fibers and to improve the buffering capacity of muscle so that additional repetitions can be performed at a given medium intensity of load.
summary: Incorporating heavy and light loads into training enhances both strength and muscle fiber development.

content: If the goal is to promote hypertrophy to maximize strength, there appears little reason to employ loads less than approximately 70% of 1RM.
summary: For hypertrophy-focused strength training, loads should be at least 70% of 1RM.

content: The compelling body of research indicates the presence of a strength–endurance continuum, in which lighter loads promote the ability to carry out submaximal resistive efforts at the expense of maximal force production (119). Increases in Type I fiber hypertrophy, as would be expected when training with low loads, have limited transfer to strength-related improvements.
summary: Training with lighter loads primarily improves endurance, while heavier loads are better for strength gains.

content: Bodybuilding-type training produced similar hypertrophy (as well as nearly similar strength increases) in about 1/4 of the time that powerlifting-type training did.
summary: Bodybuilding-style training achieved similar hypertrophy and strength gains in a shorter time compared to powerlifting.

content: Time constraints associated with the strength group allowed for only three major body areas to be worked in the study: chest (using upper-body pushing exercises), back (using upper-body pulling exercises), and thighs. The efficiency of the hypertrophy group would have allowed for additional volume in the muscle groups trained or the inclusion of exercises for other muscle groups, or both. Working specific muscles (and aspects of muscles), such as the middle and posterior deltoids, the hamstrings, and the calves, alone would have benefited overall muscle hypertrophy.
summary: The strength training protocol focused on three major muscle groups due to time constraints, while the bodybuilding routine could have been more comprehensive.

content: Almost all complained of sore joints and general fatigue, and the two dropouts from this group were because of joint-related injury.
summary: The strength training group experienced more joint pain and fatigue, leading to dropouts.

content: Although mechanistically heavy and moderately heavy weights appear to promote similar hypertrophic responses when volumes are equated, from an application standpoint, it simply is not practical to constantly lift heavy loads at the high volumes needed for maximizing muscle growth.
summary: While heavy and moderate weights can produce similar hypertrophy, the practicality of constantly lifting heavy weights for muscle growth is limited.

content: Research shows that muscle hypertrophy is diminished when loads higher than 60% of 1RM are not carried out to a point that approaches muscular failure.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy is reduced when loads above 60% of 1RM are not performed to near failure.

content: In a study by Holm and colleagues (311), subjects performed 8 repetitions of knee extensions on one leg and 36 repetitions of knee extensions on the other leg. In the light-load condition, subjects performed 1 repetition every 5th second for 3 minutes, thereby reducing the effects of fatigue; training in the heavy-load condition was carried out in a traditional fashion. Ten sets were performed each session, and training occurred 3 days a week. After 12 weeks, muscle cross-sectional area was 3-fold greater in the group that performed heavy-load training.
summary: A study compared heavy and light load training for knee extensions, finding significantly greater muscle growth with heavy loads.

content: These findings correlate with acute data showing an attenuation of muscle protein synthesis when training substantially short of failure at intensities of load below 60% of 1RM (399).
summary: Muscle protein synthesis is reduced when training is not performed to near failure at loads below 60% of 1RM.

content: A recent meta-analysis (662) sought to provide clarity on the topic by evaluating hypertrophic adaptations in randomized experimental trials that compared resistance training at ≥65% of 1RM to that at ≤60% of 1RM; both conditions were carried out to failure. Nine studies encompassing 251 subjects met inclusion criteria. Meta-analytic data showed a trend for greater growth in the heavier-load condition, but results were not statistically different. Three found a significant advantage for high-load training (119, 311, 670), and six showed no significant differences between low-load and high-load training (412, 496, 543, 588, 743, 744). Two of the six studies that failed to show significant differences demonstrated clear relative hypertrophic advantages for higher-load training, and cross-sectional area increases of 34% to 150% (588, 744).
summary: A meta-analysis of studies comparing high and low load training to failure found a trend towards greater hypertrophy with higher loads, but the results were not statistically significant.

content: All of these studies were carried out with untrained subjects, thereby limiting the generalizability of the findings. Subsequently, my lab carried out a longitudinal study (665) that compared muscular adaptations in low-load versus high-load training in resistance-trained subjects.
summary: Previous studies on this topic involved untrained individuals, so further research with resistance-trained subjects was conducted.

content: Masuda and colleagues (461) reported similar findings when employing an identical protocol.
summary: Previous research using a similar protocol to the one described in this study also found similar results.

content: Meta-analytic data showed a trend for greater growth in the heavier-load condition, but results were not statistically different. Three found a significant advantage for high-load training (119, 311, 670), and six showed no significant differences between low-load and high-load training (412, 496, 543, 588, 743, 744). Two of the six studies that failed to show significant differences demonstrated clear relative hypertrophic advantages for higher-load training, and cross-sectional area increases of 34% to 150% (588, 744). All of these studies were carried out with untrained subjects, thereby limiting the generalizability of the findings.
summary: A meta-analysis of studies on low-load vs. high-load training showed mixed results, with some studies finding significant advantages for high-load training, while others did not. All studies involved untrained subjects.

content: Subsequently, my lab carried out a longitudinal study (665) that compared muscular adaptations in low-load versus high-load training in resistance-trained subjects. Eighteen young men with an average of more than 3 years resistance training experience were randomly assigned to perform either a medium-repetition (8- to 12RM) or a high-repetition (25- to 35RM) routine. All other variables were rigidly controlled, and subjects performed 3 sets of 7 exercises for the upper and lower body on 3 nonconsecutive days a week. After 8 weeks, both groups significantly increased thickness of the biceps brachii, triceps brachii, and quadriceps femoris, but no statistical differences were noted between groups. Consistent with the concept of a strength–endurance continuum, gains in maximal muscle strength were markedly higher in the medium-repetition group, and improvements in local muscular endurance were significantly greater in the high-repetition group.
summary: A study compared low-load vs. high-load training in resistance-trained subjects, finding significant muscle growth in both groups but greater strength gains in the medium-repetition group and greater endurance gains in the high-repetition group.

content: Campos et al. (2011) studied 32 untrained young men. The study by Campos et al. (2011) randomly assigned participants to high-intensity (3- to 5RM), intermediate-intensity (9- to 11RM), or low-intensity (20- to 28RM) exercise. Exercise consisted of 2 to 4 sets of squat, leg press, and knee extensions, performed 3 days per week. The study duration was 8 weeks. Muscle biopsy was used to measure hypertrophy. Significant increases in CSA were observed for high-intensity exercise. No significant increase in CSA was observed for low-intensity exercise. Significantly greater increases in muscle strength were observed for the high-intensity group compared to the low-intensity group.
summary: A study compared high-intensity, intermediate-intensity, and low-intensity exercise in untrained men, finding greater muscle hypertrophy and strength gains in the high-intensity group compared to the low-intensity group.

content: Holm et al. (2011) studied 11 untrained young men. The study by Holm et al. (2011) used a random, counterbalanced design. Participants performed 10 sets of unilateral knee extensions, training one leg at 70% of 1RM and the contralateral leg at 15.5% of 1RM. Training was performed 3 days per week. The study duration was 12 weeks. MRI was used to measure hypertrophy. Significantly greater increases in quadriceps CSA were observed for high- vs. low-load exercise.
summary: A study compared high-load vs. low-load unilateral knee extensions in untrained men, finding greater muscle hypertrophy in the high-load group.

content: Leger et al. (2012) studied 24 untrained middle-aged men. The study by Leger et al. (2012) randomly assigned participants to either low-intensity (3- to 5RM) or high-intensity (20- to 28RM) exercise. Exercise consisted of 2 to 4 sets of squats, leg presses, and knee extensions, performed 3 days per week. The study duration was 8 weeks. CT was used to measure hypertrophy. No differences in CSA were observed between low- and high-intensity exercise.
summary: A study compared low-load vs. high-load training in untrained middle-aged men, finding no difference in muscle hypertrophy between the groups.

content: Mitchell et al. (2016) studied 18 untrained young men. The study by Mitchell et al. (2016) randomly assigned participants to perform 2 of 3 unilateral knee extension protocols: 3 sets at 30% of 1RM, 3 sets at 80% of 1RM, and 1 set at 80% of 1RM. Tempo was consistent between conditions. Training was carried out 3 days per week. The study duration was 10 weeks. MRI and muscle biopsy were used to measure hypertrophy. No differences in CSA were observed between low- and high-intensity exercise. Significantly greater strength gains were observed in the high- vs. low-load group.
summary: A study compared low-load vs. high-load unilateral knee extensions in untrained men, finding no difference in muscle hypertrophy but greater strength gains in the high-load group.

content: Ogasawara et al. (2015) studied 9 untrained young men. The study by Ogasawara et al. (2015) used a nonrandomized crossover design.
summary: A study compared different training protocols in untrained men, but the specific details of the protocols and outcomes are not provided.

content: Ogasawara et al. (2015) studied 9 untrained young men. The study by Ogasawara et al. (2015) used a nonrandomized crossover design. Participants performed 4 sets of bench press exercises at 75% of 1RM. Training was carried out 3 days per week. Tempo was consistent between conditions. After a 12-month washout period, the same protocol was performed at 30% of 1RM. The study duration was 6 weeks. MRI was used to measure hypertrophy. No differences in CSA were observed between low- and high-intensity exercise. Significantly greater increases in strength were observed favoring high over low load.
summary: Ogasawara et al. (2015) investigated the effects of low- vs. high-intensity bench press training on hypertrophy and strength in untrained men over a 6-week period.

content: Popov et al. (2018) studied 18 untrained young men. The study by Popov et al. (2018) randomly assigned participants to either high-intensity (80% of MVC) or low-intensity (50% of MVC) exercise without relaxation. Exercise consisted of leg press exercises performed 3 days per week. Tempo was consistent between conditions. The study duration was 8 weeks. MRI was used to measure hypertrophy. No differences in CSA or strength were observed between groups.
summary: Popov et al. (2018) examined the impact of high- vs. low-intensity leg press training on hypertrophy and strength in untrained men over 8 weeks.

content: Schoenfeld et al. (2016) studied 18 well-trained young men. The study by Schoenfeld et al. (2016) randomly assigned participants to a resistance training protocol of either 8- to 12RM or 25- to 35RM. All subjects performed 3 sets of 7 exercises. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 8 weeks. Ultrasound was used to measure hypertrophy. No significant differences in thickness of the biceps, triceps, or quadriceps were observed between conditions.
summary: Schoenfeld et al. (2016) investigated the effects of different resistance training rep ranges (8-12RM vs. 25-35RM) on muscle hypertrophy in well-trained men over 8 weeks.

content: Schuenke et al. (670) studied 34 untrained young women. Participants were randomly assigned to moderate intensity (80% to 85% of 1RM) at a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds, low intensity (~40% to 60% of 1RM) at a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds, or slow speed (~40% to 60% of 1RM) at a tempo of 10 seconds concentric and 4 seconds eccentric. Exercise consisted of 3 sets of squats, leg presses, and knee extensions, performed 2 or 3 days per week for 6 weeks. Significant increases in CSA for high-intensity exercise; no significant increase in CSA for low-intensity exercise.
summary: Schuenke et al. (670) examined the influence of different exercise intensities and tempos on muscle hypertrophy in untrained women over 6 weeks.

content: Tanimoto and Ishii (743) studied 24 untrained young men. Participants were randomly assigned to 50% of 1RM with a 6-second tempo and no relaxation phase between reps, 80% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps, or 50% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. Exercise consisted of 3 sets of knee extensions performed 3 days per week for 12 weeks. No differences in CSA or strength between low- and high-intensity exercise.
summary: Tanimoto and Ishii (743) investigated the effects of different training intensities and tempos on muscle hypertrophy and strength in untrained men over 12 weeks.

content: Tanimoto et al. (744) studied 36 untrained young men (12 served as nonexercising controls). Participants were randomly assigned to ~55% of 1RM with a 6-second tempo and no relaxation phase between reps or 80% to 90% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. Exercise consisted of 3 sets of squats, chest presses, lat pulldowns, abdominal bends, and back extensions, performed 2 days per week. No differences in CSA or strength between low- and high-intensity exercise.
summary: Tanimoto et al. (744) examined the impact of different training intensities and tempos on muscle hypertrophy and strength in untrained men over a training period, with a control group.

content: Van Roie et al. (783) studied 56 untrained elderly adults. Participants were randomly assigned to leg press and knee extension training at high load (2 × 10 to 15 reps at 80% of 1RM), low load (1 × 80 to 100 reps at 20% of 1RM), or low-load+ (1 × 60 reps at 20% of 1RM, followed by 1 × 10 to 20 reps at 40% of 1RM).
summary: Van Roie et al. (783) investigated the effects of different training loads on muscle hypertrophy and strength in untrained elderly adults.

content: Participants were randomly assigned to leg press and knee extension training at high load (2 × 10 to 15 reps at 80% of 1RM), low load (1 × 80 to 100 reps at 20% of 1RM), or low-load+ (1 × 60 reps at 20% of 1RM, followed by 1 × 10 to 20 reps at 40% of 1RM).
summary: Participants were assigned to different resistance training protocols with varying loads and rep ranges.

content: Exercise consisted of 3 sets of squats, chest presses, lat pulldowns, abdominal bends, and back extensions, performed 2 days per week.
summary: Participants performed a set of exercises twice a week.

content: No differences in CSA or strength between low- and high-intensity exercise. Greater increases in strength for high- and low- vs. low-load conditions.
summary: There were no differences in muscle size or strength between low and high intensity exercise, but both were superior to the low-load condition.

content: Van Roie et al. (783) studied 56 untrained elderly adults. Weiss et al. (811) studied 44 untrained young men.
summary: Two studies investigated the effects of different training protocols on muscle growth and strength.

content: Participants were randomly assigned to high-load (3- to 5RM), moderate-load (13- to 15RM), or light-load (23 to 25RM) resistance training. Exercise consisted of 3 sets of squats performed 3 days per week.
summary: Participants were assigned to different resistance training loads and performed squats three times a week.

content: No significant differences in muscle thickness between conditions.
summary: There were no significant differences in muscle thickness between the different training loads.

content: The human body is designed to carry out movement in three-dimensional space. Muscle architecture is intricately arranged to accomplish complex movement patterns efficiently and effectively.
summary: The human body is designed for three-dimensional movement, and muscle architecture facilitates this.

content: Varying exercise parameters (i.e., angle of pull, plane of movement, position of extremities) can preferentially target aspects of the musculature, as well as make synergists and stabilizers more or less active. Choice of exercise may contribute to the degree of selective hypertrophy of specific muscles.
summary: Exercise parameters and choice can selectively target muscles and contribute to hypertrophy.

content: Numerous muscles have common origins, but their fibers diverge to insert at different attachment sites. Different heads provide greater leverage for carrying out multi-planar movement.
summary: Muscles often have common origins but diverge to insert at different sites, allowing for greater leverage in multi-planar movement.

content: The anterior deltoid performs shoulder flexion, the middle deltoid performs abduction, and the posterior deltoid performs horizontal abduction. Other examples of muscles with different heads are the pectoralis major (clavicular and sternal heads), biceps brachii (short and long heads), and gastrocnemius (medial and lateral heads.)
summary: Different muscle heads perform specific movements, illustrated by examples like the deltoid and biceps brachii.

content: The direction of the fibers in a given muscle allow for greater or lesser leverage in a given movement. The trapezius is subdivided so that the upper aspect elevates the scapula, the middle aspect abducts the scapula, and the lower aspect depresses the scapula.
summary: Muscle fiber direction influences leverage, and the trapezius has subdivisions for different scapular movements.

content: Evidence suggests that it is possible to target not only different aspects of a muscle but also portions of a given muscle fiber as a result of fiber partitioning. The partitioning hypothesis is based on research showing that the arrangement of individual muscles is more complex than simply a bundle of fibers attaching at aponeuroses, tendons, or bones with a single muscle nerve innervation.
summary: Muscle fiber partitioning allows for targeting specific muscle portions, supported by research on muscle complexity.

content: Many muscles are segmented into distinct compartments, and these compartments are innervated by their own neural branches. Muscles such as the sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus, and rectus femoris are examples of muscles with fiber partitioning.
summary: Many muscles have compartments innervated by separate neural branches, exemplified by the sartorius, gracilis, semitendinosus, and rectus femoris.

content: Architectural variances of individual muscles lend support to the notion of the need to adopt a multiplanar, multiangled approach to hypertrophy training using a variety of exercises. Evidence suggests that frequent exercise rotation is warranted to fully stimulate all fibers within a muscle and thus maximize the hypertrophic response.
summary: Muscle architecture supports the need for varied exercises and frequent rotation to maximize muscle growth.

content: Neural mechanisms are primarily responsible for increases in strength during the early stages of resistance training. Lifters in the initial training phase should focus on acquiring the necessary motor learning and control to effectively carry out exercise performance.
summary: Neural mechanisms drive early strength gains, emphasizing motor learning and control in initial training phases.

content: Simplification and repetition are important in this context. Performing the same movements over and over ingrains motor patterns so that proper technique becomes second nature. For those who have difficulty with coordination, reducing degrees of freedom with machine-based training can be an effective means to enhance neural development. They can then progress to more complex variations in three-dimensional space.
summary: Repetition and simplification are crucial for motor learning, and machine-based training can aid in initial development before progressing to more complex movements.

content: A variety of exercises should be employed over the course of a periodized training program to maximize whole-body muscle hypertrophy. This should include the liberal use of free-form exercises (i.e., free weights and cables) that maximize the contribution of stabilizer muscles, as well as machine-based movements that target specific muscles or portions thereof.
summary: A periodized training program should incorporate a variety of exercises, including free weights and machines, to maximize muscle growth.

content: Both multi- and single-joint exercises should be included in a hypertrophy-specific routine to maximize muscular growth.
summary: Both multi- and single-joint exercises are important for maximizing muscle growth.

content: The biceps brachii, sartorius, and gracilis muscles contain subdivisions of individual fibers that are innervated by separate motor neurons. The sartorius and gracilis muscles are composed of relatively short, in-series fibers that terminate intrafascicularly.
summary: The biceps brachii, sartorius, and gracilis muscles have subdivisions innervated by separate motor neurons, with the sartorius and gracilis having short, in-series fibers.

content: Muscular partitions may have functional or task-oriented roles. Both the long and short heads of the biceps brachii have architectural compartments that are innervated by private branches of the primary neurons.
summary: Muscle partitions likely serve functional roles, as seen in the biceps brachii with its compartments innervated by separate branches.

content: Participants were randomly assigned to ~55% of 1RM with a 6-second tempo and no relaxation phase between reps or 80% to 90% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. Tempo was consistent between conditions.
summary: Participants were assigned to different training intensities and tempos.

content: Both multi- and single-joint exercises should be included in a hypertrophy-specific routine to maximize muscular growth. This should include free-form as well as machine-based exercises. People should use a variety of exercises to maximize whole-body muscle hypertrophy.
summary: A hypertrophy-specific routine should incorporate both multi- and single-joint exercises, including free-form and machine-based options, to maximize muscle growth.

content: Fonseca and colleagues demonstrated the importance of varying exercise selection in a study in which they compared muscular adaptations following performance of the Smith machine squat with a volume-equated combination of the Smith machine squat, leg press, lunge, and deadlift. Results showed that the varied exercise routine produced more uniform muscle hypertrophy of all four quadriceps muscles compared to performing the Smith machine squat alone. Fonseca et al. (223) conducted a study with 49 untrained young men. The study subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol involving either the Smith machine squat or a combination of the Smith machine squat, leg press, lunge, and deadlift. All subjects performed the routine twice per week at 6- to 10RM for each exercise. The study duration was 12 weeks. The volume was equated in the study. Hypertrophy was measured using MRI. Greater hypertrophy of the vastus medialis and rectus femoris muscles was observed in the varied-exercise condition. It is speculated that hypertrophic results would have been enhanced if more targeted single-joint exercises, such as the knee extension, were included in the varied routine.
summary: A study comparing the Smith machine squat to a varied routine (squat, leg press, lunge, deadlift) found that the varied routine produced more uniform quadriceps hypertrophy.

content: Excessive exercise variation may compromise the hypertrophic response. During the initial stages of training, the number of exercises in a program should be limited to allow neural patterns to become ingrained. Trained lifters can be more liberal in varying exercise selection because their neural patterns are more entrenched. Significant transfer of training from exercise variations facilitates the retention of neural patterns over time.
summary: Exercise variation should be carefully considered, limiting it initially to allow neural pattern development and increasing it as training progresses.

content: The biceps brachii, sartorius, and gracilis muscles contain subdivisions of individual fibers that are innervated by separate motor neurons. The sartorius and gracilis muscles are composed of relatively short, in-series fibers that terminate intrafascicularly. Both the long and short heads of the biceps brachii have architectural compartments that are innervated by private branches of the primary neurons. Fibers in the lateral portion of the long head of the biceps brachii are recruited for elbow flexion, fibers in the medial aspect are recruited for supination, and fibers that are centrally located are recruited for non-linear combinations of flexion and supination. The short head of the biceps brachii demonstrates greater activity in the latter part of an arm curl, whereas the long head is more active in the early phase of movement.
summary: The biceps brachii, sartorius, and gracilis muscles exhibit complex fiber organization and recruitment patterns.

content: Muscle hypertrophy occurs in a nonuniform fashion, in terms of preferential growth of both individual muscles in a muscle group and different regions within the same muscle. Knee extension exercises result in a heterogeneous hypertrophic response in which certain areas of the quadriceps femoris show greater hypertrophy than others. Similar nonuniform growth has been demonstrated in the triceps brachii following regimented elbow extension exercises. Regional hypertrophy is specific to the site of muscle activation.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy is not uniform, varying between muscles and regions within a muscle based on activation patterns.

content: Wakahara and colleagues determined muscle activation in a group of subjects performing 5 sets of 8 repetitions of the lying triceps extension exercise. Another group of subjects then underwent a 12-week supervised exercise program employing the same variables used in the acute activation study. Results showed that the extent of hypertrophy in the triceps was specific to the region of activation. Follow-up work by the same lab showed a similar outcome from the close-grip bench press exercise; triceps hypertrophy correlated to the site of activation, but occurred in a different region of the muscle compared to the previous study. Other research shows that regional differences in quadriceps femoris hypertrophy following regimented resistance training are a function of muscle oxygenation status during exercise as opposed to neuromuscular activity.
summary: Studies demonstrate that muscle hypertrophy is localized to the regions of muscle activation during exercise.

content: Evidence suggests that varying exercises enhances muscle activation.  Muscular partitions may have functional or task-oriented roles.
summary: Exercise variation and muscular partitions contribute to enhanced muscle activation and function.

content: The three basic types of muscle actions are concentric, eccentric, and isometric. Eccentric strength is approximately 20% to 50% greater than concentric strength. Forces generated during eccentric training are 45% higher than those generated during concentric training. Forces generated during eccentric training are approximately double that of isometric contractions. The greater mechanical tension per active fiber during eccentric actions is thought to be due to a reversal of the size principle of recruitment.
summary: Eccentric muscle actions generate greater force and tension compared to concentric and isometric actions.

content: The three basic types of muscle actions are concentric, eccentric, and isometric. Forces generated during eccentric training are approximately double that of isometric contractions. Forces generated during eccentric training are 45% higher than those generated during concentric training. Eccentric strength is approximately 20% to 50% greater than concentric strength.
summary: This chunk describes the three basic muscle actions and highlights the greater force production and strength gains associated with eccentric contractions compared to concentric and isometric actions.

content: During eccentric plantar flexion, Type II fibers are selectively recruited at the expense of Type I fibers. Derecruitment of the slow-twitch soleus muscle and an increase in activity of the gastrocnemius muscle occur during the eccentric component of movement. EMG data indicates selective recruitment of a small number of motor units during eccentric hamstring exercise, including additional recruitment of previously inactive motor units. Type I and Type II fibers are equally glycogen depleted following eccentric exercise, suggesting no preferential recruitment of high-threshold motor units (750).
summary: This chunk focuses on the specific muscle fiber recruitment patterns and metabolic responses observed during eccentric muscle actions.

content: The greater mechanical tension per active fiber during eccentric actions is thought to be due to a reversal of the size principle of recruitment. This is believed to be due to greater force demands on fewer active fibers, which are prone to tear when attempting to resist lengthening. Because the weakest sarcomeres are located at different regions of each myofibril, it is hypothesized that the associated nonuniform lengthening causes a shearing of myofibrils. This deforms membranes, particularly T-tubules, leading to a disturbance of calcium homeostasis that further damages muscle tissue by eliciting the release of the calcium-activated neutral proteases involved in degrading Z-line proteins (24, 65).
summary: This chunk explains the proposed mechanisms behind the increased muscle damage and potential for hypertrophy associated with eccentric exercise.

content: Although concentric and isometric exercise can induce muscle damage, the extent of damage is heightened during eccentric actions. Hypertrophic advantages of eccentric exercise are also thought to be related to muscle damage (657).
summary: This chunk highlights the relationship between muscle damage and the potential for hypertrophy, emphasizing the greater damage and potential benefits associated with eccentric exercise.

content: Franchi and colleagues found that eccentric training preferentially upregulated early MAPK activation (p38 MAPK, ERK1/2, p90RSK) compared to concentric training, but neither mode affected Akt/mTOR or inflammatory signaling 30 minutes after exercise (228). Eliasson and colleagues (201) found that maximal eccentric actions (4 sets of 6 repetitions) significantly increased early-phase (2 hours) phosphorylation of p70S6K and the ribosomal protein S6, whereas the same number of maximal concentric actions showed no effect on phosphorylation of these signaling molecules. Consistent with the study by Franchi and colleagues, neither contraction mode produced significant increases in Akt or mTOR, suggesting that eccentric actions activate p70S6K via an Akt-independent pathway. In addition, eccentric exercise was shown to promote significantly greater upregulation of STARS mRNA compared to concentric exercise (10-fold vs. 3-fold, respectively) as well as greater expression of downstream serum response factor (SFR) target genes (798). These findings suggest that eccentric exercise preferentially modulates the transcription of specific myofibrillar genes associated with adaptation to resistance exercise, possibly as a mechanism to protect against contractile-induced muscle damage.
summary: This chunk delves into the molecular signaling pathways activated by eccentric and concentric exercise, highlighting the preferential activation of specific pathways associated with muscle protein synthesis and adaptation in eccentric exercise.

content: Research investigating the effect of contraction modes on muscle protein synthesis has produced disparate results. Several studies have failed to demonstrate any differences in either mixed muscle (246, 575) or myofibrillar (166) muscle protein synthesis after submaximal eccentric or concentric resistance exercise. Conversely, Moore and colleagues (507) reported a more rapid rise in myofibrillar muscle protein synthesis following 6 sets of 10 work-matched maximal eccentric versus concentric knee extension repetitions. The discrepancies between findings suggest that although muscle protein synthesis is similar in all contraction modes during submaximal exercise, maximal eccentric actions enhance the accretion of muscle proteins.
summary: This chunk explores the existing research on the impact of eccentric and concentric exercise on muscle protein synthesis, acknowledging the conflicting findings and suggesting that maximal eccentric actions may be more effective for promoting muscle growth.

content: Concentric and eccentric muscle actions appear to recruit muscle fibers in different orders, result in different signaling responses, and produce distinct morphological adaptations in muscle fibers and fascicles. Therefore, both concentric and eccentric actions should be incorporated during training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the unique characteristics of concentric and eccentric actions and advocates for incorporating both types of contractions into training programs for optimal muscle development.

content: Longitudinal studies provide limited evidence of a hypertrophic advantage from eccentric actions. In a meta-analysis encompassing 73 subjects from three studies meeting inclusion criteria, Roig and colleagues (622) found significantly greater increases in muscle hypertrophy following eccentric versus concentric resistance training protocols. Moreover, the researchers reported that two of the three studies not included in the analysis underscored the superiority of eccentric exercise in maximizing growth. That said, several studies have failed to demonstrate a hypertrophic superiority from eccentric actions (15, 114, 228, 508), and some research actually shows greater growth-related benefits to performing concentric training (464). Contradictions in the data are likely due at least in part to volume-related differences in the studies—that is, higher loads used during eccentric exercise
summary: This chunk presents the current understanding of the potential hypertrophic benefits of eccentric exercise, acknowledging the mixed research findings and suggesting that volume differences may contribute to the discrepancies.

content: Adding supramaximal eccentric actions to a training program may enhance hypertrophic adaptations. A strategy to achieve this is performing an eccentric set or two of a given exercise with a load of approximately 120% to 140% of 1RM, and having a spotter assist in returning the weight concentrically.
summary: This chunk proposes a training strategy to maximize hypertrophy by incorporating supramaximal eccentric actions, which involve lifting a heavier weight eccentrically with assistance for the concentric portion.

content: Eccentric training produced significantly greater increases in fascicle length compared to concentric training (12% vs. 5%, respectively). Concentric actions produced significantly greater increases in pennation angle (+30% vs. +5%). These findings indicate a predisposition toward in-series hypertrophy following eccentric exercise. Fascicle length changes seem to be specific to the initial stages of resistance training; increases abate after 5 weeks of consistent training. Eccentric actions show preferential growth in the distal aspect of the vastus lateralis (8% eccentric vs. 2% concentric). Concentric actions target the midportion of the muscle (7% eccentric vs. 11% concentric). Site-specific hypertrophy might be related to regional muscle damage along the length of the fiber and consequently nonuniform changes in muscle activation. Cadore et al. (2013) studied 22 recreationally trained young men and women.
summary: This chunk details the morphological adaptations observed in muscle fibers and fascicles following eccentric and concentric training, highlighting the preferential in-series hypertrophy and site-specific growth patterns associated with eccentric exercise.

content: Fascicle length changes seem to be specific to the initial stages of resistance training; increases abate after 5 weeks of consistent training.
summary: Fascicle length changes are primarily observed in the early stages of resistance training.

content: Eccentric actions show preferential growth in the distal aspect of the vastus lateralis (8% eccentric vs. 2% concentric). Concentric actions target the midportion of the muscle (7% eccentric vs. 11% concentric). Site-specific hypertrophy might be related to regional muscle damage along the length of the fiber and consequently nonuniform changes in muscle activation.
summary: Eccentric actions promote growth in the distal vastus lateralis, while concentric actions target the midportion, potentially due to site-specific muscle damage.

content: Cadore et al. (2013) studied 22 recreationally trained young men and women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the knee extensors. All subjects performed 2 to 5 sets of 8 to 10 maximal reps. Training was carried out twice weekly for 6 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and ultrasound. No significant differences in muscle thickness between conditions were found.
summary: Cadore et al. (2013) investigated the effects of eccentric vs. concentric training on knee extensor hypertrophy in recreationally trained individuals, finding no significant differences.

content: Farup et al. (2012) studied 22 untrained young men. Subjects performed concentric actions of the knee extensors with one leg and eccentric actions with the other leg. All subjects performed 6 to 12 sets of 6- to 15RM. Eccentric actions were performed at 120% of concentric 1RM. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 12 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was a knee extension machine and MRI. No significant differences in quadriceps hypertrophy between conditions were found.
summary: Farup et al. (2012) compared eccentric and concentric training on quadriceps hypertrophy in untrained men, observing no significant differences.

content: Farup et al. (2011) studied 22 untrained young men. Subjects performed concentric actions of the knee extensors with one leg and eccentric actions with the other leg. All subjects performed 6 to 12 sets of 6- to 15RM. Eccentric actions were performed at 120% of concentric 1RM. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 12 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was a knee extension machine and muscle biopsy. Significantly greater increases in Type II fiber CSA for the concentric condition were found.
summary: Farup et al. (2011) found that concentric training led to greater increases in Type II fiber cross-sectional area compared to eccentric training in untrained men.

content: Farthing and Chilibeck (2009) studied 36 untrained young men and women. Subjects performed concentric actions of the elbow flexors with one arm and eccentric actions with the other arm. Subjects were randomly assigned to perform the actions at either a fast or slow speed. All subjects performed 2 to 6 sets of 8 maximal reps. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 8 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and ultrasound. Greater increase in muscle thickness for the eccentric condition was found.
summary: Farthing and Chilibeck (2009) compared eccentric and concentric elbow flexor training at different speeds, finding greater muscle thickness increases with eccentric training.

content: Franchi et al. (2028) studied 12 untrained young men. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions of the lower-limb extensors. All subjects performed 4 sets of 8- to 10RM. Eccentric actions were performed at 120% of concentric 1RM. Concentric actions were performed for 2 seconds; eccentric actions, for 3 seconds. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 10 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was a leg press machine and MRI. No significant differences in thigh hypertrophy between conditions were found.
summary: Franchi et al. (2028) investigated the effects of eccentric vs. concentric lower-limb extensor training on thigh hypertrophy, finding no significant differences.

content: Higbie et al. (2007) studied 54 untrained young women.
summary: Higbie et al. (2007) conducted a study on untrained young women.

content: Higbie et al. (2007) studied 54 untrained young women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the knee extensors. All subjects performed 3 sets of 10 maximal reps. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 10 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was a leg press machine and MRI. No significant differences in thigh hypertrophy between conditions were found.
summary: This study investigated the effects of eccentric vs. concentric training on knee extensor hypertrophy in untrained women, finding no significant difference.

content: Horto-bagyi et al. (2018) studied 21 untrained young men. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the knee extensors. All subjects performed 4 to 6 sets of 8 to 12 maximal reps. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 12 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and biopsy. Significantly greater increase in Type II fiber hypertrophy of the quadriceps for the eccentric condition was found.
summary: This study compared eccentric and concentric training on knee extensor hypertrophy in men, revealing greater Type II fiber hypertrophy with eccentric training.

content: Horto-bagyi et al. (2019) studied 48 untrained young men and women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the knee extensors. All subjects performed 4 to 6 sets of 8 to 12 maximal reps. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 12 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and biopsy. Significantly greater increase in quadriceps hypertrophy of all fiber types for the eccentric condition was found.
summary: This study compared eccentric and concentric training on knee extensor hypertrophy in a mixed-gender group, finding greater hypertrophy of all fiber types with eccentric training.

content: Jones and Rutherford (2013) studied 12 untrained young men and women. Subjects performed concentric actions of the knee extensors with one leg and eccentric actions with the other leg. All subjects performed 4 sets of 6 maximal reps. Eccentric actions were performed at 145% of concentric 1RM. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 12 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was a variable resistance knee extension machine and CT. No significant differences in thigh hypertrophy between conditions were found.
summary: This study compared unilateral concentric and eccentric knee extension training, finding no significant difference in thigh hypertrophy.

content: Kim et al. (2018) studied 13 young men and women (training status not disclosed). Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the shoulder abductors. All subjects performed 4 to 6 sets of 6 to 8 maximal reps. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 8 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and ultrasound. No significant differences in hypertrophy of the supraspinatus between conditions were found.
summary: This study investigated the effects of eccentric vs. concentric training on shoulder abductor hypertrophy, finding no significant difference.

content: Komi and Buskirk (1975) studied 31 untrained young men. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions of the forearm flexors. Training was carried out 4 days per week for 7 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and circumference measurements. Greater increases in upper-arm girth for the eccentric condition were found.
summary: This study compared eccentric and concentric training on forearm flexor hypertrophy, finding greater upper-arm girth increases with eccentric training.

content: Concentric actions were performed for 2 seconds; eccentric actions, for 3 seconds.
summary: This describes the temporal parameters for concentric and eccentric actions.

content: Komi and Buskirk (1975) studied 31 untrained young men. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions of the forearm flexors. Training was carried out 4 days per week for 7 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and circumference measurements. Greater increases in upper-arm girth for the eccentric condition were found.
summary: This study compared eccentric and concentric forearm flexor training in untrained men, finding greater upper-arm girth increases with eccentric training.

content: Mayhew et al. (2014) studied 20 untrained young men and women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the knee extensors. Concentric actions were performed at an intensity of 90% of maximal concentric power, whereas eccentric actions were performed at the same relative power level. Training was carried out 3 days per week for 4 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement used was an isokinetic dynamometer and biopsy. Greater increases in Type II quadriceps fiber area for the concentric condition were found.
summary: This study compared eccentric and concentric knee extensor training in untrained men and women, finding greater Type II quadriceps fiber area increases with concentric training.

content: Moore et al. (506) conducted a study with 9 untrained young men. The study design was within-subject, with subjects performing concentric elbow flexor actions with one arm and eccentric actions with the other arm. All subjects performed 2 to 6 sets of 10 maximal reps twice per week. The study duration was 9 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement method was isokinetic dynamometer. There were no significant differences in elbow flexor hypertrophy between conditions.
summary: This within-subject study compared concentric and eccentric elbow flexor training in untrained men, finding no significant differences in hypertrophy.

content: Nickols-Richardson et al. (528) conducted a study with 70 untrained young women. The study design was random assignment to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the limbs. All subjects performed 5 sets of 6 maximal reps 3 days per week. The study duration was 5 months. The hypertrophy measurement method was isokinetic dynamometer and DXA. There were no significant differences in fat-free soft tissue mass between conditions.
summary: This study compared eccentric and concentric limb training in untrained women, finding no significant differences in fat-free soft tissue mass.

content: Reeves et al. (606) conducted a study with 19 untrained elderly men and women. The study design was random assignment to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or mixed (eccentric and concentric) actions for the lower body. The mixed condition was performed for 2 sets of 10 reps with a load of ~80% of the mixed-action 5RM. The eccentric-only condition was performed for 2 sets of 10 reps with a load of ~80% of the eccentric 5RM. Training was carried out 3 days per week. The study duration was 14 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement method was knee extension and leg press machines and ultrasound. There were no significant differences in vastus lateralis thickness between conditions.
summary: This study compared eccentric and mixed (eccentric and concentric) lower body training in untrained elderly individuals, finding no significant differences in vastus lateralis thickness.

content: Seger et al. (677) conducted a study with 10 untrained young men. The study design was within-subject, with subjects performing concentric knee extensor actions with one leg and eccentric actions with the other leg. All subjects performed 4 sets of 10 maximal reps 3 days per week. The study duration was 10 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement method was isokinetic dynamometer and MRI. There were greater increases in whole quadriceps muscle hypertrophy distally for the eccentric condition.
summary: This within-subject study compared concentric and eccentric knee extensor training in untrained men, finding greater distal quadriceps hypertrophy with eccentric training.

content: Smith and Rutherford (705) conducted a study with 10 untrained young men and women. The study design was within-subject, with subjects performing concentric knee extensor actions with one leg and eccentric actions with the other leg. All subjects performed 4 sets of 10 maximal reps.
summary: This within-subject study compared concentric and eccentric knee extensor training in untrained men and women.

content: Smith and Rutherford (705) conducted a study with 10 untrained young men and women. The study design was within-subject, with subjects performing concentric knee extensor actions with one leg and eccentric actions with the other leg. All subjects performed 4 sets of 10 maximal reps. Eccentric actions were performed at 135% of concentric 1RM. Training was carried out 3 days per week. The study duration was 20 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement method was knee extension machine and CT. There were no significant differences in quadriceps hypertrophy between groups.
summary: A study by Smith and Rutherford investigated the effects of concentric and eccentric knee extensions on quadriceps hypertrophy in untrained individuals.

content: Vikne et al. (793) conducted a study with 17 resistance-trained young men. The study design was random assignment to a resistance training protocol of either eccentric or concentric actions for the elbow flexors. Training was divided between maximum and medium days. Those in the maximum training group performed 3 to 5 sets of 4- to 8RM; those in the medium training group performed 3 or 4 sets of the same repetition scheme but with lighter loads. Concentric actions were performed explosively, whereas eccentric actions were performed in 3 to 4 seconds. Training was carried out 2 or 3 days per week. The study duration was 12 weeks. The hypertrophy measurement method was a specially designed cable pulley apparatus, CT scan, and biopsy. There were significantly greater increases in whole muscle CSA of the upper arm for the eccentric condition. There were greater increases in Type I and Type II fiber area for the eccentric condition.
summary: Vikne et al. compared the effects of eccentric and concentric elbow flexor training on muscle hypertrophy in resistance-trained men.

content: Moderate rest periods are believed to provide an ideal compromise between metabolic stress and mechanical tension. A hypertrophy-type workout in which people rested 90 seconds between sets showed significantly greater increases in blood lactate concentration and reductions in pH compared to a strength-type workout with 5 minutes of rest between sets (527). Medeiros and colleagues (485) found that using 60-second rest intervals required a reduction of 5% to 10% in each successive set to allow for the maintenance of 8- to 12RM loads in resistance-trained subjects. A rest interval of 60 to 90 seconds is generally prescribed for maximizing hypertrophy because moderate rest intervals induce a favorable metabolic environment without substantially compromising mechanical forces.
summary: The optimal rest interval for hypertrophy training is generally considered to be 60-90 seconds, balancing metabolic stress and mechanical tension.

content: Only a handful of studies have directly investigated the effect of rest intervals on muscle growth over time. In a crossover design, Ahtiainen and colleagues (20) assessed the hypertrophic impact in well-trained men of taking 2-minute versus 5-minute rest intervals while performing volume-matched work bouts of lower-body resistance exercise. Training was carried out over two separate 3-month periods, in which subjects rested 2 minutes in one of the periods and 5 minutes in the other. No significant differences in muscle cross-sectional areas were seen between conditions in the study by Ahtiainen and colleagues (20). The study by Ahtiainen and colleagues (20) had several strengths including a randomized crossover design, the inclusion of trained subjects, and the use of magnetic resonance imaging to measure muscle growth. The primary issue with the study by Ahtiainen and colleagues (20) is that the 2-minute rest period is longer than what is generally advised for hypertrophy-type training. The impact on metabolic stress diminishes with longer rest periods, and in fact, blood lactate levels were not significantly different between the groups in the study by Ahtiainen and colleagues (20), which may have compromised anabolic signaling.
summary: Ahtiainen et al. investigated the impact of 2-minute versus 5-minute rest intervals on muscle growth in trained men, finding no significant differences.

content: Buresh and colleagues (108) carried out a study in which 12 untrained people performed their workout with either 1 or 2.5 minutes of rest between sets. Longer interset rest intervals produced greater muscle hypertrophy in the study by Buresh and colleagues (108). Resistance training protocols should generally provide rest periods of at least 2 minutes to maximize the hypertrophic response. Consistently training with shorter rest intervals has been shown to promote adaptations that ultimately facilitate the ability to sustain a significantly higher mean percentage of 1RM during training (384). These adaptations include increased capillary and mitochondrial density as well as an improved capacity to buffer hydrogen ions and shuttle them out of muscle, thereby minimizing performance decrements. High-repetition sets may benefit from short rest periods given the reduced need to exert maximal force and the greater potential for realizing the adaptations associated with improved buffering. It therefore seems prudent to include training cycles that limit rest intervals to 60 to 90 seconds to take advantage of any additive effects of metabolic stress if they indeed exist. A study investigated the impact of short versus long rest intervals on hypertrophy. Subjects were randomized to perform multiple sets of 7 exercises for the major muscle groups of the upper and lower body with either 1 or 3 minutes of rest between sets.
