summary: Research suggests that longer rest intervals (2+ minutes) are generally optimal for maximizing muscle hypertrophy, although shorter intervals (60-90 seconds) may be beneficial for high-repetition sets.

content: The hypertrophy measurement method was isokinetic dynamometer and MRI.
summary: The study used isokinetic dynamometry and MRI to measure muscle hypertrophy.

content: A study investigated the impact of short versus long rest intervals on hypertrophy. Subjects were randomized to perform multiple sets of 7 exercises for the major muscle groups of the upper and lower body with either 1 or 3 minutes of rest between sets. After 8 weeks, the longer rest condition produced greater increases in the anterior thigh muscles, and a strong trend for greater increases was noted in the triceps brachii. Beneficial effects of longer rest periods on hypertrophy were attributed to the ability to maintain a higher volume load over the course of the study.
summary: This study compared short (1 minute) and long (3 minute) rest intervals for hypertrophy, finding greater gains with longer rests due to higher volume.

content: In a novel research design, DeSouza and colleagues randomized 20 resistance-trained men to either a group that used a constant rest interval or a group that used descending rest intervals. All of the men began by performing 3 sets of 10 to 12 repetitions with 2 minutes of rest for the first 2 weeks. Thereafter, the length of the rest interval progressively decreased to 30 seconds in the descending rest interval group over an ensuing 6-week period, whereas that of the constant rest interval group remained the same. After 8 weeks, both groups had significantly increased hypertrophy of the upper and lower extremities; no significant differences were noted in rest interval conditions despite a reduction in training volume for the descending group. A follow-up study using essentially the same protocol but with subjects receiving creatine supplementation again found no significant hypertrophic differences between constant and descending rest intervals.
summary: This research explored the effects of constant versus descending rest intervals on hypertrophy, finding no significant difference between the two approaches.

content: Ahtiainen et al. (20) studied 13 resistance-trained young men. The study design was a within-subject crossover design. Subjects performed a resistance training protocol with either 2 or 5 minutes of rest between sets. Training consisted of a multiset split-body routine of 8 to 12 reps carried out 4 days per week. The study duration was 12 weeks. Volume was equated. MRI was used to measure hypertrophy. No significant differences in muscle CSA between groups were seen.
summary: This study compared 2 and 5 minute rest intervals for hypertrophy in resistance-trained men, finding no significant difference.

content: Buresh et al. (108) studied 12 untrained young men. The study design was a randomized assignment to a resistance training protocol with either 1 or 2.5 minutes of rest between sets. Training consisted of a multiset split-body routine of 8 to 11 reps carried out 4 days per week. The study duration was 10 weeks. Volume was equated. Hydrostatic weighing and circumference measurements were used to measure hypertrophy. Significantly greater increases in arm CSA and a trend for greater increases in thigh CSA for the longer rest interval condition were observed.
summary: This study compared 1 and 2.5 minute rest intervals for hypertrophy in untrained men, finding greater gains with the longer rest interval.

content: Schoenfeld et al. (651) studied 21 resistance-trained young men. The study design was a randomized assignment to a resistance training protocol with either 1 or 3 minutes of rest between sets. Training consisted of 7 exercises for the total body of 8 to 12 reps carried out 3 days per week.
summary: This study compared 1 and 3 minute rest intervals for hypertrophy in resistance-trained men.

content: These adaptations include increased capillary and mitochondrial density as well as an improved capacity to buffer hydrogen ions and shuttle them out of muscle, thereby minimizing performance decrements. High-repetition sets may benefit from short rest periods given the reduced need to exert maximal force and the greater potential for realizing the adaptations associated with improved buffering. It therefore seems prudent to include training cycles that limit rest intervals to 60 to 90 seconds to take advantage of any additive effects of metabolic stress if they indeed exist.
summary: This passage discusses the benefits of short rest periods for high-repetition sets, promoting buffering adaptations and metabolic stress.

content: Repetition duration represents the sum of the concentric, eccentric, and isometric components of a repetition, and is predicated on the tempo at which the repetition is performed. Tempo is often expressed as a three-digit arrangement in which the first number is the time (in seconds) to complete the concentric action, the second number is the isometric transition phase between concentric and eccentric actions, and the third number is the time to complete the eccentric action. For example, a tempo of 2-0-3 would indicate a repetition taking 2 seconds on the concentric action, not pausing at the top of the movement, and then taking 3 seconds to perform the eccentric action. In the preceding example, the repetition duration would be 5 seconds. To a certain degree, tempo can be volitionally manipulated. The extent depends on two factors: the intensity of load and the accumulated fatigue. Heavier loads take longer to lift; the closer the load is to the person’s 1RM, the slower the concentric action will be, even when the intent is to move the weight as quickly as possible. Moreover, the onset of fatigue will also influence tempo.
summary: This passage explains the concept of repetition duration and tempo in weightlifting, highlighting the influence of load and fatigue.

content: Schoenfeld et al. (651) studied 21 resistance-trained young men. The study design was a randomized assignment to a resistance training protocol with either 1 or 3 minutes of rest between sets. Training consisted of 7 exercises for the total body of 8 to 12 reps carried out 3 days per week. The study duration was 8 weeks. Volume was equated. Ultrasound was used to measure hypertrophy. Significantly greater increases in anterior thigh muscle thickness and a trend for greater increases in the triceps brachii thickness for the longer rest interval condition were observed.
summary: This study investigated the effects of rest interval duration (1 vs. 3 minutes) on hypertrophy in resistance-trained men, finding greater muscle thickness increases with longer rest periods.

content: Villanueva et al. (794) studied 22 untrained elderly men. The study design was a randomized assignment to a resistance training protocol with either 1 or 4 minutes of rest between sets. Training consisted of 2 or 3 sets of 4 to 6 reps carried out 3 days per week. The study duration was 8 weeks. Volume was equated. DXA was used to measure hypertrophy. Significantly greater increases in lean body mass for the shorter rest interval condition were observed.
summary: This study examined the impact of rest interval duration (1 vs. 4 minutes) on hypertrophy in untrained elderly men, revealing greater lean mass gains with shorter rest periods.

content: 80 subjects participated in a study on muscle hypertrophy. Repetition duration was stratified into four groups: fast/heavy (sets of 6 to 12 repetitions with a total repetition duration of 0.5 to 4 seconds), fast/light (sets of 20 to 30 with a total repetition duration of 0.5 to 4 seconds), medium (sets of 6 to 12 with a total repetition duration of 4 to 8 seconds), or light (sets of 6 to 12 with a total repetition duration of >8 seconds). A meta-analysis of the study results showed no significant differences in muscle hypertrophy in the training durations evaluated. When considering only studies that employed traditional dynamic constant external resistance (i.e., isotonic) training, it can be inferred that there are no discernable differences in hypertrophy using durations up to 6 seconds. Subanalysis of the data indicated that super-slow training is likely detrimental to maximizing hypertrophy.
summary: This meta-analysis investigated the influence of repetition duration on muscle hypertrophy, finding no significant differences for durations up to 6 seconds and suggesting that super-slow training may be less effective.

content: Keogh and colleagues (359) assessed muscle activation in a group of trained lifters during the bench press under a variety of training conditions, including a very slow tempo and a traditional tempo. Those in the slow lifting condition used a repetition duration of 10 seconds (5 seconds for both concentric and eccentric actions), whereas those in the traditional training condition attempted to lift the load as fast as possible. Each condition was carried out to the point of concentric muscular failure. In comparison to the slow tempo, mean EMG activity of the pectoralis major during traditional lifting was markedly higher on the concentric portion of the movement (by ~18%, 19%, and 12% for the first, middle, and last repetition, respectively). During eccentric actions, the activation advantage for training at a traditional versus a slow tempo increased to 32%, 36%, and 36% in the first, middle, and last repetition, respectively.
summary: This study compared muscle activation during bench press using slow (10 seconds) and traditional tempos, finding significantly higher activation for the traditional tempo, particularly during the eccentric phase.

content: In the only study to date that directly evaluated muscle hypertrophy subsequent to superslow versus traditional training, Schuenke and colleagues (670) randomized untrained young females to perform multiple sets of the squat, leg press, and knee extension 2 or 3 days a week for 6 weeks. The superslow group carried out repetitions using a 14-second duration (10 seconds concentric, 4 seconds eccentric); the traditional training group used a traditional tempo. Current evidence suggests that little difference exists in muscle hypertrophy when training at isotonic repetition durations from 0.5 to 6 seconds. Training at very slow volitional durations (>10 seconds per repetition) appears to produce inferior increases in muscle growth. Concentric and eccentric tempos of 1 to 3 seconds can be considered viable options. It is conceivable that combining different repetition durations could enhance the hypertrophic response to resistance training, although this hypothesis requires further study.
summary: This study compared muscle hypertrophy from superslow (14-second) vs. traditional training, finding greater gains with traditional training.  Current evidence suggests optimal repetition durations for hypertrophy are between 0.5 and 6 seconds, with very slow tempos being less effective.

content: One group employed a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds on both concentric and eccentric actions. Both groups performed 6- to 10RM per set. The loading when training in superslow fashion was ~40% to 60% of 1RM. The loading when training in a traditional tempo was ~80% to 85% of 1RM. Poststudy increases in Type IIa and Type IIx fibers were substantially greater using a traditional tempo (~33% and 37%, respectively) versus superslow training (~12% and 19%, respectively). There was a distinctly greater decrease in total Type IIx fiber area in the traditional group compared to the superslow group (~39% vs. 28%, respectively). There was a correspondingly greater increase in total Type IIa fiber area (~30% vs. 11%, respectively). Satellite cell content was significantly greater after traditional compared to superslow training across fiber types.
summary: This study compared muscle fiber adaptations between traditional and superslow training, finding greater increases in Type IIa and IIx fibers, and satellite cell content with traditional training.

content: Hydrostatic weighing and circumference measurements were used to measure hypertrophy.
summary: Hypertrophy was measured using hydrostatic weighing and circumference measurements.

content: The loading when training in a traditional tempo was ~80% to 85% of 1RM. Poststudy increases in Type IIa and Type IIx fibers were substantially greater using a traditional tempo (~33% and 37%, respectively) versus superslow training (~12% and 19%, respectively). There was a distinctly greater decrease in total Type IIx fiber area in the traditional group compared to the superslow group (~39% vs. 28%, respectively). There was a correspondingly greater increase in total Type IIa fiber area (~30% vs. 11%, respectively). Satellite cell content was significantly greater after traditional compared to superslow training across fiber types.
summary: Traditional tempo training (80-85% 1RM) resulted in greater muscle fiber hypertrophy and satellite cell activation compared to superslow training.

content: Some investigators have postulated that intentionally slowing concentric velocity reduces the momentum during a repetition, thereby heightening the tension on a muscle. It has been shown that the effects of momentum are inconsequential in a concentric movement of 2 seconds versus 10 seconds when the load is kept constant.
summary: Slowing concentric velocity may increase muscle tension, but the effect of momentum is minimal when comparing 2-second and 10-second concentric movements with constant load.

content: Performing the concentric phase of a repetition at 2 seconds resulted in a greater lactate accumulation compared to an explosive concentric contraction despite an equated volume and lower power in the slower cadence. Nogueira and colleagues found that performing concentric actions explosively with a 1-second concentric repetition produced greater increases in muscle thickness compared to performing the repetitions at 2 to 3 seconds. The study by Nogueira and colleagues used light loads (40% to 60% of 1RM) and sets were terminated well short of muscular failure.
summary: Slower concentric contractions (2 seconds) increase lactate accumulation but may not be optimal for muscle hypertrophy compared to explosive contractions (1 second).

content: Roschel and colleagues found similar activation of Akt, mTOR, and p70S6K following 5 sets of 8 eccentric repetitions at a slow (20° per second) versus fast (210° per second) velocity. Shepstone and colleagues reported a trend for greater increases in muscle cross-sectional area of the elbow flexors with faster eccentric repetitions (210° per second vs. 20° per second). Farthing and Chilibeck demonstrated that fast (180° per second) eccentric actions produced greater increases in muscle thickness as compared to both slow (30° per second) and fast concentric actions, but not slow eccentric actions.
summary: Eccentric training velocity may not significantly impact signaling pathways (Akt, mTOR, p70S6K) but faster eccentric repetitions may promote greater muscle hypertrophy.

content: All of the studies that showed a benefit to faster eccentric actions used isokinetic dynamometry.
summary: Studies demonstrating benefits of faster eccentric repetitions for muscle hypertrophy utilized isokinetic dynamometry.

content: Tanimoto and Ishii found that untrained young men performing 12 weeks of knee extensions using a 3-0-3-0 cadence (no rest between eccentric and concentric repetitions) experienced a similar hypertrophic response as men using a 1-1-1-0 cadence (relaxing for 1 second between each phase).
summary: Untrained men experienced similar muscle hypertrophy with both 3-0-3-0 and 1-1-1-0 cadences during knee extensions.

content: Despite widespread belief that exercise order should proceed from large- to small-muscle groups, the benefit has not been demonstrated in controlled research studies. These recommendations are based on the premise that the performance of multi-joint exercises is impaired when the smaller secondary synergists are prefatigued by prior single-joint exercises. For example, performance of the arm curl would fatigue the biceps brachii, thereby impeding the ability to overload the larger latissimus dorsi muscle during subsequent performance of the lat pulldown.
summary: While commonly believed, research does not support the benefit of exercising from large to small muscle groups. This practice is based on the assumption that pre-fatiguing smaller muscles hinders performance of larger, multi-joint exercises.

content: Despite wide acceptance that exercise order should proceed from large- to small-muscle groups, research is equivocal on the topic. Acute studies show that performance, as determined by the number of repetitions performed, is compromised in exercises performed toward the end of a session regardless of the size of the muscle trained (692). However, given the heavier loads used during multijoint movements, the absolute magnitude of the decreases are generally greater in these exercises when they are performed after those involving small-muscle groups. Thus, volume load tends to be better preserved when large-muscle exercises are placed early in the training bout.
summary: Research on exercise order's impact on performance is inconclusive. While performance declines towards the end of a workout regardless of muscle group, heavier loads used in multi-joint exercises may result in greater volume loss when performed later.

content: Evidence indicates a hypertrophic benefit for muscles worked first in a resistance training bout. Therefore, exercise order should be prioritized so that lagging muscles are trained earlier on in the session. In this way the person expends the greatest energy and focus on the sets of most importance. Whether the muscle group is large or small is of secondary concern.
summary: Prioritizing lagging muscle groups earlier in a workout may enhance hypertrophy. The order should focus on maximizing effort and focus on key muscle groups.

content: Several studies have attempted to directly quantify the effects of exercise order on muscle hypertrophy. Simao and colleagues (691) investigated the performance of upper-body exercises when progressing from large to small muscle groups compared to small to large muscle groups in untrained men. Exercises included the bench press, lat pulldown, triceps extension, and arm curl. Training was carried out twice per week for 12 weeks. Muscle thickness of the triceps brachii increased only in the group that performed small-muscle-group exercises first, although differences in the thickness of the biceps were similar on an absolute basis.
summary: Studies examining exercise order's impact on hypertrophy suggest that prioritizing small muscle groups first may be beneficial for specific muscles like the triceps brachii.

content: The same lab replicated this basic study design and similarly found greater increases in triceps thickness when the Claflin et al. (141) conducted a study with 63 untrained young and old men and women. Subjects in Claflin et al. (141) were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol at either a high velocity (hip 250° to 350° per second, knee 100 to 160° per second) or low velocity (hip 30° to 90° per second, knee 20° to 40° per second). All subjects in Claflin et al. (141) performed 2 sets of 10 reps with a 3rd set that induced failure using between 5 and 15 reps. Training in Claflin et al. (141) was carried out 3 days per week. Claflin et al. (141) investigated repetition durations of 0.5 to 0.66 seconds, 1 to 2 seconds, 2 to 6 seconds, and 4 to 8 seconds. The study duration in Claflin et al. (141) was 14 weeks.
summary: Claflin et al. investigated the effects of training velocity (high vs. low) and repetition duration (0.5-8 seconds) on muscle hypertrophy in untrained individuals over 14 weeks.

content: Subjects in Claflin et al. (141) were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol at either a high velocity (hip 250° to 350° per second, knee 100 to 160° per second) or low velocity (hip 30° to 90° per second, knee 20° to 40° per second). All subjects in Claflin et al. (141) performed 2 sets of 10 reps with a 3rd set that induced failure using between 5 and 15 reps. Training in Claflin et al. (141) was carried out 3 days per week. Claflin et al. (141) investigated repetition durations of 0.5 to 0.66 seconds, 1 to 2 seconds, 2 to 6 seconds, and 4 to 8 seconds. The study duration in Claflin et al. (141) was 14 weeks. Claflin et al. (141) used a biopsy to measure hypertrophy. Claflin et al. (141) found no effect of training on Type I fibers; however, there was an 8.2% increase in Type II fibers irrespective of tempo.
summary: Claflin et al. (141) investigated the effects of different repetition tempos on muscle hypertrophy in a 14-week resistance training study.

content: Subjects in Keeler et al. (356) were randomly assigned to either superslow or traditional Nautilus resistance training protocols. Subjects in Keeler et al. (356) performed 1 set of 8- to 12RM for 8 exercises targeting the entire body. Training in Keeler et al. (356) was carried out 3 days per week. Keeler et al. (356) investigated repetition durations of 6 seconds and 15 seconds. The study duration in Keeler et al. (356) was 10 weeks. Keeler et al. (356) used a BodPod to measure body composition. Keeler et al. (356) found no significant differences in body composition.
summary: Keeler et al. (356) compared the effects of superslow and traditional resistance training on body composition in a 10-week study.

content: Subjects in Munn et al. (513) were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of 1 or 3 sets of elbow flexion exercise in either a slow or fast fashion. Training in Munn et al. (513) was at 6- to 8RM for 3 days per week. Munn et al. (513) investigated repetition durations of 2 seconds and 6 seconds. The study duration in Munn et al. (513) was 6 weeks. Munn et al. (513) used skinfold and circumference measurements to measure hypertrophy. Munn et al. (513) found no significant differences in lean mass between conditions.
summary: Munn et al. (513) examined the effects of set number and repetition tempo on muscle hypertrophy in a 6-week resistance training study.

content: Subjects in Neils et al. (522) were randomly assigned to a protocol of either superslow at 50% of 1RM or traditional resistance training at 80% of 1RM. All subjects in Neils et al. (522) performed 1 set of 6- to 8RM for 7 exercises targeting the entire body. Training in Neils et al. (522) was carried out 3 days per week. Neils et al. (522) investigated repetition durations of 6 seconds and 15 seconds. The study duration in Neils et al. (522) was 8 weeks. Neils et al. (522) used DXA to measure body composition. Neils et al. (522) found no significant differences in body composition between conditions.
summary: Neils et al. (522) compared the effects of superslow and traditional resistance training on body composition in an 8-week study.

content: Subjects in Nogueira et al. (534) were randomly assigned to an equal work output resistance training protocol in which concentric actions were performed either as fast as possible or at a cadence of 2 to 3 seconds. All subjects in Nogueira et al. (534) performed 3 sets of 8 reps of 7 exercises targeting the entire body. Loads in Nogueira et al. (534) were 40% to 60% of 1RM, and eccentric tempo was 2 to 3 seconds for both conditions. Training in Nogueira et al. (534) was carried out twice weekly. Nogueira et al. (534) investigated repetition durations of 3 to 4 seconds and 4 to 6 seconds. The study duration in Nogueira et al. (534) was 10 weeks. Nogueira et al. (534) used ultrasound to measure hypertrophy. Nogueira et al. (534) found significantly greater increases in thickness of the biceps brachii for the fast condition.
summary: Nogueira et al. (534) investigated the effects of concentric repetition tempo on muscle hypertrophy in a 10-week resistance training study.

content: Subjects in Rana et al. (601) conducted a study with 34 untrained young women.
summary: Rana et al. (601) conducted a study with 34 untrained young women.

content: Nogueira et al. (534) investigated repetition durations of 3 to 4 seconds and 4 to 6 seconds. The study duration in Nogueira et al. (534) was 10 weeks. Nogueira et al. (534) used ultrasound to measure hypertrophy. Nogueira et al. (534) found significantly greater increases in thickness of the biceps brachii for the fast condition.
summary: Nogueira et al. (534) investigated the effects of different repetition durations (3-4 seconds vs. 4-6 seconds) on biceps brachii hypertrophy over a 10-week period, using ultrasound to measure changes.

content: Rana et al. (601) conducted a study with 34 untrained young women. Subjects in Rana et al. (601) were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of moderate intensity (80% to 85% of 1RM) at a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds, low intensity (~40% to 60% of 1RM) at a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds, or slow speed (~40% to 60% of 1RM) at a tempo of 10 seconds concentric and 4 seconds eccentric. All subjects in Rana et al. (601) performed 3 sets of 6- to 10RM of 3 lower-body exercises. Training in Rana et al. (601) was carried out 2 or 3 days per week. Rana et al. (601) investigated repetition durations of 2 to 4 seconds and 14 seconds. The study duration in Rana et al. (601) was 6 weeks. Rana et al. (601) used a BodPod to measure FFM. Rana et al. (601) found no significant differences in FFM between conditions.
summary: Rana et al. (601) examined the effects of different repetition tempos (1-2 seconds, 10 seconds concentric/4 seconds eccentric) on FFM in untrained women over a 6-week period.

content: Schuenke et al. (670) conducted a study with 34 untrained young women. Subjects in Schuenke et al. (670) were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of moderate intensity (80% to 85% of 1RM) at a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds, low intensity (~40% to 60% of 1RM) at a tempo of 1 to 2 seconds, or slow speed (~40% to 60% of 1RM) at a tempo of 10 seconds concentric and 4 seconds eccentric. All subjects in Schuenke et al. (670) performed 3 sets of 6- to 10RM of 3 lower-body exercises. Training in Schuenke et al. (670) was carried out 2 or 3 days per week. Schuenke et al. (670) investigated repetition durations of 2 to 4 seconds and 14 seconds. The study duration in Schuenke et al. (670) was 6 weeks. Schuenke et al. (670) used a biopsy to measure CSA. Schuenke et al. (670) found significantly greater increases in CSA for the faster condition.
summary: Schuenke et al. (670) compared the effects of different repetition tempos (1-2 seconds, 10 seconds concentric/4 seconds eccentric) on muscle CSA in untrained women over a 6-week period, using biopsies to measure changes.

content: Tanimoto and Ishii (743) conducted a study with 24 untrained young men. Subjects in Tanimoto and Ishii (743) were randomly assigned to 50% of 1RM with a 6-second tempo and no relaxing phase between reps, ~80% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps, or ~50% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. All subjects in Tanimoto and Ishii (743) performed 3 sets at 8RM of knee extension exercises. Training in Tanimoto and Ishii (743) was carried out 3 days per week. Tanimoto and Ishii (743) investigated repetition durations of 2 seconds, 6 seconds. The study duration in Tanimoto and Ishii (743) was 12 weeks. Tanimoto and Ishii (743) used MRI to measure muscle CSA. Tanimoto and Ishii (743) found no significant differences in muscle CSA between conditions.
summary: Tanimoto and Ishii (743) examined the effects of different repetition tempos (2 seconds, 6 seconds) and intensities (50% and 80% of 1RM) on muscle CSA in untrained men over a 12-week period, using MRI to measure changes.

content: Tanimoto et al. (744) conducted a study with 36 untrained young men. Subjects in Tanimoto et al. (744) were randomly assigned to ~55% to 60% of 1RM with a 6-second tempo and no relaxing phase between reps or ~80% to 90% of 1RM with a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. All subjects in Tanimoto et al. (744) performed 3 sets at 8RM of 5 exercises targeting the entire body. Training in Tanimoto et al. (744) was carried out twice weekly. Tanimoto et al. (744) investigated repetition durations of 2 seconds and 6 seconds. The study duration in Tanimoto et al. (744) was 13 weeks. Tanimoto et al. (744) used ultrasound to measure muscle thickness. Tanimoto et al. (744) found no significant differences in muscle thickness between conditions.
summary: Tanimoto et al. (744) compared the effects of different repetition tempos (2 seconds, 6 seconds) and intensities (~55-60% vs. ~80-90% of 1RM) on muscle thickness in untrained men over a 13-week period, using ultrasound to measure changes.

content: Watanabe et al. (807) conducted a study with 40 untrained elderly men and women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either a 6-second tempo and no relaxing phase between reps or a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. All subjects performed 3 sets of 8 reps at 50% of 1RM of knee extension and knee flexion exercises. Training was carried out twice weekly.
summary: Watanabe et al. (807) investigated the effects of different repetition tempos (6 seconds vs. 2 seconds) on untrained elderly individuals over a period of time, focusing on knee extension and flexion exercises.

content: Watanabe et al. (807) conducted a study with 40 untrained elderly men and women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either a 6-second tempo and no relaxing phase between reps or a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. All subjects performed 3 sets of 8 reps at 50% of 1RM of knee extension and knee flexion exercises. Training was carried out twice weekly. Ultrasound measurements revealed significantly greater quadriceps thickness for the slow condition (6-second tempo).
summary: A study on elderly individuals found that a slower tempo resistance training protocol led to greater quadriceps thickness compared to a faster tempo.

content: Watanabe et al. (808) conducted a study with 18 untrained elderly men and women. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either a 6-second tempo and no relaxing phase between reps or a 2-second tempo and 1 second of relaxation between reps. All subjects performed 3 sets of 13 reps at 30% of 1RM of knee extension exercises. Training was carried out twice weekly. MRI measurements showed significantly greater increases in quadriceps hypertrophy for the slow condition (6-second tempo) over 12 weeks.
summary: Another study on elderly individuals showed that a slower tempo resistance training protocol resulted in greater quadriceps hypertrophy over 12 weeks.

content: Young and Bilby (852) conducted a study with 18 untrained young men. Subjects were randomly assigned to a resistance training protocol of either fast concentric contractions or slow controlled movements. All subjects performed 4 sets at 8- to 12RM of the barbell half-squat exercise. Training was carried out 3 days per week. Ultrasound measurements showed no significant differences in muscle thickness between conditions (fast vs. slow).
summary: A study comparing fast and slow resistance training in young men found no difference in muscle thickness.

content: Fisher et al. (2018) studied 39 resistance-trained young men and women. Fisher et al. (2018) randomly assigned subjects to a resistance training protocol with exercises performed either from compound to single joint or rotating between a single joint exercise followed by a compound exercise. All subjects in Fisher et al. (2018) performed a single set at a moderate intensity of load to muscular failure. Training in Fisher et al. (2018) was carried out 2 days per week for 12 weeks. Fisher et al. (2018) measured hypertrophy using BodPod.
summary: A study investigated the effect of exercise order (compound to single joint vs. alternating) on hypertrophy in resistance-trained individuals.

content: Simao et al. (2018) studied 31 recreationally trained young men. Simao et al. (2018) randomly assigned subjects to a resistance training protocol where exercise order either began with large- and progressed to small-muscle-group exercises or began with small- and progressed to large-muscle-group exercises. The protocol in Simao et al. (2018) consisted of 2 to 4 sets of 4 upper-body exercises: 2 compound movements and 2 single-joint movements carried out twice per week. Intensity of load in Simao et al. (2018) was periodized from light to heavy each month over the course of the study, descending from 12- to 15RM to 3- to 5RM. Simao et al. (2018) measured hypertrophy using ultrasound.
summary: This study examined the impact of exercise order (large to small vs. small to large muscle groups) on hypertrophy in recreationally trained men.

content: Spineti et al. (2018) studied 30 recreationally trained young men. Spineti et al. (2018) randomly assigned subjects to a resistance training protocol where exercise order either began with large- and progressed to small-muscle-group exercises or began with small- and progressed to large-muscle-group exercises. The protocol in Spineti et al. (2018) consisted of 2 to 4 sets of 4 upper-body exercises: 2 compound movements and 2 single-joint movements carried out twice per week. Intensity of load in Spineti et al. (2018) was carried out in an undulating periodized fashion alternating between light (12- to 15RM), moderate (8- to 10RM), and heavy (3- to 5RM). Spineti et al. (2018) measured hypertrophy using ultrasound.
summary: This study investigated the effect of exercise order (large to small vs. small to large muscle groups) on hypertrophy in recreationally trained men, using an undulating periodization scheme.

content: The vastus medialis is maximally activated during the final 60° of ROM. Similar findings have been reported during the arm curl: the short head appears to be more active in the latter phase of the movement (i.e., greater elbow flexion), whereas the long head is more active in the early phase. The body of literature shows a clear hypertrophic benefit to training through a full ROM.
summary: Training through a full range of motion (ROM) is beneficial for muscle activation and hypertrophy.

content: Pinto and colleagues (581) showed that full ROM training of the elbow flexors (0 to 130° of flexion) produced greater increases in muscle thickness compared to partial-range training (50 to 100° of flexion). The difference in effect size strongly favored the full ROM condition (1.09 vs. 0.57, respectively), indicating that the magnitude of variance was meaningful.
summary: A study demonstrated that full ROM elbow flexion exercises led to greater muscle thickness compared to partial-range training.

content: McMahon and colleagues (482) showed that although knee extension at full ROM (0 to 90°) and partial ROM (0 to 50°) both increased quadriceps muscle cross-sectional area, the magnitude of hypertrophy was significantly greater at 75% of femur length in the full-range condition.
summary: Full ROM knee extensions resulted in greater quadriceps hypertrophy compared to partial ROM knee extensions.

content: Bloomquist and colleagues (81) showed that deep squats (0 to 120° of knee flexion) promoted increases in cross-sectional area across the entire frontal thigh musculature, whereas shallow squats (0 to 60° of knee flexion) elicited significant growth only in the two most proximal sites. Furthermore, the overall change in cross-sectional area was greater at all measured sites in the deep squat group.
summary: Deep squats (0 to 120° of knee flexion) led to greater overall muscle growth in the quadriceps compared to shallow squats (0 to 60° of knee flexion).

content: There is evidence that training at longer muscle lengths (i.e., when the muscle is in a stretched position) promotes greater hypertrophic adaptations than training at shorter muscle lengths.
summary: Training at longer muscle lengths appears to be more effective for muscle growth.

content: The difference in effect size strongly favored the full ROM condition (1.09 vs. 0.57, respectively), indicating that the magnitude of variance was meaningful. McMahon and colleagues (482) showed that although knee extension at full ROM (0 to 90°) and partial ROM (0 to 50°) both increased quadriceps muscle cross-sectional area, the magnitude of hypertrophy was significantly greater at 75% of femur length in the full-range condition. Bloomquist and colleagues (81) showed that deep squats (0 to 120° of knee flexion) promoted increases in cross-sectional area across the entire frontal thigh musculature, whereas shallow squats (0 to 60° of knee flexion) elicited significant growth only in the two most proximal sites. Furthermore, the overall change in cross-sectional area was greater at all measured sites in the deep squat group.
summary: Research consistently demonstrates that training with full range of motion (ROM) leads to greater muscle hypertrophy compared to partial ROM.

content: There is evidence that training at longer muscle lengths (i.e., when the muscle is in a stretched position) promotes greater hypertrophic adaptations than training at shorter muscle lengths. McMahon and colleagues (481) compared the hypertrophic response to knee extensions at shortened (0 to 50° of knee flexion) or lengthened (40 to 90° of knee flexion) positions. Results showed significantly greater increases in distal cross-sectional area of the quadriceps (53% vs. 18%) as well as fascicle length (29% vs. 14%) in favor of the long- versus short-length training, respectively. Moreover, IGF-1 levels were significantly greater following long- versus short-length training (31% vs. 7%, respectively), suggesting that exercise at long muscle lengths induces greater metabolic and mechanical stress. Other research also shows a clear hypertrophic advantage to training at longer muscle lengths during knee extension exercises (535). The combination of findings indicates that stretched muscle is in an optimal position for hypertrophy.
summary: Training at longer muscle lengths, where the muscle is stretched, appears to promote greater muscle hypertrophy compared to shorter muscle lengths.

content: Bloom-quist et al. (81) studied 24 untrained young males. Bloom-quist et al. (81) had subjects perform either deep squats (0 to 120° of knee flexion) or shallow squats (0 to 60° of knee flexion). Bloom-quist et al. (81) subjects performed 3 to 5 sets of 6 to 10 reps for 3 days per week. Bloom-quist et al. (81) study lasted 12 weeks. Bloom-quist et al. (81) used MRI and DXA to measure hypertrophy. Bloom-quist et al. (81) found significantly greater increases in frontal thigh CSA and greater absolute gains in lean mass for the 0 to 120° condition.
summary: A study by Bloomquist et al. (81) found that deep squats (0 to 120° of knee flexion) led to greater muscle hypertrophy compared to shallow squats (0 to 60° of knee flexion) in untrained young males.

content: McMahon et al. (482) studied 26 recreationally active young men and women. McMahon et al. (482) had subjects perform either full ROM (0 to 90° of knee flexion) or partial ROM (0 to 50° of knee flexion) lower-body training. McMahon et al. (482) subjects performed 3 sets at 80% of 1RM for 3 days per week. McMahon et al. (482) study lasted 8 weeks. McMahon et al. (482) used ultrasound to measure hypertrophy. McMahon et al. (482) found significantly greater increases in vastus lateralis CSA for the full ROM condition.
summary: McMahon et al. (482) demonstrated that full range of motion (0 to 90°) lower-body training resulted in greater muscle hypertrophy compared to partial ROM (0 to 50°) training in recreationally active young adults.

content: Pinto et al. (581) studied 40 untrained young males. Pinto et al. (581) had subjects perform elbow flexion exercises with either full ROM (0 to 130°) or partial ROM (50 to 100°). Pinto et al. (581) subjects performed 2 to 4 sets of 8- to 20RM twice per week. Pinto et al. (581) study lasted 10 weeks. Pinto et al. (581) used ultrasound to measure hypertrophy. Pinto et al. (581) found no significant differences between conditions.
summary: Pinto et al. (581) found no significant difference in muscle hypertrophy between full ROM and partial ROM elbow flexion exercises in untrained young males.

content: Training to failure may enhance hypertrophy by increasing metabolic stress. Continuing to train under conditions of anaerobic glycolysis heightens the buildup of metabolites, which theoretically augments postexercise anabolism. The continued compression of vessels induces greater acute hypoxia in the working muscles, which may further contribute to hypertrophic adaptations.
summary: Training to muscular failure may enhance hypertrophy by increasing metabolic stress and hypoxia in the working muscles.

content: Goto and colleagues (262) compared hypertrophic adaptations between two groups of recreationally trained men performing 3 to 5 sets of 10 repetitions with an interset rest period of 60 seconds. One group performed repetitions continuously to failure, and the other group took a 30-second rest period at the midpoint of each set. After 12 weeks, muscle cross-sectional area was markedly greater in the group that carried out training to failure compared to the group that did not.
summary: Goto and colleagues (262) found that training to failure resulted in greater muscle hypertrophy compared to training with a rest period within sets.

content: Although these results are intriguing, the style of training does not replicate a traditional nonfailure approach in which sets are stopped just short of all-out effort. At most, the study shows that stopping well short of failure attenuates hypertrophic adaptations. Conversely, Sampson and Groeller (642) found no differences in untrained people between training to failure at 85% of 1RM and stopping 2 repetitions short of failure at this intensity of load. This suggests that failure might be less important when training with heavy loads.
summary: While training to failure may enhance hypertrophy, its importance may be less significant when training with heavy loads.

content: A recent study by Giessing and colleagues (247) revealed that well-trained subjects gained significantly greater lean mass when training to muscular failure at 80% of 1RM than when using a self-determined termination of a set at 60% of 1RM.
summary: Giessing and colleagues (247) found that training to failure at 80% of 1RM led to greater lean mass gains in well-trained individuals compared to a self-determined termination of sets at 60% of 1RM.

content: At most, the study shows that stopping well short of failure attenuates hypertrophic adaptations. This suggests that failure might be less important when training with heavy loads. A recent study by Giessing and colleagues (247) revealed that well-trained subjects gained significantly greater lean mass when training to muscular failure at 80% of 1RM than when using a self-determined termination of a set at 60% of 1RM.
summary: This chunk explores the impact of training to failure on hypertrophy, suggesting it may be more beneficial for heavier loads and well-trained individuals.

content: Limitations of the study include the use of a single-set training protocol, which as previously discussed is suboptimal for maximal hypertrophic gains, and different intensities of load between conditions.
summary: This chunk highlights limitations of a specific study regarding training protocol and load intensity.

content: Although training to failure may enhance the hypertrophic stimulus, there is evidence that it also increases the potential for overtraining and psychological burnout. Izquierdo and colleagues (338) reported reductions in resting IGF-1 concentrations and a blunting of resting testosterone levels in a group of physically active men when failure training was consistently employed over the course of a 16-week resistance training protocol. Such hormonal alterations are consistent with chronic overtraining, suggesting a detrimental effect of repeatedly working to the point of failure.
summary: This chunk discusses the potential downsides of consistently training to failure, including overtraining and hormonal imbalances.

content: Goto et al. (262) studied 26 untrained young men. Participants were randomly assigned to perform either 3 to 5 sets of 10RM or the same routine with a 30-second rest at the midpoint of each set so that failure was not induced. All groups performed 2 upper-body exercises and 1 lower-body exercise twice per week. The study duration was 12 weeks. MRI measurements showed significantly greater increases in quadriceps CSA for the training to fatigue condition.
summary: This chunk describes a study comparing training to fatigue versus stopping short of failure, finding greater muscle growth with fatigue.

content: Samson and Groeller (642) studied 28 untrained young men. Participants were randomly assigned to perform resisted elbow flexion under one of three conditions: a control condition that performed both concentric and eccentric components at a speed of 2 seconds; a rapid shortening condition that performed maximal acceleration during the concentric action followed by a 2-second eccentric action; or a stretch–shortening group that performed both eccentric and concentric components with maximal acceleration. The control group trained to failure; the other two groups did not. Training consisted of 4 sets at 85% of 1RM performed 3 days per week. MRI measurements showed no significant differences in elbow flexor CSA between groups.
summary: This chunk details a study comparing different training techniques (control, rapid shortening, stretch-shortening) with and without training to failure, finding no significant differences in muscle growth.

content: Schott et al. (667) studied 7 untrained young men and women. Participants performed either an intermittent isometric knee extension protocol consisting of 4 sets of 10 reps lasting 3 seconds with a 2-second rest between reps and a 2-minute rest between sets or a continuous protocol of 4 sets of isometric actions lasting 30 seconds with a 1-minute rest between sets. Training was carried out 3 days per week. CT measurements showed greater increases in quadriceps hypertrophy for the training to fatigue condition.
summary: This chunk describes a study comparing intermittent and continuous isometric training protocols, finding greater muscle growth with training to fatigue.

content: The literature suggests a benefit to performing at least some sets to failure in a hypertrophy-oriented program. This seems to be particularly important when employing high-repetition training because of the relationship between the proximity to failure and muscle activation during light-load training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the potential benefits of incorporating training to failure, especially in high-repetition training.

content: Persistently training to failure increases the potential for nonfunctional overreaching and perhaps overtraining. The best approach is to periodize the use and frequency of failure training to maximize muscular adaptations while avoiding an overtrained state. An example would be performing an initial cycle in which all sets are stopped a repetition or two short of failure, followed by taking the last set of each exercise to failure, and then culminating in a brief cycle in which the majority of sets are carried out to failure.
summary: This chunk advises against consistently training to failure and recommends a periodized approach to maximize benefits and minimize overtraining.

content: Multiset protocols favoring high volumes of resistance training optimize the hypertrophic response. Beginners should perform approximately 40 to 70 repetitions per muscle group per session. More advanced lifters may need double the repetition amount of beginners. Volume should be progressively increased over the course of a training cycle. Periods of reduced training volume should be integrated on a regular basis to facilitate the recovery process.
summary: This chunk outlines principles for optimizing hypertrophy through multiset protocols, volume manipulation, and periodization.

content: Higher training frequencies appear to confer benefits, at least over short-term training protocols. Split routines allow for a greater volume of work per muscle group per session, potentially enhancing muscular adaptations via the dose–response relationship between volume and hypertrophy. It may be beneficial to periodize frequency over time, altering the number of times a muscle group is trained weekly in accordance with individual response.
summary: This chunk discusses the benefits of higher training frequencies and split routines, suggesting periodization based on individual response.

content: Training across a wide spectrum of repetition ranges (1 to 20+) is recommended to ensure the complete development of the whole muscle. There is merit to focusing on a medium-repetition range (6- to 12RM) and devoting specific training cycles to lower- and higher-repetition training.
summary: This chunk recommends incorporating a variety of repetition ranges for comprehensive muscle development.

content: Once facility has been established with the basic movement patterns, a variety of exercises should be employed over the course of a periodized training program to maximize whole-body muscle hypertrophy. This should include the liberal use of free-form (i.e., free weights and cables) and machine-based exercises. Both multi- and single-joint exercises should be included in a hypertrophy-specific routine to maximize muscular growth.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of exercise variety, including both free-form and machine-based exercises, for maximizing muscle growth.

content: Training across a wide spectrum of repetition ranges (1 to 20+) is recommended to ensure the complete development of the whole muscle. There is merit to focusing on a medium-repetition range (6- to 12RM) and devoting specific training cycles to lower- and higher-repetition training.
summary: A variety of repetition ranges should be incorporated for optimal muscle development, with a focus on the medium range and dedicated cycles for lower and higher reps.

content: Once facility has been established with the basic movement patterns, a variety of exercises should be employed over the course of a periodized training program to maximize whole-body muscle hypertrophy. This should include the liberal use of free-form (i.e., free weights and cables) and machine-based exercises.
summary: Periodized training programs should utilize a variety of exercises, including free weights, cables, and machines, to maximize muscle growth.

content: Both multi- and single-joint exercises should be included in a hypertrophy-specific routine to maximize muscular growth. Both concentric and eccentric actions should be incorporated during training.
summary: Hypertrophy routines should incorporate both multi-joint and single-joint exercises, along with both concentric and eccentric movements.

content: Evidence of the benefits of combining isometric actions with dynamic actions is lacking at this time. The addition of supramaximal eccentric loading may enhance the hypertrophic response.
summary: While the benefits of combining isometric and dynamic actions are unclear, supramaximal eccentric loading may improve muscle growth.

content: An optimal rest interval for hypertrophy training does not appear to exist. Research indicates that resting at least 2 minutes between sets provides a hypertrophic advantage over resting for shorter periods. Including training cycles that limit rest periods to 60 to 90 seconds may allow a lifter to take advantage of any additive effects of metabolic stress, if they exist.
summary: Rest intervals for hypertrophy training can vary, with longer rests (2+ minutes) generally beneficial, and shorter rests (60-90 seconds) potentially offering metabolic stress benefits.

content: Current evidence suggests little difference in muscle hypertrophy when training with isotonic repetition durations ranging from 0.5 to 6 seconds to muscular failure. Thus, it would seem that a fairly wide range of repetition durations can be employed if the primary goal is to maximize muscle growth. Training at very slow volitional durations (>10 sec per repetition) appears to be suboptimal for increasing muscle size and thus should be avoided. Combining repetition durations could conceivably enhance the hypertrophic response to resistance training.
summary: A wide range of repetition durations can be effective for hypertrophy, avoiding very slow repetitions. Combining different durations may further enhance muscle growth.

content: Evidence indicates a hypertrophic benefit for muscles worked first in a resistance training bout. Therefore, lagging muscles should be trained earlier in the session.
summary: Training lagging muscles earlier in a workout session may promote greater hypertrophy.

content: Full ROM movements should form the basis of a hypertrophy-oriented program. Integrating some partial-range movements may enhance hypertrophy.
summary: Full range of motion exercises are foundational for hypertrophy, with some partial-range movements potentially offering additional benefits.

content: Hypertrophy-oriented programs should include sets taken to muscular failure as well as those that are terminated short of an all-out effort.
summary: Hypertrophy programs should incorporate both sets taken to failure and sets terminated short of failure.

content: The use of failure training should be periodized to maximize muscular adaptations while avoiding an overtrained state.
summary: Periodization of failure training is crucial for maximizing muscle growth while preventing overtraining.

content: It is commonly thought that aerobic endurance exercise produces little to no increase in muscle hypertrophy. This belief is consistent with evidence showing that aerobic-type exercise mediates catabolic pathways, whereas anaerobic exercise mediates anabolic pathways.
summary: Aerobic exercise is often perceived as detrimental to muscle growth, as it is associated with catabolic pathways, while anaerobic exercise promotes anabolic pathways.

content: Atherton and colleagues (47) conducted pioneering work to elucidate differences in the intracellular signaling response between the two types of exercises. Using an ex vivo model, they electrically stimulated isolated rat muscles with either intermittent high-frequency bursts to simulate resistance-type training or continuous low-frequency activation to simulate aerobic-type training. Postintervention analysis revealed that AMPK phosphorylation in the low-frequency condition increased approximately 2-fold immediately and 3 hours poststimulation, whereas phosphorylation was suppressed in the high-frequency condition over the same period. Conversely, phosphorylation of Akt was a mirror image of AMPK results: markedly greater phosphorylation was seen in the high-frequency condition.
summary: Research has investigated the signaling differences between aerobic and resistance exercise, showing that AMPK is activated by aerobic exercise, while Akt is activated by resistance exercise.

content: AMPK acts as an energy sensor to turn on catabolic signaling cascades, whereas Akt promotes the intracellular signaling responses associated with anabolism. These findings led to the AMPK–Akt switch hypothesis (see figure 4.1), which states that aerobic and anaerobic exercise produce opposing signaling responses and thus are incompatible for optimizing muscular adaptations (47).
summary: The AMPK-Akt switch hypothesis suggests that aerobic and anaerobic exercise induce opposing signaling pathways, potentially hindering simultaneous muscle growth optimization.

content: Subsequent research, however, indicates that the concept of a switch that regulates anabolic and catabolic signaling pathways is at best overly simplistic and ultimately somewhat misleading. Considerable overlap has been shown to exist between candidate genes involved in aerobic and strength phenotypes, indicating that the two muscle traits are not at opposite ends of the molecular spectrum (765). Multiple studies have shown increased mTOR activation following aerobic endurance exercise (71, 458, 460), whereas resistance training has consistently been found to increase the levels of AMPK (149, 188, 380, 797). To this end, research shows that of 263 genes analyzed in the resting state, only 21 were differently expressed in aerobic endurance–trained athletes and strength-trained athletes (724).
summary: While the AMPK-Akt switch hypothesis is simplistic, research suggests that aerobic and strength training share many molecular pathways, and both can contribute to muscle growth.

content: This chapter addresses how aerobic endurance exercise affects muscle growth. The topic is addressed both when aerobic exercise is performed in isolation and when it is combined with resistance exercise (i.e., concurrent training). Contrary to popular belief, a majority of studies show that aerobic training can promote a hypertrophic response in untrained subjects. Reported short-term (12 weeks) gains in skeletal muscle mass from aerobic training are similar to those seen in some resistance training protocols, and findings are demonstrated across a spectrum of age ranges in both men and women (379).
summary: This chapter explores the impact of aerobic exercise on muscle growth, both independently and in combination with resistance training. Contrary to common belief, aerobic training can promote muscle growth in untrained individuals.

content: The following mechanisms have been proposed to account for aerobic exercise–induced muscle growth (379), but the specific roles of these factors and their interactions have yet to be determined: Increased insulin-mediated anabolic signaling Increased muscle protein synthetic response to nutrition and insulin
summary: Several mechanisms, including increased insulin signaling and muscle protein synthesis, have been proposed to explain how aerobic exercise contributes to muscle growth, but further research is needed to clarify their roles.

content: Contrary to popular belief, a majority of studies show that aerobic training can promote a hypertrophic response in untrained subjects. Reported short-term (12 weeks) gains in skeletal muscle mass from aerobic training are similar to those seen in some resistance training protocols, and findings are demonstrated across a spectrum of age ranges in both men and women (379).
summary: Aerobic training can induce muscle hypertrophy in untrained individuals, with short-term gains comparable to some resistance training protocols.

content:
The following mechanisms have been proposed to account for aerobic exercise–induced muscle growth (379), but the specific roles of these factors and their interactions have yet to be determined:
Increased insulin-mediated anabolic signaling
Increased muscle protein synthetic response to nutrition and insulin
Increased basal postabsorptive muscle protein synthesis
Increased amino acid delivery
Increased blood flow and skeletal muscle perfusion
Decreased myostatin
Decreased chronic inflammation
Decreased FOXO signaling
Decreased protein and DNA damage
Increased mitochondrial proliferation and dynamics
Increased mitochondrial energetics (e.g., decreased ROS and increased ATP)
summary: Several mechanisms, including increased insulin signaling, protein synthesis, and mitochondrial function, have been proposed to explain aerobic exercise-induced muscle growth.

content: Although most studies have evaluated the muscular adaptations associated with lower-body aerobic training, there is evidence that hypertrophy can be achieved from upper-body arm cycle ergometry as well (778). The extent of hypertrophic adaptations is contingent on intensity, frequency, volume, and mode, as well as additional factors.
summary: Hypertrophy can occur from both lower-body and upper-body aerobic training, and the extent of adaptation depends on various factors like intensity, frequency, and volume.

content: Decreases in muscle cross-sectional area of approximately 20% have been noted in both Type I and Type II fibers after 13 weeks of marathon run training. Low-intensity exercise is not beneficial to hypertrophy and, in fact, seems to be detrimental when carried out over long durations (773). Current research suggests that at least some of the training should be carried out at a minimum of 80% of heart rate reserve (HRR).
summary: Endurance training like marathon running can lead to muscle fiber atrophy, while low-intensity exercise is detrimental to hypertrophy. Training intensity should be at least 80% of heart rate reserve for potential hypertrophy benefits.

content: Training with brief high-intensity intervals (85% of V .O2peak) interspersed with recovery was shown to increase thigh muscle cross-sectional area by 24% in middle-aged people with type 2 diabetes, indicating a potential dose–response relationship between hypertrophy and aerobic intensity. Higher aerobic training volumes would seemingly be required in untrained younger people to promote an adaptive response.
summary: High-intensity interval training (85% of VO2peak) can increase muscle cross-sectional area, suggesting a dose-response relationship between intensity and hypertrophy. Untrained younger individuals may require higher training volumes for adaptation.

content: Each session lasted 45 minutes, and training occurred 5 days per week. Intensity was progressively increased so that participants ultimately worked at 85% of heart rate reserve over the last 2 months of the study. Only the older men increased muscle mass; no muscular changes were seen in the younger men. Attendance of the younger subjects was significantly less than that of their older counterparts, implying a hypertrophic benefit to greater aerobic training frequency.
summary: Studies on aerobic training and hypertrophy have shown that older men benefit more from training, potentially due to higher attendance and adherence.

content: The vast majority of studies on the topic to date have involved cycling exercise, and most of these trials have shown increased muscle protein accretion with consistent training. Studies using noncycling activities have produced mixed results.
summary: Cycling exercise is the most common activity studied for its effects on muscle hypertrophy, with consistent training generally leading to increased muscle protein accretion. Other activities have shown mixed results.

content: The previously mentioned study by Schwartz and colleagues (673) found increased muscle mass in elderly but not young male subjects following 6 months of a walk/jog/run protocol. In a study of elderly women, Sipila and Suominen (697) showed that a combination of step aerobics and track walking at intensities up to 80% of HRR did not significantly increase muscle cross-sectional area after 18 weeks of training.
summary: Studies on walking/running and step aerobics for hypertrophy have shown mixed results, with some studies showing increases in muscle mass in elderly men but not younger men, while others show no significant changes in muscle cross-sectional area in elderly women.

content: Untrained elderly men achieved levels of hypertrophy similar to those of their younger counterparts following 12 weeks of cycle ergometry training despite completing approximately half of the total mechanical workload. Longer periods of sedentarism reduce the total volume necessary for increasing muscle mass, which lends credence to the hypothesis that reviving muscle lost over time is easier than increasing levels that are close to baseline.
summary: Elderly men can achieve similar levels of hypertrophy as younger men with cycle ergometry training, and longer periods of inactivity may make it easier to regain lost muscle mass.

content: Jubrias and colleagues (345) reported no muscle cross-sectional area changes in elderly men and women following a 24-week stair climbing and kayaking-type aerobic exercise protocol performed with progressively increased intensity up to 85% of HRR. Table 4.1 provides a summary of the research related to aerobic training and muscle hypertrophy.
summary: Some aerobic exercise protocols, like stair climbing and kayaking, have not shown significant muscle hypertrophy in elderly individuals.

content: A follow-up study employing a similar protocol in younger and older men showed that 12 weeks of cycle ergometry produced an increase in Type I fiber cross-sectional area of approximately 20% (288). Type IIa fiber diameter actually decreased in younger subjects, although not significantly, whereas that of the older subjects remained relatively constant.
summary: Cycle ergometry training increased Type I fiber cross-sectional area in both younger and older men, while Type IIa fiber diameter decreased in younger men and remained stable in older men.

content: Aerobic exercise may have a detrimental effect on hypertrophy of the faster fiber types. Other studies show beneficial effects of aerobic training on Type II fiber cross-sectional area in both older (132, 152) and younger (32) subjects. Evidence also suggests that an increase in mitochondrial proteins is responsible for at least some of the increased fiber growth associated with aerobic endurance training (433).
summary: The effects of aerobic exercise on Type II fiber hypertrophy are mixed, with some studies showing detrimental effects while others show beneficial effects. Mitochondrial protein synthesis may contribute to fiber growth.

content: A number of studies have reported that aerobic exercise increases only basal mitochondrial protein synthesis and has no effect on myofibrillar protein synthesis (185, 257, 312, 828). Recent work by Di Donato and colleagues (180) showed that both mitochondrial and myofibrillar protein fractions were elevated following an acute bout of high-intensity (90% of maximal heart rate) and low-intensity (66% of maximal heart rate) aerobic exercise.
summary: While some studies suggest aerobic exercise primarily increases mitochondrial protein synthesis, recent research indicates that both mitochondrial and myofibrillar protein fractions can be elevated after both high-intensity and low-intensity aerobic exercise.

content: Aerobic exercise may have a detrimental effect on hypertrophy of the faster fiber types.  Recent work by Di Donato and colleagues (180) showed that both mitochondrial and myofibrillar protein fractions were elevated following an acute bout of high-intensity (90% of maximal heart rate) and low-intensity (66% of maximal heart rate) aerobic exercise. Only the high-intensity condition showed sustained muscle protein synthesis elevations at 24 to 28 hours postexercise recovery. Based on these acute results, it would seem that sarcoplasmic proteins account for a considerable portion of aerobic-induced hypertrophic adaptations. Given evidence that the growth of a given muscle fiber is achieved at the expense of its aerobic endurance capacity (784), the accretion of mitochondrial proteins seems to have a negative impact on the ability to maximize gains in contractile proteins.
summary: This chunk explores the potential negative impact of aerobic exercise on muscle hypertrophy, particularly in Type II fibers, and suggests that mitochondrial adaptations may come at the expense of contractile protein growth.

content: Other studies show beneficial effects of aerobic training on Type II fiber cross-sectional area in both older (132, 152) and younger (32) subjects. Evidence also suggests that an increase in mitochondrial proteins is responsible for at least some of the increased fiber growth associated with aerobic endurance training (433). A number of studies have reported that aerobic exercise increases only basal mitochondrial protein synthesis and has no effect on myofibrillar protein synthesis (185, 257, 312, 828).
summary: This chunk presents contrasting evidence regarding the effects of aerobic exercise on Type II fiber hypertrophy, highlighting both potential benefits and limitations.

content: In those who are sedentary, virtually any training stimulus—including aerobic exercise—is sufficient to overload muscle. This necessarily results in an adaptive response that promotes tissue remodeling. However, the intensity of aerobic training is not sufficient to progressively overload muscle in a manner that promotes further adaptations over time. Thus, it stands to reason that the body would quickly plateau after an initial increase in muscle size. Aerobic exercise can increase hypertrophy in sedentary people, primarily in Type I muscle fibers. The extent of hypertrophic increases depends on the level of sedentarism; greater gains are seen in the elderly than in the young.
summary: This chunk explains how aerobic exercise can induce muscle growth in sedentary individuals but may not be sufficient for long-term adaptations due to insufficient progressive overload.

content: Intensities of ≥80% of HRR are generally needed to elicit significant muscular growth. Although definitive evidence regarding the effects of aerobic volume on hypertrophy is lacking, research indicates that longer periods of sedentarism reduce the total weekly duration required to promote the accretion of lean mass. With respect to the modality of exercise, cycling appears to have the greatest hypertrophic benefit, although the paucity of studies on alternative modalities makes it difficult to draw firm conclusions on this variable.
summary: This chunk discusses the influence of exercise intensity, volume, and modality on aerobic-induced hypertrophy, highlighting the need for higher intensities and longer durations for significant gains.

content: Importantly, muscular gains are limited to the early phases after initiating a regimented aerobic exercise program. Results plateau in a relatively short time, and evidence suggests that persistent aerobic training can actually have a detrimental impact on Type II fiber hypertrophy. Early-phase increases in aerobic-induced hypertrophy may be in part due to quantitative or qualitative mitochondrial adaptations, or both.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the transient nature of aerobic-induced hypertrophy, suggesting that prolonged training may hinder muscle growth.

content: Inactivity induces negative alterations in mitochondrial morphology, and these effects are exacerbated by prolonged sedentarism (147). Mitochondrial dysfunction is associated with increased activation of AMPK and subsequent stimulation of protein degradation, ultimately causing atrophy (259). Aerobic training enhances mitochondrial protein fractions, which would confer a positive effect on anabolic processes.
summary: This chunk explores the relationship between mitochondrial health, inactivity, and muscle atrophy, highlighting the beneficial effects of aerobic training on mitochondrial function.

content: Although aerobic exercise can positively affect muscle mass in the untrained, compelling evidence indicates that it is suboptimal for promoting muscle growth in physically active people. For those who are sedentary, virtually any stimulus challenges the neuromuscular system and thus leads to an accretion of muscle proteins. Adaptations in these early stages are therefore more indicative of the novelty of the exercise bout as opposed to an increased potential for chronic adaptation. Well-trained people have already adapted to lower-level stresses, and it therefore remains highly dubious whether aerobic training would provide enough of a stimulus for further muscular adaptation.
summary: This chunk contrasts the effects of aerobic exercise on muscle growth in untrained versus trained individuals, suggesting that it is less effective for promoting hypertrophy in those who are already physically active.

content: In trained lifters, the mechanical strain associated with aerobic endurance exercise does not rise to the level necessary for mechanotransducers to switch on mTORC1 signaling (797). Aerobic endurance athletes display slight increases in Type I fiber size while showing a reduction in hypertrophy of Type II fibers (198). Even very intense aerobic exercise does not seem to confer a beneficial hypertrophic effect in those who are highly physically active. This was demonstrated by the fact that 6 weeks of high-intensity interval training resulted in a significant decrease in Type II fiber cross-sectional area in a group of well-trained distance runners (373).
summary: This chunk provides evidence that aerobic exercise, even at high intensities, may not effectively stimulate muscle growth in trained individuals, potentially leading to a reduction in Type II fiber size.

content: Muscular adaptations to aerobic training exist on a continuum, and hypertrophic responses ultimately depend on a variety of individual and environmental factors. Between-study comparisons suggest that early-phase gains in muscle mass are similar between aerobic and resistance training protocols (238). Within-study results indicate a clear hypertrophic advantage to resistance training. Pooled data from studies directly comparing hypertrophy in the two types of exercise show a strong overall mean effect size for resistance training (0.92), whereas aerobic training produced a weak overall effect (0.27); these differences were statistically significant (831). Moreover, increases in muscle size following aerobic training are not well correlated with increased force capacity, indicating that hypertrophic adaptations are not entirely functional (433).
summary: This chunk acknowledges the variability in muscle growth responses to aerobic training and emphasizes the superior hypertrophy-inducing effects of resistance training.

content: Aerobic exercise is often performed in combination with resistance training for accelerating fat loss, enhancing sport performance, or both. This strategy, called concurrent training, has been shown to have a positive effect on weight management (10). Evidence suggests that the addition of aerobic exercise to a regimented resistance training program may compromise muscle growth. Negative hypertrophic effects from concurrent training have been attributed to a phenomenon known as chronic interference (figure 4.2), the hypothesis for which alleges that trained muscle cannot simultaneously adapt optimally morphologically or metabolically to both strength and aerobic endurance training (831).
summary: This chunk discusses the potential benefits and drawbacks of concurrent training, highlighting the possibility of compromised muscle growth due to chronic interference.

content: Aerobic exercise is often performed in combination with resistance training for accelerating fat loss, enhancing sport performance, or both. This strategy, called concurrent training, has been shown to have a positive effect on weight management (10).
summary: Concurrent training combines aerobic and resistance exercise for benefits like fat loss and performance enhancement.

content: Evidence suggests that the addition of aerobic exercise to a regimented resistance training program may compromise muscle growth. Negative hypertrophic effects from concurrent training have been attributed to a phenomenon known as chronic interference (figure 4.2), the hypothesis for which alleges that trained muscle cannot simultaneously adapt optimally morphologically or metabolically to both strength and aerobic endurance training (831).
summary: Concurrent training may hinder muscle growth due to chronic interference, where muscles struggle to adapt optimally to both strength and endurance training.

content: Like the AMPK–Akt switch hypothesis, the chronic interference hypothesis states that these competing adaptations produce divergent intracellular signaling responses that mitigate muscular gains. There is evidence that concurrent training heightens mTOR and p70 S6K to a greater extent than resistance training alone does (432).
summary: Chronic interference theory suggests that concurrent training's competing adaptations lead to divergent signaling responses, potentially hindering muscle growth.

content: Studies show no deleterious effects of concurrent training on muscle protein synthesis (127, 185). Discrepancies in the findings may be related to a number of factors. Importantly, the time course of evaluation in the current literature was generally limited to several hours postexercise and thus does not provide a complete snapshot of the adaptive response, which can last in excess of 24 hours. These findings are specific to acute bouts of exercise, whereas any interference would seemingly manifest over a period of weeks or months.
summary: While some studies show no negative effects of concurrent training on muscle protein synthesis, discrepancies exist due to limited evaluation timeframes. Interference effects likely manifest over longer periods.

content: Acute factors associated with aerobic training may interfere with resistance training capacity. Aerobic exercise can cause residual fatigue, substrate depletion, or both, which ultimately impairs the quality of the resistance training bout (238). Muscular adaptations are predicated on the capacity to train with an intensity of effort that sufficiently stimulates myofiber growth. If this ability is compromised, muscular gains necessarily suffer.
summary: Acute effects of aerobic exercise, like fatigue and substrate depletion, can negatively impact resistance training quality and hinder muscle growth.

content: Another potential issue with concurrent training is an increased potential for overtraining. When the volume or intensity of training exceeds the body's ability to recover, physiological systems are disrupted. The stress of adding aerobic exercise to an intense hypertrophy-oriented resistance training program can overtax recuperative abilities, leading to an overtrained state. The interference effects of aerobic exercise associated with overtraining may be mediated by a catabolic hormonal environment and chronic muscle glycogen depletion (493).
summary: Concurrent training increases the risk of overtraining if volume or intensity exceeds recovery capacity, potentially due to hormonal imbalances and glycogen depletion.

content: Long-term training studies investigating muscular adaptations to concurrent training have produced conflicting findings. When considering the body of literature as a whole, evidence suggests that aerobic exercise blunts the hypertrophic response to resistance training. A meta-analysis by Wilson and colleagues (831) revealed that the effect size for muscular gains was reduced by almost 50% in those who solely lifted weights when aerobic endurance training was added to the mix.
summary: Long-term studies on concurrent training yield mixed results, but overall evidence suggests that aerobic exercise diminishes muscle growth from resistance training.

content: Research directly assessing the hypertrophy-related effects of aerobic endurance exercise intensities during concurrent training is lacking.
