summary: This chunk highlights the importance of finding the optimal range of dietary fat intake for testosterone production.

content: Evidence shows that the type of dietary fat consumed has a direct influence on body composition. Overfeeding young men and women of normal weight foods high in n-6 fatty acids caused an increase in lean tissue mass approximately 3-fold compared to comparable overfeeding with saturated fats.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the impact of different types of dietary fat on body composition.

content: PUFAs have been shown to enhance the fluidity of the cell membrane, whereas SFAs have the opposite effect. Cell membranes serve a critical role in regulating the passage of nutrients, hormones, and chemical signals into and out of cells. When membranes harden, they are desensitized to external stimuli, inhibiting cellular processes including protein synthesis. Cell membranes that are more fluid have an increased permeability, allowing substances and secondary messenger molecules associated with protein synthesis to readily penetrate the cytoplasm.
summary: This chunk explains the influence of different types of fatty acids on cell membrane fluidity and its impact on cellular processes.

content: This provides a physiological basis for a beneficial impact of PUFAs on muscle protein synthesis, compared to the negative effects of excess SFAs, which reduce the fluidity of the cell membrane. The n-3 fatty acids are believed to have a particularly important role in protein metabolism. A number of studies show that n-3 fatty acid supplementation results in greater accretion of muscle proteins compared to other types of fats in both animals and humans.
summary: This chunk highlights the benefits of PUFAs, particularly n-3 fatty acids, for muscle protein synthesis.

content: These effects may be in part regulated by n-3 fatty acid–mediated increases in cell membrane fluidity, which facilitates an enhanced mTOR/p70S6K signaling response. Additional benefits may be attributed to reductions in protein breakdown associated with the inhibition of the ubiquitin–proteasome pathway.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the mechanisms by which n-3 fatty acids promote muscle protein synthesis.

content: Although these findings are intriguing, the aforementioned studies were not carried out in conjunction with a structured resistance training protocol. It therefore remains speculative as to what, if any, effects n-3 fatty acids have for those seeking to maximize hypertrophic adaptations. No concrete guidelines can be given as to the amount of dietary fat needed to maximize muscle growth.
summary: This chunk acknowledges the limitations of existing research and the need for further investigation into the effects of n-3 fatty acids on muscle growth in the context of resistance training.

content: The balance of calories after accounting for the consumption of protein and carbohydrate determines the caloric surplus. A minimum of 1 g/kg/day of dietary fat appears sufficient to prevent hormonal alterations. It is prudent to obtain the majority of fat calories from unsaturated sources. Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) are conceivably important for enhancing muscle protein synthesis and should be prioritized over saturated fatty acids (SFAs).
summary: This chunk provides practical recommendations for dietary fat intake, emphasizing the importance of unsaturated fats and calorie balance.

content: The frequency of nutrient consumption can influence muscle protein accretion. Evidence of a leucine threshold suggests consuming multiple protein-rich meals throughout the day. Studies show dose-dependent and saturable effects at 10 g of EAAs, which is equivalent to approximately 20 g of a high-quality protein source. Muscle protein synthesis becomes unresponsive to any further increases in intake once the saturable level is reached.
summary: This chunk discusses the impact of meal frequency and protein intake on muscle protein synthesis.

content: Circulating amino acids are then shunted to fuel other protein-requiring processes, to suppress proteolysis, or toward oxidation.
summary: This chunk explains the fate of excess amino acids when the muscle protein synthesis threshold is reached.

content: The frequency of nutrient consumption can influence muscle protein accretion. Evidence of a leucine threshold suggests consuming multiple protein-rich meals throughout the day. Studies show dose-dependent and saturable effects at 10 g of EAAs, which is equivalent to approximately 20 g of a high-quality protein source. Muscle protein synthesis becomes unresponsive to any further increases in intake once the saturable level is reached. Circulating amino acids are then shunted to fuel other protein-requiring processes, to suppress proteolysis, or toward oxidation.
summary: Optimal protein intake frequency for muscle growth is influenced by the leucine threshold, with multiple meals throughout the day recommended.

content: With muscle full status, myofibrillar muscle protein synthesis is stimulated within 1 hour, but the stimulation returns to baseline within 3 hours despite sustained elevations in amino acid availability. It is hypothesized that consuming protein every few hours throughout the day optimizes muscle protein accretion by continually elevating levels of muscle protein synthesis and attenuating muscle protein breakdown.
summary: Muscle protein synthesis is stimulated by protein intake, but the effect is transient, suggesting the need for frequent protein consumption.

content: Twenty-four well-trained men were randomized to consume 80 g of whey protein as either a pulse feeding (8 × 10 g every 1.5 hours), an intermediate feeding (4 × 20 g every 3 hours), or a bolus feeding (2 × 40 g every 6 hours) during 12 hours of recovery after a resistance training bout. The intermediate feeding condition was superior to either the pulse or bolus feeding condition for stimulating muscle protein synthesis over the recovery period.
summary: Intermediate protein feeding (4 × 20 g every 3 hours) was most effective for stimulating muscle protein synthesis after resistance training.

content: A recent study by Mamerow and colleagues (449) provides additional insight into the topic. In a randomized crossover design, 8 healthy subjects followed isoenergetic and isonitrogenous diets at breakfast, lunch, and dinner for two separate 7-day periods. During one condition, protein was distributed approximately evenly throughout each meal; in the other, it was skewed so that almost 2/3 of the daily protein dose was consumed at dinner. Protein intake was sufficient for maximal anabolism, amounting to 1.6 g/kg/day. All meals were individually prepared by the research staff. Results showed that muscle protein synthesis was approximately 25% greater when protein intake was evenly distributed compared to a skewed distribution.
summary: Evenly distributing protein intake throughout the day leads to greater muscle protein synthesis compared to a skewed distribution.

content: Several longitudinal studies have investigated the effects of protein intake frequency on body composition in conjunction with mixed meals. In a 2-week intervention on elderly women, Arnal and colleagues (43) demonstrated that protein pulse feeding (women consumed 79% of total daily protein in a single feeding of ~52g) resulted in a greater retention of fat-free mass compared to a condition in which protein feedings were equally spread over the course of 4 daily meals. Alternatively, a follow-up study by the same researchers using an almost identical nutritional protocol found no difference between pulse- and spread-feeding frequencies in a group of young women (44). These findings are consistent with those of Adechian and colleagues (16), who reported no differences in body composition between protein pulse feeding (80% protein in one meal) and spread feeding (4 equally spaced portions of protein) in a group of young obese women.
summary: Protein pulse feeding may be more beneficial for elderly individuals, while younger individuals may benefit from a more evenly distributed intake.

content: The discrepancies in studies can seemingly be attributed to the age-related differences in the subjects. As previously mentioned, the aging process desensitizes muscle to protein feedings, resulting in a greater per-meal requirement to hit the leucine threshold. It is estimated that elderly people require high-quality protein in a dose of approximately 40 g for a maximal anabolic response; younger people require approximately half this amount (834, 845). The spread-feeding group in the study of elderly subjects consumed approximately 26 g of protein per meal (43), which would put them far below the leucine threshold during each feeding. The pulse-feeding group, on the other hand, would have hit the leucine threshold in the 80% protein meal, which may have been sufficient to promote a superior anabolic effect. In the studies of young subjects (16, 44), the spread-feeding group consumed >20 g per serving, thus exceeding the leucine threshold.
summary: Age-related differences in muscle sensitivity to protein intake may explain discrepancies in study findings.

content: A limitation of these studies is that subjects did not perform resistance exercise, thereby impeding generalizability to those seeking to maximize hypertrophy. Given that the anabolic effect of a protein-rich meal lasts approximately 5 to 6 hours (410), it is reasonable to conclude that young people should consume three meals, spread throughout the day, containing at least 10 g of EAAs to optimize muscle growth. This frequency pattern ensures that the body remains in anabolism over the course of the day and takes full advantage of the >24-hour sensitizing effect of resistance training on skeletal muscle (49). Elderly people may require up to double this amount.
summary: Optimal protein intake frequency for muscle growth likely involves consuming three meals throughout the day for young individuals, with elderly individuals potentially requiring more frequent meals.

content: The period after exercise is often considered the most critical part of nutrient timing from a muscle-building standpoint. This is based on the premise of an anabolic window of opportunity, whereby the provision of nutrients within approximately 1 hour of the completion of exercise enhances the hypertrophic response to the bout (360). According to nutrient timing theory, delaying consumption outside of this limited window has negative repercussions on muscle growth. Some researchers have even postulated that the timing of nutrient consumption is of greater importance to body composition than absolute daily nutrient consumption (121).
summary: Nutrient timing, particularly within the post-exercise period, is believed to be crucial for maximizing muscle growth.

content: Protein is clearly the critical nutrient for optimizing the hypertrophic response. Anabolism is primarily mediated by EAAs, with minimal contribution from nonessential amino acids (89, 767). It has been proposed that consumption of carbohydrate potentiates the anabolic effects of postexercise protein intake, thereby increasing muscle protein accretion (334). Intense exercise causes the depletion of a substantial proportion of stored fuels (including glycogen and amino acids) and elicits structural perturbations (irritation or damage) of muscle fibers. Hypothetically, providing the body with nutrients following such exercise not only facilitates the repletion of energy reserves and remodeling of damaged tissue, but actually does so in a supercompensated manner that ultimately heightens muscular development.
summary: Protein, particularly EAAs, is essential for muscle growth, and carbohydrate may enhance its effects. Post-exercise nutrient intake replenishes depleted stores and supports muscle repair.

content: Numerous studies support the efficacy of nutrient timing for acutely increasing muscle protein synthesis following a resistance training bout over and above that of placebo (602, 766, 768, 769). These findings provide compelling evidence that exercise sensitizes muscles to nutrient administration.
summary: Research supports the benefits of nutrient timing for maximizing muscle protein synthesis after resistance training.

content: Intense exercise causes the depletion of a substantial proportion of stored fuels (including glycogen and amino acids) and elicits structural perturbations (irritation or damage) of muscle fibers. Hypothetically, providing the body with nutrients following such exercise not only facilitates the repletion of energy reserves and remodeling of damaged tissue, but actually does so in a supercompensated manner that ultimately heightens muscular development.
summary: Intense exercise depletes energy stores and damages muscle fibers. Post-exercise nutrition replenishes these stores and promotes muscle repair, potentially leading to enhanced muscle growth.

content: The concept of an anabolic window of opportunity was initially formulated from acute muscle protein synthesis data. In one of the earliest studies on the topic, Okamura and colleagues (550) found a significantly greater protein synthetic response when dogs were infused with amino acids immediately after 150 minutes of treadmill exercise compared to delaying administration for 2 hours. Subsequently, a human trial by Levenhagen and colleagues (416) showed that lower-body (and whole-body) protein synthesis of the legs increased significantly more when protein was ingested immediately following 60 minutes of cycling at 60% of V.O2max versus delaying consumption by 3 hours.
summary: Early research on muscle protein synthesis after exercise suggested a limited 'anabolic window' for optimal nutrient uptake.

content: This raises the possibility that results were attributed to greater mitochondrial and perhaps other sarcoplasmic protein fractions as opposed to the synthesis of contractile elements. In contrast, Rasmussen and colleagues (602) investigated the acute impact of protein timing after resistance training and found no significant differences in the protein synthetic response between consuming nutrients 1 hour and consuming nutrients 3 hours postexercise.
summary: Some early studies on protein timing may have focused on non-contractile protein synthesis, leading to conflicting results.

content: The aforementioned studies, although providing interesting mechanistic insight into postexercise nutritional responses, are limited to generating hypotheses regarding hypertrophic adaptations as opposed to drawing practical conclusions about the efficacy of nutrient timing for building muscle. Acute measures of muscle protein synthesis taken in the postworkout period are often decoupled from the chronic upregulation of causative myogenic signals (148) and do not necessarily predict long-term hypertrophic adaptations from regimented resistance training (765). In addition, postworkout elevations in muscle protein synthesis in untrained subjects are not replicated in those who are resistance trained (12).
summary: Acute studies on protein synthesis don't always translate to long-term muscle growth, and trained individuals may respond differently.

content: The only way to determine whether a nutrient’s timing produces a true hypertrophic effect is by performing training studies that measure changes in muscle size over time. A number of longitudinal studies have directly investigated the effects of postexercise protein ingestion on muscle growth. The results of these trials are contradictory, seemingly because of disparities in study design and methodology.
summary: Longitudinal studies measuring muscle growth are needed to determine the true impact of nutrient timing on hypertrophy.

content: My lab conducted a meta-analysis of the protein timing literature (659). Inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis were randomized controlled trials involving one group receiving protein within 1 hour pre- or postworkout and the other group not receiving protein for at least 2 hours after the exercise bout. Studies included in the meta-analysis had to span at least 6 weeks and provide a minimum dose of 6 g of EAAs. A total of 23 studies comprising 525 subjects were analyzed. Simple pooled analysis of data showed a small but significant effect (0.20) on muscle hypertrophy favoring timed protein consumption. Regression analysis found that virtually the entire effect was explained by greater protein consumption in the timing group versus the nontiming group (~1.7 g/kg vs. 1.3 g/kg, respectively). The average protein consumption in the nontimed groups was well below what is deemed necessary for maximizing the protein synthesis associated with resistance training. Only a few studies actually endeavored to match protein intake between conditions. A subanalysis of these studies revealed no effects associated with protein timing.
summary: A meta-analysis of protein timing studies found a small, likely protein intake-related, benefit for muscle hypertrophy.

content: The findings provide strong evidence that any effect of protein timing on muscle hypertrophy is relatively small, if there is one at all. The inclusion of carbohydrate in postworkout nutrition intake is often claimed to be synergistic to protein consumption with respect to promoting a hypertrophic response (334). This assertion is primarily based on theorized anabolic actions of carbohydrate-mediated insulin release. Although insulin has known anabolic properties (78, 221), emerging research shows that the hormone has a permissive rather than stimulatory role in regulating protein synthesis (577). Insulin secretion has little impact on postexercise anabolism at physiological levels (265).
summary: The purported synergistic effect of carbohydrates on muscle growth with protein is likely minimal, as insulin's role in protein synthesis is more permissive than stimulatory.

content: Evidence suggests a threshold below which plasma insulin levels cause a refractory response of muscle protein synthesis to the stimulatory effect of resistance training (369). Studies have failed to show any additive effects of carbohydrate on enhancing postexercise muscle protein synthesis when combined with amino acid provision (251, 381, 721). The principal effects of insulin on lean body mass are related to its role in reducing muscle catabolism (174, 243, 305, 362). Anticatabolic effects are believed to involve insulin-mediated phosphorylation of PI3K/Akt, which in turn blunts activation of the Forkhead family of transcription factors (364). An inhibition of other components of the ubiquitin–proteasome pathway are also theorized to play a role in the process (265).
summary: Insulin's primary effect on muscle growth is likely through reducing muscle breakdown, rather than directly stimulating protein synthesis.

content: Muscle protein breakdown is only slightly elevated immediately postexercise and then rapidly rises thereafter (398). When in the fasted state, proteolysis is markedly increased at 195 min postexercise, and protein balance remains negative (584). The extent of protein breakdown increases by up to 50% at the 3-hour mark, and heightened proteolysis can persist for up to 24 hours after an intense resistance training bout (398). Given that muscle hypertrophy represents the difference between myofibrillar protein synthesis and proteolysis, a decrease in protein breakdown would conceivably enhance the accretion of contractile proteins and thus facilitate hypertrophy.
summary: Muscle protein breakdown significantly increases after exercise, particularly in a fasted state. Reducing this breakdown could enhance muscle growth.

content: Muscle protein breakdown is only slightly elevated immediately postexercise and then rapidly rises thereafter. The extent of protein breakdown increases by up to 50% at the 3-hour mark, and heightened proteolysis can persist for up to 24 hours after an intense resistance training bout. Given that muscle hypertrophy represents the difference between myofibrillar protein synthesis and proteolysis, a decrease in protein breakdown would conceivably enhance the accretion of contractile proteins and thus facilitate hypertrophy.
summary: Muscle protein breakdown increases after exercise, potentially hindering hypertrophy. Reducing protein breakdown could enhance muscle growth.

content: When in the fasted state, proteolysis is markedly increased at 195 min postexercise, and protein balance remains negative.  For those who resistance train partially or fully fasted, on the other hand, consuming protein immediately postworkout becomes increasingly more important to promote anabolism.
summary: Fasted training increases protein breakdown, emphasizing the importance of post-workout protein intake for fasted individuals.

content: Insulin's impact on net muscle protein balance plateaus at 3 to 4 times fasting levels, which is a range of ~15 to 30 mU/L. A solid meal of 75 g carbohydrate, 37 g protein, and 17 g fat raises insulin concentrations 3-fold over fasting conditions within a half hour after consumption and increases to 5-fold after 1 hour; at the 5-hour mark, levels remain double those seen during fasting. Typical mixed meals achieve this effect 1 to 2 hours after consumption, and levels remain elevated for 3 to 6 hours or more depending on the size of the meal.
summary: Insulin levels increase after meals, peaking 1-2 hours post-consumption and remaining elevated for several hours. This effect is maximized by meals containing carbohydrates, protein, and fat.

content: The effect of exercise on insulin-stimulated glucose uptake shows a strong positive correlation to the magnitude of glycogen use during the bout. Mechanisms responsible for this phenomenon include heightened translocation of the glucose transporter type 4 (GLUT4) protein responsible for facilitating entry of glucose into muscle and an increase in the activity of glycogen synthase—the principal enzyme involved in promoting glycogen storage.
summary: Exercise increases glucose uptake, correlating with glycogen depletion. This is mediated by GLUT4 translocation and increased glycogen synthase activity.

content: Glycogen is considered critical to the performance of hypertrophy-type protocols. MacDougall and colleagues found that 3 sets of elbow flexion exercises at 80% of 1RM performed to muscular failure decreased mixed-muscle glycogen concentration by 24%. Similar findings were reported for the vastus lateralis: 3 sets of 12RM depleted glycogen stores by 26.1%, and 6 sets led to a 38% reduction. Extrapolation of these results to a typical high-volume bodybuilding workout involving multiple exercises and sets for the same muscle group indicates that the majority of local glycogen stores are depleted during such training.
summary: Glycogen is essential for hypertrophy training. Resistance exercise depletes muscle glycogen, with higher volume leading to greater depletion.

content: Even if glycogen is completely depleted during exercise, full replenishment of these stores is accomplished within 24 hours regardless of whether carbohydrate intake is delayed postworkout. The need to quickly replenish glycogen is only relevant for those who perform 2-a-day split resistance training bouts (i.e., morning and evening) in which the same muscles are worked during the respective sessions.
summary: Glycogen stores fully replenish within 24 hours, regardless of post-workout carbohydrate intake. Rapid replenishment is only crucial for individuals performing two daily workouts targeting the same muscle groups.

content: A 45 g dose of whey isolate produces insulin levels sufficient to maximize net muscle protein balance (15 to 30 mU/L). Consuming carbohydrate immediately after exercise significantly increases the rate of muscle glycogen repletion; delaying intake by just 2 hours decreases the rate of resynthesis by as much as 50%.
summary: Whey protein and post-workout carbohydrates maximize muscle protein synthesis and glycogen replenishment, respectively. Delaying carbohydrate intake reduces glycogen resynthesis.

content: The body is primed for anabolism following intense exercise. Muscles become sensitized to nutrient intake so that muscle protein synthesis is blunted until amino acids are consumed. The body of research suggests that the anabolic window of opportunity is considerably larger than the 1-hour postworkout period often cited in the literature.
summary: Post-exercise, the body is primed for muscle growth.  The anabolic window extends beyond the commonly cited 1-hour period.

content: It is important to consume high-quality protein (at a dose of ~0.4 to 0.5 g/kg of lean body mass) both pre- and postexercise within about 4 to 6 hours of each other depending on meal size. Those who resistance train partially or fully fasted should consume protein (at a dose of ~0.4 to 0.5 g/kg of lean body mass) as quickly as possible postworkout, preferably within 45 minutes of the bout. Those who perform 2-a-day (morning and evening workouts in the same day) should consume carbohydrate (at a dose of ~1.0 to 1.5 g/kg of lean body mass) within 1 hour postworkout.
summary: Optimal protein intake is crucial both pre- and post-workout, with fasted individuals needing immediate post-workout protein and those with double daily workouts requiring post-workout carbohydrates.

content: Although research is somewhat equivocal, it seems prudent to consume high-quality protein (at a dose of ~0.4 to 0.5 g/kg of lean body mass) both pre- and postexercise within about 4 to 6 hours of each other depending on meal size. For those who train partially or fully fasted, on the other hand, consuming protein immediately postworkout becomes increasingly more important to promote anabolism. A positive energy balance is necessary for maximizing the hypertrophic response to resistance training, but overconsumption ultimately is detrimental to gains. Those seeking to maximize hypertrophy should consume at least 1.7 g/kg/day of protein.
summary: Consuming protein pre- and post-workout is recommended, with fasted individuals prioritizing immediate post-workout protein. While a positive energy balance is necessary for hypertrophy, excessive calorie intake is detrimental. Protein intake should be at least 1.7 g/kg/day for optimal muscle growth.

content: Dietary fat should comprise the balance of nutrient intake after setting protein and carbohydrate amounts. People should focus on obtaining a majority of fat from unsaturated sources. At least three meals consisting of a minimum of 25 g of high-quality protein should be consumed every 5 to 6 hours to maximize anabolism.
summary: Dietary fat should make up the remaining calories after protein and carbohydrate intake. Unsaturated fats are preferred. Consuming three meals with at least 25g of protein every 5-6 hours maximizes anabolism.

content: Nutrient timing around the exercise bout should be considered in the context of the periworkout period.  An inhibition of other components of the ubiquitin–proteasome pathway are also theorized to play a role in the process.
summary: Nutrient timing is important around exercise, considering the periworkout period. Other factors, such as the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway, may also influence muscle protein breakdown.

content: Nutrient timing around the exercise bout should be considered in the context of the periworkout period.
summary: Nutrient timing is important for exercise performance and recovery.

summary: This study examines the concept of a post-exercise anabolic window and the optimal timing of nutrient intake.

content: Arnal, MA, Mosoni, L, Boirie, Y, Houlier, ML, Morin, L, Verdier, E, Ritz, P, Antoine, JM, Prugnaud, J, Beaufrere, B, and Mirand, PP published a study in Am J Clin Nutr in 1999 titled 'Protein pulse feeding improves protein retention in elderly women'. content: Aronson, D, Violan, MA, Dufresne, SD, Zangen, D, Fielding, RA, and Goodyear, LJ published a study in J Clin Invest in 1997 titled 'Exercise stimulates the mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway in human skeletal muscle'. Aronson, D, Wojtaszewski, JF, Thorell, A, Nygren, J, Zangen, D, Richter, EA, Ljungqvist, O, and Fielding, RA published a study in J Clin Invest in 1997 titled 'Exercise stimulates the mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway in human skeletal muscle'. RA and Goodyear (1998) showed that extracellular-regulated protein kinase cascades are activated in response to injury in human skeletal muscle.
summary: These studies demonstrate the activation of the mitogen-activated protein kinase pathway in response to both exercise and injury in human skeletal muscle.

content: Atherton et al. (2005) found that selective activation of AMPK-PGC-1alpha or PKB-TSC2-mTOR signaling can explain specific adaptive responses to endurance or resistance training-like electrical muscle stimulation. Atherton et al. (2010) showed that muscle full effect after oral protein is time-dependent and concordant and discordant between human muscle protein synthesis and mTORC1 signaling. Atherton and Smith (2012) discussed muscle protein synthesis in response to nutrition and exercise. Atherton (2013) questioned if there is an optimal time for warfighters to supplement with protein.
summary: These studies by Atherton and colleagues explore the signaling pathways and timing of protein intake that influence muscle protein synthesis in response to exercise and nutrition.

content: Baechle and Earle (2008) wrote the book Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning.
summary: This book provides a comprehensive overview of strength training and conditioning principles.

content: Baker et al. (1994) studied the effect of manipulating volume and intensity on strength.
summary: This study investigates the relationship between training volume, intensity, and strength gains.

content: Bamman et al. (2001) found that mechanical load increases muscle IGF-I and androgen receptor mRNA concentrations in humans. Bamman et al. (2003) reported gender differences in resistance-training-induced myofiber hypertrophy among older adults. Bamman (2007) suggested taking two NSAIDs and calling on satellite cells in the morning. Bamman et al. (2007) used cluster analysis to test the importance of myogenic gene expression during myofiber hypertrophy in humans.
summary: These studies by Bamman and colleagues examine the role of mechanical load, gender, and gene expression in muscle hypertrophy.

content: Barash et al. (2004) showed rapid muscle-specific gene expression changes after a single bout of eccentric contractions in the mouse.
summary: This study investigates the immediate gene expression changes in muscle following eccentric contractions.

content: Barnett et al. (1995) studied the effects of variations of the bench press exercise on the EMG activity of five shoulder muscles.
summary: This study analyzes the electromyographic activity of shoulder muscles during different variations of the bench press exercise.

content: Barton (2006) found that viral expression of insulin-like growth factor-I isoforms promotes different responses in skeletal muscle. Barton et al. (2002) reported that muscle-specific expression of insulin-like growth factor I counters muscle decline in mdx mice. Barton-Davis et al. (1999) investigated the contribution of satellite cells to IGF-I induced hypertrophy of skeletal muscle.
summary: These studies explore the role of insulin-like growth factor-I and satellite cells in muscle growth and regeneration.

content: Bassel-Duby and Olson (2006) discussed signaling pathways in skeletal muscle remodeling.
summary: This review discusses the signaling pathways involved in skeletal muscle adaptation and repair.

content: Baum et al. (1985) discussed the utilization of nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs.
summary: This article reviews the use of nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs in various medical contexts.

content: Beh m (1995) discussed neuromuscular implications and applications of resistance training.
summary: This article explores the neuromuscular adaptations and applications of resistance training.

content: Belcastro et al. (1998) proposed an exercise-induced muscle injury: A calpain hypothesis.
summary: This study proposes a calpain-mediated mechanism for exercise-induced muscle injury.

content: Bell et al. (2000) studied the effect of concurrent strength and endurance training on skeletal muscle properties and hormone concentrations in humans.
summary: This study investigates the combined effects of strength and endurance training on muscle characteristics and hormone levels.

content: Bellamy et al. (2014) investigated the acute satellite cell response and skeletal muscle hypertrophy following resistance training.
summary: This study examines the immediate satellite cell activation and muscle growth response to resistance exercise.

content: Ben-Sira et al. (1995) studied the effect of different types of strength training on concentric strength in women.
summary: This study compares the effects of various strength training protocols on concentric strength in women.

content: Benton et al. (2011) reported short-term effects of resistance training frequency on body composition and strength in middle-aged women.
summary: This study investigates the impact of different resistance training frequencies on body composition and strength in middle-aged women.

content: Bentwich et al. (2005) identified hundreds of conserved and nonconserved human microRNAs.
summary: This study identifies a large number of microRNAs in humans, both conserved and novel.

content: Benziane et al. (2008) reported divergent cell signaling after short-term intensified endurance training in human skeletal muscle.
summary: This study investigates the distinct cellular signaling pathways activated by short-term intensified endurance training.

content: Long-chain n-3 fatty acids enhance neonatal insulin-regulated protein metabolism in piglets by differentially altering muscle lipid composition.
summary: This study examines the impact of long-chain n-3 fatty acids on protein metabolism and muscle lipid composition in piglets.

content: The effects of supra-physiologic doses of testosterone on muscle size and strength in normal men were shown by Bhasin et al. (1996).
summary: This study investigates the effects of high doses of testosterone on muscle growth and strength in healthy men.

content: Bhasin et al. (1996) showed the effects of supra-physiologic doses of testosterone on muscle size and strength in normal men. Bhasin et al. (2001) provided proof of the effect of testosterone on skeletal muscle. Bhasin et al. (2005) found that older men are as responsive as young men to the anabolic effects of graded doses of testosterone on skeletal muscle.
summary: These studies by Bhasin et al. investigated the effects of testosterone on muscle growth and strength in both young and older men.

content: Bodine et al. (2001) found that the Akt/mTOR pathway is a crucial regulator of skeletal muscle hypertrophy and can prevent muscle atrophy in vivo. Bodnar et al. (2014) showed that hypermuscular mice with a mutation in the myostatin gene display altered calcium signaling.
summary: These studies explored the molecular mechanisms underlying muscle growth and atrophy, highlighting the roles of the Akt/mTOR pathway and myostatin.

content: Blazevich et al. (2003) reported on training-specific muscle architecture adaptation after 5 weeks of training in athletes. Blazevich et al. (2007) examined the influence of concentric and eccentric resistance training on architectural adaptation in human quadriceps muscles.
summary: These studies by Blazevich et al. examined how different types of resistance training affect muscle structure and adaptation.

content: Bloomquist et al. (2013) studied the effect of range of motion in heavy load squatting on muscle and tendon adaptations. Bondesen et al. (2004) found that the COX-2 pathway is essential during early stages of skeletal muscle regeneration. Bondesen et al. (2006) reported that the COX-2 pathway regulates growth of atrophied muscle via multiple mechanisms.
summary: These studies investigated the impact of training variables and specific pathways on muscle and tendon adaptations, including regeneration.

content: Borkman et al. (1993) studied the relation between insulin sensitivity and the fatty-acid composition of skeletal-muscle phospholipids. Borsheim et al. (2002) investigated essential amino acids and muscle protein recovery from resistance exercise.

content: Bottaro et al. (2011) examined the effect of resistance training for strength and muscle thickness: Effect of number of sets and muscle group trained. Botton et al. (2013) conducted an electromyographical analysis of the deltoid between different strength training exercises.

content:
Muscle damage and muscle remodeling were examined by Flann, LaStayo, McClain, Hazel, and Lindstedt in J Exp Biol. 214: 674-679, 2011. 
Changes in exercises are more effective than in loading schemes to improve muscle strength, as reported by Fonseca, Roschel, Tricoli, de Souza, Wilson, Laurentino, Aihara, de Souza Leao, and Ugrinowitsch in J Strength Cond Res. 28(11): 3085-3092, 2014. 
Myofibrillar disruption following acute concentric and eccentric resistance exercise in strength-trained men was studied. 
Changes in human skeletal muscle ultrastructure and force production after acute resistance exercise were observed.
summary: This chunk explores the effects of resistance exercise on muscle damage, remodeling, and ultrastructure.

content:
The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM. 
The deadlift is a posterior chain exercise targeting the erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. Conventional deadlifts place greater demand on the erector spinae compared to sumo deadlifts.
summary: This chunk describes two compound exercises: the squat and the deadlift, outlining their target muscle groups and variations.

content:
Acute molecular responses in untrained and trained muscle subjected to aerobic and resistance exercise training versus resistance training alone were studied by Fernandez-Gonzalo, Lundberg, and Tesch in Acta Physiol. (Oxf.) 209: 283-294, 2013. 
Similar acute molecular responses to equivalent volumes of isometric, lengthening, or shortening mode resistance exercise were observed.
summary: This chunk focuses on the acute molecular responses of muscle to different exercise types.

content:

content:

content:

content:

content: Harridge (2007) studied the plasticity of human skeletal muscle, from gene expression to in vivo function.  Hill and Goldspink (2003) investigated the expression and splicing of the insulin-like growth factor gene in rodent muscle, linking it to muscle satellite (stem) cell activation following local tissue damage. Hill, Wernig, and Goldspink (2003) explored muscle satellite (stem) cell activation during local tissue injury and repair.
summary: Research on the plasticity of skeletal muscle, gene expression, and the role of satellite cells in muscle repair.

content: Harries, Lubans, and Callister (2015) compared resistance training progression models on maximal strength in sub-elite adolescent rugby union players. Hartman, Clark, Bembens, Kilgore, and Bemben (2007) compared twice-daily and once-daily training sessions in male weightlifters. Helms, Fitschen, Aragon, Cronin, and Schoenfeld (2014) provided recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation, covering resistance and cardiovascular training. Helms, Aragon, and Fitschen (2014) offered evidence-based recommendations for natural bodybuilding contest preparation, focusing on nutrition and supplementation. Helms, Zinn, Rowlands, and Brown (2014) conducted a systematic review of dietary protein during caloric restriction in resistance-trained lean athletes, advocating for higher intakes.
summary: Studies on resistance training progression models, training frequency, and dietary recommendations for bodybuilding.

content: Haussinger, Lang, and Gerok (1994) studied the regulation of cell function by the cellular hydration state. Haussinger (1996) discussed the role of cellular hydration in the regulation of cell function. Hawley, Burke, Phillips, and Spriet (2011) examined the nutritional modulation of training-induced skeletal muscle adaptations. Hawley (2009) explored molecular responses to strength and endurance training, questioning their compatibility.
summary: Research on the role of cellular hydration in cell function and the impact of nutrition on muscle adaptations to training.

content: Hebert-Losier, Schneiders, Garcia, Sullivan, and Simoneau (2012) investigated the influence of knee flexion angle and age on triceps surae muscle activity during heel raises. Hedayatpour and Falla (2012) examined non-uniform muscle adaptations to eccentric exercise and their implications for training and sport.  Hickson (1980) discussed the interference of strength development by simultaneously training for strength and endurance.
summary: Studies on muscle activation patterns, muscle adaptations to eccentric exercise, and the interference effect of concurrent strength and endurance training.

content: Higbie, Cureton, Warren, and Prior (1996) examined the effects of concentric and eccentric training on muscle strength, cross-sectional area, and neural activation.  Heron and Richmond (1993) investigated in-series fiber architecture in long human muscles.  Heslin, Newman, Wolf, Pisters, and Brennan (1992) studied the effect of hyperinsulinemia on whole-body and skeletal muscle leucine carbon kinetics in humans.
summary: Research on the effects of concentric and eccentric training, muscle fiber architecture, and hyperinsulinemia on muscle metabolism.

content: Henneman, Somjen, and Carpenter (1965) explored the functional significance of cell size in spinal motoneurons. Hornberger, McLoughlin, Leszczynski, Armstrong, Jameson, Bowen, Hwang, Hou, Moustafa, Carlson, Hatfield, Diamond, and Esser (2003) found that selenoprotein-deficient transgenic mice exhibited enhanced exercise-induced muscle growth. summary: Studies on the functional significance of cell size, glycogen availability, selenoprotein deficiency, and mechanotransduction in muscle growth.

content: Hoeger, Barette, Hale, and Hopkins (1987) studied the relationship between repetitions and selected percentages of one repetition maximum. summary: Research on the relationship between repetitions and one-repetition maximum, muscle adaptations to different loading intensities, and exercise-induced ultrastructural changes.

content: Hornberger, Chu, Mak, Hsiung, Huang, and Chien (2006) investigated the role of phospholipase D and phosphatidic acid in the mechanical activation of mTOR signaling in skeletal muscle. The role of phospholipase D and phosphatidic acid in the mechanical activation of mTOR signaling in skeletal muscle was studied by Hornberger et al. (2006). Prostaglandin F2(alpha) stimulates growth of skeletal muscle cells via an NFAT C2-dependent pathway according to Horsley and Pavlath (2003).
summary: Studies on the role of phospholipase D, phosphatidic acid, and prostaglandin F2(alpha) in muscle growth and signaling.

content: Hortobagyi et al. (1996) studied adaptive responses to muscle lengthening and shortening in humans. Hortobagyi et al. (2000) studied changes in muscle strength, muscle fiber size, and myofibrillar gene expression after immobilization and retraining in humans.
summary: Research on muscle adaptations to lengthening and shortening, and the effects of immobilization and retraining on muscle strength and fiber size.

content: The role of phospholipase D and phosphatidic acid in the mechanical activation of mTOR signaling in skeletal muscle was studied by Hornberger et al. (2006). summary: This chunk explores the molecular mechanisms of muscle growth, including the roles of phospholipase D, phosphatidic acid, and prostaglandin F2(alpha).

content: Adaptive responses to muscle lengthening and shortening in humans were studied by Hortobagyi et al. (1996). Changes in muscle strength, muscle fiber size, and myofibrillar gene expression after immobilization and retraining in humans were studied by Hortobagyi et al. (2000).

content: Kraemer, WJ conducted a series of studies on the physiological basis for strength training in American football. Kraemer, WJ, Marchitelli, L, Gordon, SE, Harman, E, Dziados, JE, Mello, R, Frykman, P, McCurry, D, and Fleck, SJ researched hormonal and growth factor responses to heavy resistance exercise protocols. Kraemer, WJ, Gordon, SE, Fleck, SJ, Marchitelli, LJ, Mello, R, Dziados, JE, Friedl, K, Harman, E, Maresh, C, and Fry, AC studied endogenous anabolic hormonal and growth factor responses to heavy resistance exercise in males and females. content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat, a compound exercise targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, is effective for strength development with a typical prescription of 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM. Deep squats enhance glute activation compared to parallel squats.

content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat, a compound exercise targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, is more effective for glute activation when performed below parallel.

content: Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) isoforms exist. IGF-1 levels decrease with age. IGF-1 is involved in exercise-induced muscle damage (EIMD). Insulin is involved in intracellular signaling.
summary: IGF-1, involved in muscle damage and decreasing with age, is a key factor in exercise physiology alongside insulin's role in intracellular signaling.

content: Intensity of effort is defined as the level of exertion during exercise. Intensity of load is defined as the amount of weight lifted. Periodizing intensity of load involves systematically varying the weight lifted over time.
summary: Intensity, both effort and load, is crucial in training, and periodization involves strategically changing the weight lifted over time.

content: Interleukins (ILs) are signaling molecules involved in inflammation. Intracellular signaling pathways are involved in muscle protein synthesis. The JNK module is a signaling pathway involved in muscle protein synthesis.
summary: Inflammation, muscle protein synthesis, and specific signaling pathways like JNK are interconnected processes in exercise physiology.

content: Isometric actions involve muscle contraction without a change in muscle length. Isotonic rep duration refers to the time taken to complete a repetition with a constant muscle tension. Degrees of freedom of joints refer to the range of motion possible at a joint.
summary: Muscle actions, rep duration, and joint range of motion are fundamental concepts in exercise mechanics.

content: Lactate production is increased during exercise. Lactic acid is a byproduct of anaerobic metabolism.
summary: Lactate production, a byproduct of anaerobic metabolism, increases during exercise.

content: Lagging muscle groups are those that are not trained as frequently as other muscle groups. Large-muscle exercise should be performed before lagging muscle groups in exercise order.
summary: Training lagging muscle groups less frequently than others requires strategic exercise order, prioritizing large muscle groups first.

content: The lateral raise is an exercise that targets the deltoid muscle.
summary: The lateral raise is a specific exercise targeting the deltoid muscle.

content: Lactate production is increased during exercise. Lactic acid is a byproduct of anaerobic metabolism.
summary: Lactate production increases during exercise, as lactic acid is a byproduct of anaerobic metabolism.

content: Lagging muscle groups are those that are not trained as frequently as other muscle groups. Large-muscle exercise should be performed before lagging muscle groups in exercise order.
summary: Lagging muscle groups, those trained less frequently, should be exercised after larger muscle groups.

content: The lateral raise is an exercise that targets the deltoid muscle. The lat pulldown is an exercise that targets the latissimus dorsi muscle.
summary: The lateral raise targets the deltoid muscle, while the lat pulldown targets the latissimus dorsi muscle.

content: Lean mass is greater in powerlifters compared to bodybuilders. Sedentarism can lead to a decrease in lean mass. Leg (lower) muscle training is important for overall fitness. The leg press is an exercise that targets the quadriceps, hamstrings, and glutes.
summary: Powerlifters tend to have greater lean mass than bodybuilders. Sedentary behavior can decrease lean mass, while leg training, such as the leg press, is crucial for overall fitness.

content: Exercise frequency is important for maximizing muscle protein synthesis. Increasing lean mass involves increasing exercise frequency. Limits to exercise frequency exist.
summary: Exercise frequency is crucial for maximizing muscle protein synthesis and increasing lean mass, but there are limits to how often one can train.

content: Protein consumption is important for maximizing muscle protein synthesis. Leucine is an essential amino acid that plays a role in muscle protein synthesis. The leucine threshold is the amount of leucine required to stimulate muscle protein synthesis.
summary: Protein intake, particularly leucine, is essential for maximizing muscle protein synthesis, with a specific threshold of leucine required for stimulation.

content: Periodization involves systematically varying training variables over time. Linear periodization involves gradually increasing the intensity of training over time. Nonlinear periodization involves varying the intensity of training in a non-linear fashion. Reverse linear periodization involves starting with high intensity and gradually decreasing it over time.
summary: Periodization involves systematically varying training variables. Different periodization models include linear, nonlinear, and reverse linear approaches.

content: The length–tension relationship describes the relationship between muscle length and force production.
summary: The length–tension relationship explains how muscle length influences its ability to generate force.

content: Load (training) refers to the amount of weight lifted during exercise. Moderate-load training involves lifting weights that are moderate in intensity. Light-load training involves lifting weights that are lighter than those used in traditional resistance training.
summary: Load refers to the weight lifted during exercise. Moderate-load and light-load training differ in intensity compared to traditional resistance training.

content: Mode (training) refers to the type of exercise performed. Movement plane refers to the direction of movement. Multijoint exercise involves multiple joints.
summary: Mode refers to the type of exercise, movement plane describes the direction of movement, and multijoint exercises engage multiple joints.

content: Muscle action types include concentric, eccentric, and isometric contractions.
summary: Muscle contractions can be concentric (shortening), eccentric (lengthening), or isometric (static).

content: Muscle damage occurs during exercise and is a part of the muscle-building process. Muscle failure occurs when a muscle can no longer perform a repetition.
summary: Muscle damage, a natural part of exercise, contributes to muscle growth. Muscle failure occurs when a muscle can no longer complete a repetition.

content: Muscle protein synthesis is the process of building new muscle protein. Muscle protein balance refers to the difference between muscle protein synthesis and muscle protein breakdown.
summary: Muscle protein synthesis builds new muscle protein, while muscle protein balance considers the difference between synthesis and breakdown.

content: Motor learning is the process of acquiring new motor skills. Motor neurons are nerve cells that control muscle contractions. Motor units are the basic functional units of muscle contraction.
summary: Motor learning involves acquiring new motor skills. Motor neurons control muscle contractions, and motor units are the fundamental units of muscle activation.

content: Long-chain fatty acids are a type of fat that can be used as an energy source during exercise. Monosaccharides are simple sugars that can be used as an energy source during exercise.
summary: Long-chain fatty acids and monosaccharides are both energy sources utilized during exercise.

content: Momentum (of repetition) refers to the use of momentum to complete a repetition.
summary: Momentum refers to using body movement to assist in completing a repetition.

content: Leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) is a signaling molecule involved in muscle growth.
summary: LIF is a signaling molecule that plays a role in muscle growth.

content: mRNA is a molecule that carries genetic information from DNA to the ribosomes, where proteins are synthesized.
summary: mRNA carries genetic information from DNA to ribosomes, where protein synthesis occurs.

summary: This chunk defines key muscle physiology concepts: action types, damage, failure, protein synthesis, and balance.

content: Brad Schoenfeld, PhD, CSCS, CSPS, FNSCA, is widely regarded as one of the leading strength and fitness experts in the United States. As a personal trainer, Schoenfeld has worked with numerous elite-level physique athletes, including many top pros. Schoenfeld was elected to the National Strength and Conditioning Association’s Board of Directors in 2012. He is the author of multiple consumer-oriented fitness books, including The M.A.X. Muscle Plan and Strong and Sculpted (formerly Sculpting Her Body Perfect). He is a regular columnist for Muscular Development magazine. Schoenfeld has been published or featured in virtually every major fitness magazine (including Muscle and Fitness, MuscleMag, Ironman, Oxygen, and Shape).
summary: This chunk provides biographical information about Brad Schoenfeld, a prominent strength and fitness expert.

content: Schoenfeld earned his PhD in health promotion and wellness at Rocky Mountain University. Schoenfeld’s research focused on elucidating the mechanisms of muscle hypertrophy and their application to resistance training. He has published more than 80 peer-reviewed scientific papers.
summary: Schoenfeld's academic background includes a PhD and extensive research on muscle hypertrophy and its application to resistance training.

content: Schoenfeld is the author of multiple consumer-oriented fitness books, including The M.A.X. Muscle Plan and Strong and Sculpted (formerly Sculpting Her Body Perfect). He is a regular columnist for Muscular Development magazine. Schoenfeld has been published or featured in virtually every major fitness magazine (including Muscle and Fitness, MuscleMag, Ironman, Oxygen, and Shape).
summary: Schoenfeld is a prolific author and contributor to various fitness publications, sharing his expertise with a wide audience.

content: As a personal trainer, Schoenfeld has worked with numerous elite-level physique athletes, including many top pros. Schoenfeld serves as a fitness expert and contributor to www.bodybuilding.com, www.diet.com, and www.t-nation.com.
summary: Schoenfeld's experience includes working with elite athletes and contributing to popular fitness websites.

content: Schoenfeld was elected to the National Strength and Conditioning Association’s Board of Directors in 2012. He serves on the editorial advisory boards for several journals, including the Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research and Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition.
summary: Schoenfeld holds prominent positions within the fitness and research communities, demonstrating his influence and expertise.

content: He has appeared on hundreds of television shows and radio programs across the United States. Schoenfeld heads their human performance laboratory.
summary: Schoenfeld's reach extends beyond print and online, as he actively participates in media appearances and leads a human performance laboratory.

content: He is an assistant professor of exercise science at Lehman College in the Bronx, New York.
summary: Schoenfeld's professional role includes teaching and research at Lehman College.
